### [1] [Resolving Endpoint Underfitting in Diffusion Bridges via Noise Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28962)
*Yurong Gao,Zicheng Zhang,Congying Han,Tiande Guo,Xinmin Qiu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion bridge models suffer from underfitting near the target endpoint due to noise-level mismatch. The authors propose Noise-Aligned Diffusion Bridge (NADB) with a mean network and noise-aligned mapping to resolve this, achieving better results in image restoration and translation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion bridge models mimic score-matching but cause underfitting near the target endpoint ($t \to 0$) due to large discrepancies in noise levels between network input and regression target.

Method: NADB reformulates the diffusion bridge by (1) employing a mean network to provide a cleaner conditional target, and (2) introducing a noise-aligned mapping relationship to resolve the noise mismatch.

Result: Experimental validation across multiple image restoration and image translation tasks demonstrates the effectiveness of NADB over existing methods.

Conclusion: The noise-aligned approach corrects underfitting near the target endpoint, improving diffusion bridge model performance.

Abstract: Diffusion bridge models offer a powerful framework for connecting two data distributions, such as in image restoration and translation. Many existing methods learn this bridge by mimicking the score-matching formulation of standard diffusion models. In this work, we find that this way leads to an anomalous underfitting phenomenon near the target endpoint, as the process approaches the target distribution ($t \to 0$). This underfitting, characterized by significant drift in the predicted variance and direction, results from an excessively large discrepancy in noise levels between the network's input and its regression target.To resolve this issue, we propose the Noise-Aligned Diffusion Bridge (NADB).Our approach reformulates the diffusion bridge by first employing a mean network to provide a cleaner conditional target, and then introducing a novel, noise-aligned mapping relationship. This new formulation resolves the noise mismatch and corrects the underfitting near the target endpoint. Experimental validation across multiple image restoration and image translation tasks demonstrates the effectiveness of our approach. Code is available at https://github.com/gyr02/NADB.

</details>


### [2] [GAP3D: Generative Alignment of VLM Latents to Patch-Level Embeddings for 3D Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28995)
*Polytimi Anna Gkotsi,Andrii Zadaianchuk,Mohammad Mahdi Derakhshani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GAP3D is a modular diffusion-based approach that aligns VLM-generated latents to a pre-trained image encoder's patch-level feature space, enabling frozen downstream generative models to use VLMs as prompt encoders for geometry-aware tasks like 3D asset generation without expensive end-to-end training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM-as-prompt-encoder approaches require costly end-to-end training or compress features into dense representations, losing spatial structure needed for geometry-aware tasks like 3D generation.

Method: A modular, diffusion-based framework that aligns VLM latents directly to the complete patch-level feature space of a pre-trained image encoder, allowing a frozen generative model to leverage VLMs while preserving spatial conditioning signals.

Result: Bypasses large-scale 3D data by training on general-domain image-text pairs; exhibits emergent zero-shot capability for multimodal prompts despite text-only training; partially bridges the representation gap between VLM and image-encoder spaces.

Conclusion: GAP3D takes first steps toward modular integration of foundation models via generative alignment to dense embedding spaces, though currently prioritizing high-level semantics over fine-grained detail.

Abstract: Recent approaches integrating vision-language models (VLMs) as prompt encoders for generative model conditioning typically rely on expensive end-to-end training or map features to compressed representations, discarding the dense spatial structure required for geometry-aware tasks like 3D asset generation. To address this, we propose GAP3D, a modular, diffusion-based approach that aligns VLM-generated latents directly to the complete, patch-level feature space of a pre-trained image encoder, enabling a frozen downstream generative model to utilize a VLM as prompt encoder while maintaining a spatially structured conditioning signal. Evaluated on 3D asset generation, our method bypasses the need for large-scale 3D data by training mainly on general-domain image-text pairs. It also exhibits emergent zero-shot capabilities for multimodal prompts, despite being trained exclusively on text input. Finally, while currently prioritizing high-level semantics over fine-grained detail, GAP3D demonstrates that the representation gap between VLM and image-encoder feature spaces can be partially bridged through diffusion-based alignment, taking the first steps towards a modular integration of foundation models through generative alignment to dense embedding spaces.

</details>


### [3] [Auditing Training-Free 3D Shape Retrieval with Diffused Geodesic Moments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29004)
*Zhicheng Du,Changyue Liu,Wenji Xi,Zhaotian Xie,Zhuo Deng,Ziheng Zhang,Yang Liu,Lan Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces Diffused Geodesic Moments (DGM), a training-free shape descriptor, and uses it to audit evaluation protocols. Findings show that aggregation and protocol choices often dominate descriptor performance, with GMSD-HKS achieving top scores and DGM being useful for specific deployment scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation metrics for training-free shape descriptors conflate multiple components (signal design, normalization, aggregation, codebook fitting, metric choices), making it difficult to assess individual components in isolation. The authors aim to disentangle these factors through a protocol audit.

Method: The paper proposes Diffused Geodesic Moments (DGM), a seed-conditioned descriptor that computes sparse implicit heat responses, converts them to distance-like fields, and summarizes each vertex by low-order moments across seeds and scales. DGM serves as both a practical baseline and an analytical tool for isolating protocol effects. Aggregation-matched experiments are conducted on FAUST-Reg and TOSCA benchmarks.

Result: GMSD-HKS achieves the highest scores (0.621/0.820 and 0.865/0.963 mAP/top-1). WKS remains a strong classical signal. DGM is useful mainly when sparse solves, non-spectral deployment, or symmetry-informative seed frames are priorities. The input field and aggregation protocol can dominate the moment formula.

Conclusion: The paper contributes a reproducible protocol-cascade analysis, a cross-shape alignment diagnostic for functional-map compatibility, and concrete recommendations for designing and reporting training-free shape descriptors. The key methodological finding is that protocol choices can overshadow the descriptor's own design.

Abstract: Reported retrieval scores for training-free shape descriptors conflate local signal design, normalization, aggregation, codebook fitting, and metric choices, making isolated component evaluation difficult. This paper reframes descriptor evaluation as a {\em protocol audit}. We introduce Diffused Geodesic Moments (DGM), a seed-conditioned descriptor that computes sparse implicit heat responses, converts them to distance-like fields, and summarizes each vertex by low-order moments across seeds and scales. DGM is used both as a practical non-spectral baseline and as an instrument for isolating protocol effects. On the registered FAUST benchmark split (FAUST-Reg) and the TOSCA shape collection, aggregation-matched experiments show that an independent Geometric Moment Shape Descriptor baseline built on Heat Kernel Signature features (GMSD-HKS) obtains the highest scores in this implementation ($0.621/0.820$ and $0.865/0.963$ mean average precision (mAP)/top-1), Wave Kernel Signature (WKS) remains a strong classical signal, and DGM is useful mainly when sparse solves, non-spectral deployment, or symmetry-informative seed frames are priorities. The broader finding is methodological: the input field and aggregation protocol can dominate the moment formula. The paper contributes a reproducible protocol-cascade analysis, a cross-shape alignment diagnostic for functional-map compatibility, and concrete recommendations for designing and reporting training-free shape descriptors.

</details>


### [4] [Trajectory Constraints for Imaging Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29012)
*Chaoyan Huang,Haijie Yuan,Saiprasad Ravishankar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TRACE is a training-free framework that stabilizes reconstruction trajectories in imaging inverse problems by explicitly coupling adjacent states, using neural approximations of proximal updates to improve quality without additional training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based and iterative methods for imaging inverse problems produce intermediate estimates during reconstruction but do not explicitly regularize transitions between consecutive states, leading to unstable reconstruction paths.

Method: TRACE introduces a training-free trajectory-constrained reconstruction framework that couples adjacent states along the trajectory, approximated by neural mappings for intractable proximal updates, creating a diffusion-like process with explicit temporal coupling.

Result: Experiments on linear and nonlinear image reconstruction tasks show TRACE improves reconstruction quality; trajectory-level analyses confirm temporal coupling directly affects state transitions along the reconstruction path.

Conclusion: Temporal coupling bounds trajectory variation and this control is preserved under untrained network updates, offering a principled way to stabilize reconstruction trajectories without additional training data.

Abstract: Diffusion-based and iterative methods have become effective tools for solving imaging inverse problems. Their reconstruction process naturally forms a trajectory of intermediate estimates. Although these intermediate estimates define a reconstruction trajectory, most methods do not explicitly regularize the transitions between consecutive states. To address this limitation, we introduce TRACE, a training-free TRAjectory-Constrained rEconstruction framework that stabilizes the reconstruction path by coupling adjacent states along the trajectory. This gives a trajectory-level model that can be interpreted as a sequence of proximal updates. Since the exact proximal update is generally intractable, we approximate it with a neural mapping. This yields a diffusion-like reconstruction process with an explicit coupling between neighboring states. We provide a stability analysis showing that temporal coupling bounds trajectory variation and that this control is preserved under untrained network updates. Experiments on linear and nonlinear image reconstruction tasks show that TRACE improves reconstruction quality. Trajectory-level analyses and ablations confirm that temporal coupling directly affects state transitions along the reconstruction path.

</details>


### [5] [Embodied3DBench: Benchmarking Low-Level Embodied Spatial Intelligence of Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29074)
*Jiyao Zhang,Mingxu Zhang,Yitong Peng,Haoxuan Liu,Chenshuo Wang,Yuxing Long,Haoyang Huang,Dongjiang Li,Nan Duan,Hui Shen,Hao Dong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Embodied3DBench is a benchmark with 21k+ QA pairs across 6 tasks evaluating VLMs' spatial intelligence in 3D environments. Current models excel at high-level spatial reasoning but struggle with interaction-oriented perception. A 1.3M QA synthetic dataset is provided to bridge this gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs lack systematic evaluation of low-level spatial intelligence for embodied 3D environments, particularly regarding robot-centric interaction perception.

Method: The benchmark includes 6 tasks across two groups: Spatial Structural Understanding (Grounding, Spatial Relation Prediction, Multi-view Correspondence) and Interaction-Oriented Perception (Affordance Prediction, Grasp Point Prediction, Trajectory Prediction), with 21k+ QA pairs and a synthetic 1.3M QA training dataset.

Result: Evaluating 13 state-of-the-art models revealed strong high-level spatial reasoning but fragile interaction-oriented perception. Fine-tuning on the synthetic 1.3M QA dataset significantly improved low-level spatial intelligence.

Conclusion: Embodied3DBench fills a critical gap by providing both a systematic evaluation framework and a scalable data solution for developing interaction-aware multimodal systems.

Abstract: Are current Vision Language Models (VLMs) ready to comprehend and reason about complex embodied interactions in 3D environments? We introduce Embodied3DBench, a robot-centric benchmark targeting low-level spatial intelligence in embodied 3D environments. To systematically evaluate these foundational perceptual capabilities, the benchmark includes 6 task categories divided into two core groups: Spatial Structural Understanding (Grounding, Spatial Relation Prediction, and Multi-view Correspondence) and Interaction-Oriented Perception (Affordance Prediction, Grasp Point Prediction, and Trajectory Prediction). The benchmark spans 12 subcategories and contains over 21k high-quality question-answer pairs. We evaluate 13 state-of-the-art models, and the results show that while current models exhibit relatively strong high-level spatial reasoning, such as understanding object-to-object positional relations, they remain fragile in interaction-oriented perception, highlighting a significant lack of robust 3D-aware interaction priors. To actively bridge this capability gap revealed by our benchmark, we further synthesize a large-scale training dataset comprising 1.3M QA pairs. Notably, fine-tuning on this dataset yields significant improvements in low-level spatial intelligence. Ultimately, Embodied3DBench fills a critical gap by providing both a systematic evaluation framework and a scalable data solution, setting a clear target for the development of interaction-aware multimodal systems.

</details>


### [6] [A Deep Learning Iterative Framework for Sentinel-1 Stripmap Enhancement Based on Azimuth Doppler Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29088)
*Juan Francisco Amieva,Christian Ayala,Roberto Del Prete,Mikel Galar*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a self-supervised SAR image enhancement framework for Sentinel-1 Stripmap imagery using azimuth subaperture decomposition, outperforming MERLIN in PSNR and SSIM while revealing a trade-off with speckle smoothing (ENL).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sentinel-1 Stripmap mode offers the highest resolution but suffers from speckle noise and spatial constraints, motivating the need for effective enhancement strategies that do not rely on external sensors, simulated ground truth, or multi-temporal stacks.

Method: A self-supervised enhancement framework that exploits physical consistency between subaperture reconstructions and full-aperture images to generate paired training data, integrating single- and multi-frame learning with an iterative inference scheme for progressive refinement.

Result: The proposed approach consistently outperforms MERLIN in PSNR and SSIM on real Sentinel-1 Stripmap data, though MERLIN achieves higher ENL, indicating a trade-off between structural fidelity and speckle smoothing.

Conclusion: Subaperture-based supervision offers a physically grounded, reproducible, and operationally viable approach for SAR image enhancement, extendable to other SAR platforms, polarizations, and acquisition modes.

Abstract: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) imagery enables all-weather, day-and-night Earth observation; however, it remains difficult to interpret due to speckle noise and other intrinsic imaging artifacts. Sentinel-1 (S1) constitutes one of the most widely used spaceborne SAR missions, offering systematic global coverage, high temporal resolution, dual-polarization imaging, and free data availability. Among S1 modes, Stripmap (SM) provides the highest resolution, yet speckle noise and spatial constraints often hinder applications requiring finer spatial detail. This motivates the need for effective image enhancement strategies. In this work, we propose a self-supervised enhancement framework for S1 SM imagery based on azimuth subaperture decomposition. The method exploits the physical consistency between subaperture reconstructions and the corresponding full-aperture image to generate paired training data without external sensors, simulated ground truth, or multi-temporal stacks. The proposed framework integrates single- and multi-frame learning and incorporates an iterative inference scheme that progressively refines image quality. Experiments on real S1 SM data show that the proposed approach consistently outperforms the widely adopted self-supervised deep learning baseline MERLIN, in terms of PSNR and SSIM, while MERLIN attains higher ENL, highlighting a trade-off between structural fidelity and speckle smoothing. Overall, the results demonstrate that subaperture-based supervision provides a physically grounded, reproducible, and operationally viable approach for SAR image enhancement using S1 data. It is worth noting that the proposed approach can be extended to other SAR platforms, polarizations, and acquisition modes.

</details>


### [7] [Low-Magnification SEM May Suffice: Interpretable Deep Learning for Multi-Scale Fracture-Cause Classification in Zirconia-Toughened Alumina](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29798)
*Julian Schmid,Pawel Astankow,Tom Vater,Julius Beck,Robert Cichon,Danny Krautz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An interpretable ViT achieves 90.7% accuracy in classifying fracture causes in ceramic hip/knee implants from SEM images, performing well even at low magnification and aligning with expert fractographic criteria.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fractographic workflows for identifying fracture origins in ceramic implants are time-consuming, partly subjective, and dependent on high-magnification SEM, creating a need for automated, reliable, and efficient quality assurance methods.

Method: A vision-transformer (ViT) was fine-tuned on 8,493 SEM images of BIOLOX delta ceramic implants, classified into three defect categories (green body, hard machining, material defects). A two-stage perceptual-hash/SSIM leakage audit ensured data integrity, and Grad-CAM was used for interpretability.

Result: The ViT achieved 0.907 accuracy and 0.888 macro-F1 in stratified five-fold cross-validation under severe class imbalance. Performance at low magnification (50x) was comparable to high magnification (1k-10kx), and Grad-CAM consistently highlighted canonical fractographic features (mirrors, hackles, pores, machining marks).

Conclusion: Interpretable ViTs serve as a complementary tool for ceramic-implant quality assurance, enabling low-magnification pre-screening and reducing reliance on time-intensive high-magnification inspection.

Abstract: Reliable identification of fracture origins in alumina matrix composite hip and knee implants is critical for quality assurance and patient safety, yet current fractographic workflows are time-consuming, partly subjective, and reliant on high-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM). We present an interpretable vision-transformer (ViT) workflow for automated classification of fracture causes in an alumina matrix composite (BIOLOX delta, CeramTec GmbH) widely used in total joint replacements. A dataset of 8,493 SEM images (50x-10,000x) was curated from five years of in-production burst and proof tests and annotated into three defect categories defined along the manufacturing chain: green body, hard machining, and material defects. Under severe class imbalance, the fine-tuned ViT reached an accuracy of 0.907 and a macro-F1 of 0.888 in stratified five-fold cross-validation, with a two-stage perceptual-hash/SSIM leakage audit confirming negligible specimen overlap. Notably, performance at low magnification (50x) was comparable to that at high magnification (1k-10kx), indicating that macro-scale features - mirror geometry and hackle line fields - already encode sufficient diagnostic signal. Grad-CAM attributions consistently localised on canonical fractographic cues (mirrors, hackles, pores, machining marks), aligning with established fractographic criteria. Together, these results position interpretable ViTs as a complementary tool for ceramic-implant quality assurance, enabling low-magnification pre-screening and reducing reliance on time-intensive high-magnification inspection.

</details>


### [8] [Lightweight Complementary-Cue Fusion for Robust Video Face Forgery Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29092)
*Sunghwan Baek,Tariq Anwaar,Karanveer Singh,Rita Singh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight fusion block adding only 292 parameters to Xception combines handcrafted features (wavelet-denoised + phase-spectrum or LBP) and achieves 3.8-4.4% AUC gains over baseline, outperforming larger models like F3Net and SRM across eight benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current face video forgery detectors rely on large, wide, or dual-stream backbones. The authors argue that carefully paired handcrafted features can achieve better accuracy with a much smaller model, challenging the trend toward scale-driven design.

Method: Two detectors built on Xception baseline (21.9M params): LFWS (fusion of low-frequency Wavelet-Denoised Feature + phase-spectrum from SPSL) and LFWL (fusion of WDF + Local Binary Patterns). Both use a single 1x1 convolution block adding only 292 parameters.

Result: AUC improves from 74.8% to 78.6% on FaceForensics++ and from 70.5% to 74.9% on DFDC-Preview (gains of 3.8% and 4.4%). Consistently outperforms F3Net, SRM, and SPSL across eight public benchmarks without extra data or test-time augmentation.

Conclusion: Carefully paired handcrafted features fused via a lightweight block provide competitive robustness at significantly lower cost than comparable frequency-based detectors, suggesting a need to reevaluate scale-driven design choices in face video forgery detection.

Abstract: Current face video forgery detectors use wide or dual-stream backbones. We show that a single, lightweight fusion of two handcrafted cues can achieve higher accuracy with a much smaller model. Based on the Xception baseline model (21.9 million parameters), we build two detectors: LFWS, which adds a 1x1 convolution to combine a low-frequency Wavelet-Denoised Feature (WDF) with a phase-spectrum channel derived from Spatial-Phase Shallow Learning (SPSL), and LFWL, which merges WDF with Local Binary Patterns (LBP) in the same way. This extra module adds only 292 parameters, keeping the total at 21.9 million, smaller than F3Net (22.5 million) and less than half the size of SRM (55.3 million). Even with this minimal overhead, the fused models increase the average area under the curve (AUC) from 74.8% to 78.6% on FaceForensics++ and from 70.5% to 74.9% on DFDC-Preview, gains of 3.8% and 4.4% over the Xception baseline. They also consistently outperform F3Net, SRM, and SPSL in eight public benchmarks, without extra data or test-time augmentation. These results show that carefully paired, handcrafted features, combined through the lightweight fusion block, can provide competitive robustness at a significantly lower cost than comparable frequency-based detectors. Our findings suggest a need to reevaluate scale-driven design choices in face video forgery detection.

</details>


### [9] [Boosting Image Quality Assessment Performance: Unsupervised Score Fusion by Deep Maximum a Posteriori Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30269)
*Zhongling Wang,Raymond Zhou,Shahrukh Athar,Wenbo Yang,Zhou Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an unsupervised IQA score fusion framework using deep MAP estimation to combine multiple biased IQA models into a stronger one with uncertainty estimation and bad model rejection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Individual IQA models are often biased toward certain image content or distortions. Fusing multiple models can harness strengths and mitigate weaknesses.

Method: A general framework for unsupervised IQA score fusion using deep Maximum a Posteriori (MAP) estimation with fine-grained uncertainty estimation at the score level.

Result: The proposed model outperforms individual IQA models and other fusion methods, and demonstrates capability of rejecting bad models during fusion.

Conclusion: Deep MAP estimation is an effective approach for unsupervised IQA score fusion, improving accuracy while reducing uncertainty.

Abstract: Over the past decades, numerous Image Quality Assessment (IQA) models have emerged, aiming to predict the perceptual quality of images. However, individual models are often biased toward certain types of image content or distortions, depending on the design principle and process. An intuitive idea is to harness the strengths and mitigate the weaknesses of each IQA model, by fusing the scores of multiple models into a stronger one. Here we make one of the first attempts to seek an optimal solution for the idea and propose a general framework for unsupervised IQA score fusion using deep Maximum a Posteriori (MAP) estimation. The proposed model conducts fine-grained uncertainty estimation at the score level to increase the accuracy and reduce the uncertainty in fused predictions. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of the proposed model over individual IQA models and other fusion methods. It also exhibits an interesting capability of rejecting ``bad" models in the fusion process.

</details>


### [10] [GeRaF: Neural Geometry Reconstruction from Radio Frequency Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29097)
*Jiachen Lu,Hailan Shanbhag,Haitham Al Hassanieh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeRaF introduces neural implicit learning for 3D geometry reconstruction from RF signals, addressing challenges like noise, cubic complexity in volumetric rendering, and specular reflections through filter-based rendering, physics-based RF volumetric rendering, and lensless sampling/alpha blending strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RGB and LiDAR methods fail under occlusion, while RF signals can see through occlusions but suffer from low resolution, noise, and fundamentally different signal propagation (lensless, full-space sampling, specular reflections).

Method: GeRaF uses (1) filter-based rendering to suppress irrelevant signals, (2) a physics-based RF volumetric rendering pipeline, (3) lensless sampling and lensless alpha blending for feasible full-space training, with MLPs learning signed distance functions, reflectiveness, and signal power.

Result: GeRaF achieves near-range 3D geometry reconstruction from RF signals, enabling millimeter-level geometry reconstruction in real-world settings for the first time.

Conclusion: GeRaF is the first method to successfully apply neural implicit learning to RF-based 3D reconstruction, overcoming fundamental challenges in lensless, noisy, specular RF signal modeling.

Abstract: GeRaF is the first method to use neural implicit learning for near-range 3D geometry reconstruction from radio frequency (RF) signals. Unlike RGB or LiDAR-based methods, RF sensing can see through occlusion but suffers from low resolution and noise due to its lensless imaging nature. While lenses in RGB imaging constrain sampling to 1D rays, RF signals propagate through the entire space, introducing significant noise and leading to cubic complexity in volumetric rendering. Moreover, RF signals interact with surfaces via specular reflections, requiring fundamentally different modeling. To address these challenges, GeRaF (1) introduces filter-based rendering to suppress irrelevant signals, (2) implements a physics-based RF volumetric rendering pipeline, and (3) proposes a novel lensless sampling and lensless alpha blending strategy that makes full-space sampling feasible during training. By learning signed distance functions, reflectiveness, and signal power through MLPs and trainable parameters, GeRaF takes the first step towards reconstructing millimeter-level geometry from RF signals in real-world settings.

</details>


### [11] [Seeing through boxes: Non-Line-of-Sight 3D Reconstruction from Radar Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29098)
*Jiachen Lu,Hailan Shanbhag,Haitham Al Hassanieh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeRaF 2.0 is a unified neural framework that leverages both Line-of-Sight and Non-Line-of-Sight geometry to reconstruct object shapes from RF signals, using visual LoS priors to achieve stable training and state-of-the-art performance in RF-based geometry reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing NLoS 3D neural reconstruction methods suffer from unstable optimization, noisy surface geometry, and surface ambiguity due to neglecting the role of LoS geometry outside enclosed regions, which provides useful physical constraints for signal propagation.

Method: GeRaF 2.0 integrates visual LoS priors into a neural field formulation to model and guide RF propagation from LoS into NLoS regions, enabling unified reconstruction of both visible and hidden geometry.

Result: Achieves stable training, physically consistent reconstruction, and sets a new state-of-the-art in RF-based geometry reconstruction.

Conclusion: Leveraging LoS geometry priors is key to overcoming limitations of existing NLoS neural reconstruction, enabling accurate and robust RF-based 3D reconstruction.

Abstract: Reconstructing object geometry from radio frequency (RF) signals is fundamentally challenging due to the lensless imaging nature of RF sensing, which leads to low spatial resolution and high noise. Unlike light signals, RF signals can penetrate occlusions and thus capture information about hidden scenes. Existing Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) 3D neural reconstruction methods can recover coarse surfaces inside enclosed environments but often suffer from unstable optimization, noisy surface geometry, and surface ambiguity, failing to produce accurate zero-level sets from the signed distance field (SDF). These limitations largely stem from neglecting the role of Line-of-Sight (LoS) geometry outside the enclosed region, which provides valuable physical constraints for modeling signal propagation. In this paper, we introduce a Unified LoS and NLoS neural geometry reconstruction framework GeRaF 2.0 that leverages the outside LoS geometry to model and guide RF propagation from the LoS region into the NLoS region. By integrating visual LoS priors into the neural field formulation, GeRaF 2.0 achieves stable training and physically consistent reconstruction of both visible and hidden geometry, setting a new state-of-the-art in RF-based geometry reconstruction.

</details>


### [12] [Robust Cross-Domain Generalization Using Unlabeled Target Data with Source-Domain Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29122)
*Yuyue Zhou,Shrimanti Ghosh,Michael,Xie,Justin JY Kim,Jessica Knight,Steel McDonald,Vincent Man,Jacob L. Jaremko,Abhilash Hareendranathan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a target-informed self-supervised pretraining and model-ensemble strategy to improve cross-device generalization of ultrasound AI for pediatric wrist fracture assessment, achieving over 6% Dice improvement on an unlabeled target domain while keeping source and target datasets strictly separate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical imaging AI models trained with dense annotations generalize poorly to data from different ultrasound scanners or clinical sites due to domain shifts, and retraining with new annotations is difficult, costly, and raises data privacy concerns.

Method: The approach combines masked image modeling (MIM) and contrastive learning for target-domain self-supervised pretraining without labels, plus a confidence-aware infusion head for adaptive integration of predictions. Labeled source data (Philips Lumify probe) is used for supervised training, while unlabeled target data (TeleMED portable probe) is used for pretraining with strict separation.

Result: On 318 images from 62 pediatric POCUS videos, the method achieved over 6% Dice improvement on the target domain compared to the baseline.

Conclusion: This is a label-efficient and privacy-preserving approach for cross-device-robust ultrasound AI, extendable to multi-center studies or federated learning setups.

Abstract: It is often desirable to generalize medical imaging AI models trained with dense annotations to data acquired from different ultrasound scanners or clinical sites; however, retraining these models with new annotations is often difficult and costly. We examine this challenge in pediatric wrist fracture assessment using point-of-care ultrasound (POCUS), where fractures are common and can be effectively triaged via ultrasound. AI has shown radiologist-level performance for fracture detection, often aided by high-quality bony structure segmentation. However, due to significant domain shifts, models perform poorly on data from other centers or probes, and obtaining segmentation labels across devices is impractical due to manual annotation effort and data privacy concerns. To address this, we propose a target-informed self-supervised pretraining and model-ensemble strategy. Specifically, our approach combines masked image modeling (MIM) and contrastive learning to learn target-domain structural representations without labels, and introduces a confidence-aware infusion head to adaptively integrate predictions. The source dataset, collected with a Philips Lumify probe, contained dense labels, while the target dataset, acquired with a TeleMED portable probe, was unlabeled. The datasets were kept strictly separate throughout the entire process. Our method used labeled source data for supervised training and leveraged target-domain pretraining to improve generalization. On 318 images from 62 pediatric POCUS videos, this approach significantly improved cross-device performance, achieving over 6% Dice improvement on the target domain versus the baseline. These results demonstrate a label-efficient and privacy-preserving approach for cross-device-robust ultrasound AI, offering a framework that can be extended to multi-center studies or federated learning setups.

</details>


### [13] [Eulerian Gaussian Splatting using Hashed Probability Pyramids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29136)
*Mia Gaia Polansky,George Kopanas,Stephan Garbin,Todd Zickler,Dor Verbin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A probabilistic splat-based radiance field that replaces heuristic 3DGS densification with gradient-based optimization of a learnable density via a multi-scale hierarchical grid and variance-reduced gradient estimator, achieving SOTA quality on mip-NeRF 360 with 3DGS-level speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D Gaussian Splatting relies on hand-tuned heuristics (e.g., ADC) for primitive relocation, splitting, and culling. The authors aim to eliminate these brittle priors by replacing them with principled gradient-based optimization of a volumetric probability density.

Method: (1) Treat Gaussian primitive locations as samples from a persistent learnable density. (2) Instantiate the density via a memory-efficient multi-scale hierarchical grid for end-to-end optimization. (3) Derive an unbiased gradient estimator with control variates to reduce variance and stabilize training.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality on the mip-NeRF 360 benchmark while preserving the real-time rendering speed of 3DGS.

Conclusion: By replacing heuristic primitive manipulation with learned density optimization, the framework improves reconstruction quality, eliminates brittle priors, and enables natural volume exploration without sacrificing rendering efficiency.

Abstract: We introduce a probabilistic splat-based radiance field framework that retains the fast rasterization and test-time efficiency of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) while replacing heuristic primitive manipulation with gradient-based optimization of a volumetric probability density. Rather than relocating, splitting, or culling Gaussians via hand-tuned densification (e.g., ADC), we treat primitive locations as samples drawn from a persistent, learnable density. We instantiate this density using a novel, memory-efficient multi-scale hierarchical grid that enables end-to-end gradient-based optimization. To stabilize the optimization, we derive an unbiased gradient estimator with control variates that markedly reduces variance. By allowing probability mass to flow to where the loss demands, our framework eliminates brittle priors and naturally explores the volume, achieving state-of-the-art reconstruction quality on mip-NeRF 360 while preserving 3DGS-level rendering speed.

</details>


### [14] [Uni-RCM: Unified Reference-guided Cross-modal Mapping for Multi-Class Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29455)
*Yangchen Wu,Huiqiang Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Uni-RCM is a unified cross-modal framework for multi-class industrial anomaly detection that uses a reference guide block to filter category-specific noise and an offline residual quantizer to model normal distributions, achieving SOTA on MVTec-3D AD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-modal anomaly detection methods require separate models per product category, limiting scalability. Unified models suffer from inter-class interference and feature confusion, degrading accuracy.

Method: (1) A reference guide block with learnable reference features to dynamically filter category-specific noise across modalities. (2) An offline residual quantizer using multiple cascaded codebooks to characterize normal distributions.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on MVTec-3D AD dataset in multi-class setting for both image-level detection and pixel-level localization.

Conclusion: Uni-RCM effectively addresses inter-class interference in unified multi-class anomaly detection, achieving superior scalability and accuracy.

Abstract: Multi-modal industrial anomaly detection typically relies on separate models for each product category, fundamentally limiting practical scalability. When shifting to a unified paradigm that handles diverse classes simultaneously, detection accuracy often degrades due to inter-class interference and feature manifold confusion. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Unified Reference guided Cross-modal Mapping framework, named Uni-RCM. At its core, we propose a reference guide block to dynamically filter out category-specific noise by introducing a learnable reference feature, which captures the commonalities across different modalities. Besides, an offline residual quantizer is proposed to characterize the normal distribution by multiple cascaded codebooks. Extensive evaluations on the MVTec-3D AD dataset demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance in the challenging multi-class setting and in terms of image-level detection and pixel-level localization.

</details>


### [15] [Guidance Contrastive Token Credit Assignment for Discrete Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29198)
*Shufan Li,Konstantinos Kallidromitis,Akash Gokul Yusuke Kato,Kazuki Kozuka,Aditya Grover*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GCPO improves reinforcement learning by assigning token-level advantages via contrastive predictions, outperforming GRPO and DAPO on text-to-image generation and chain-of-thought reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing group-advantage-based RL methods (GRPO, DAPO) use sample-level rewards that assign uniform credit across all tokens, failing to capture fine-grained, token-level contributions.

Method: GCPO (Guidance Contrastive Policy Optimization) enables per-token credit assignment by contrasting model predictions under positive and negative prompts, assigning token-level advantages proportional to the difference between these contrastive predictions.

Result: GCPO emphasizes semantically relevant regions (visual areas aligned with prompts in text-to-image, critical keywords in reasoning traces) and consistently outperforms GRPO and DAPO baselines on both benchmarks.

Conclusion: GCPO is an effective, general, and scalable optimization strategy for discrete policy learning.

Abstract: Group-advantage-based reinforcement learning methods, such as GRPO and DAPO, have demonstrated strong performance across diverse domains, including mathematical reasoning and text-to-image generation. However, their reliance on sample-level rewards introduces a key limitation as uniform credit assignment across all tokens fails to capture fine-grained, token-level contributions. To address this issue, we propose Guidance Contrastive Policy Optimization (GCPO), a novel algorithm that enables per-token credit assignment by contrasting model predictions under positive and negative prompts. Rather than uniformly broadcasting sample-level advantages, GCPO assigns token-level advantages proportional to the difference between these contrastive predictions, allowing more precise and informative learning signals. Empirically, we find that GCPO emphasizes semantically relevant regions such as visual areas aligned with textual prompts in text-to-image generation, and critical keywords within reasoning traces for chain-of-thought tasks. Through extensive experiments, GCPO consistently outperforms GRPO and DAPO baselines on both text-to-image generation and chain-of-thought reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness as a general and scalable optimization strategy for discrete policy learning.

</details>


### [16] [MetaRanker: Human-in-the-loop Active Ranking for Metalens Image Quality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29212)
*Yujin Park,Haejun Chung,Ikbeom Jang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MetaRanker is a human-in-the-loop active ranking framework that evaluates metalens image quality based on semantic interpretability (human recognition reliability) rather than traditional distortion-based metrics like PSNR, achieving rankings closely aligned with human assessments while reducing pairwise annotations by ~80%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional distortion-based fidelity objectives (e.g., PSNR) used for training reconstruction models in metalens imaging pipelines are weakly correlated with human preference and downstream utility, reflecting the perception-distortion trade-off. A better evaluation metric grounded in human interpretability is needed.

Method: MetaRanker combines a probabilistic preference model with uncertainty-aware query selection and leverages vision-language models (VLMs) as lightweight semantic priors to guide the sampling of informative pairwise comparisons. Human judgments remain the primary supervision signal, while VLMs only help prioritize which comparisons to query.

Result: Across real-world and synthetic metalens datasets with distinct degradation profiles, MetaRanker produces rankings most closely aligned with human assessments and reduces pairwise annotation effort by approximately 80% compared to exhaustive pairwise evaluation.

Conclusion: Standard IQA metrics show limited alignment with human interpretability in the metalens domain. MetaRanker offers a practical, perceptually grounded approach for metalens evaluation and co-design, bridging the gap between computational reconstruction and human perception.

Abstract: Image quality in modern imaging systems emerges from the coupled effects of the sensor, optics, and computational reconstruction. Ultra-thin metalenses offer a path toward substantial miniaturization of optical modules, but practical designs often exhibit pronounced chromatic and field-dependent aberrations that necessitate computational reconstruction. In current metalens pipelines, reconstruction models are commonly trained and selected using distortion-based fidelity objectives, such as PSNR, yet these proxies can be weakly correlated with human preference and downstream utility, reflecting the well-known perception--distortion trade-off. We introduce MetaRanker, a human-in-the-loop active ranking framework that formalizes metalens image quality in terms of semantic interpretability, defined as the degree to which humans can reliably recognize objects and structures in the presence of optical artifacts. MetaRanker combines a probabilistic preference model with uncertainty-aware query selection, and leverages vision--language models to provide lightweight semantic priors. Importantly, these priors are used only to guide the sampling of informative comparisons; human judgments remain the primary supervision signal throughout. Across real-world and synthetic metalens datasets with distinct degradation profiles, MetaRanker produces rankings that align most closely with human assessments, while reducing the number of pairwise annotations required by approximately 80% relative to exhaustive pairwise evaluation. Finally, we show that standard image quality assessment metrics exhibit limited alignment with human interpretability in the metalens domain, positioning MetaRanker as a practical step toward perceptually grounded metalens evaluation and co-design.

</details>


### [17] [Towards the automated segmentation of epicardial and mediastinal fats: A multi-manufacturer approach using intersubject registration and random forest](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29217)
*É. O. Rodrigues,A. Conci,F. F. C. Morais,M. G. Pérez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a fully automatic method to segment two types of cardiac adipose tissues (epicardial and mediastinal fat) on CT images using registration, feature extraction, and data mining classification, achieving 98.4% accuracy and 96.8% Dice similarity index.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cardiac fat quantity is correlated with various health risks (carotid stiffness, coronary artery calcification, atrial fibrillation, etc.) and varies independently from overall body fat, making its quantitative analysis essential for clinical assessment.

Method: The method involves three steps: (1) registration to align input images to a standard, (2) feature extraction from pixels and their surroundings, and (3) segmentation using data mining classification algorithms to classify each pixel as epicardial fat, mediastinal fat, or other tissue.

Result: Mean accuracy of 98.4% for epicardial and mediastinal fat segmentation, mean true positive rate of 96.2%, and mean Dice similarity index of 96.8%.

Conclusion: The proposed fully automatic method effectively segments cardiac adipose tissues with high accuracy and reproducibility, requiring minimal user intervention, making it suitable for clinical decision support systems.

Abstract: The amount of fat on the surroundings of the heart is correlated to several health risk factors such as carotid stiffness, coronary artery calcification, atrial fibrillation, atherosclerosis, cancer incidence and others. Furthermore, the cardiac fat varies unrelated to the overall fat of the subject, and, therefore, it reinforces the quantitative analysis of these adipose tissues as being essential. Clinical decision support systems are computer programs capable of evaluating information and providing a corresponding diagnosis or data to complement the physicists' analyses. The aim of this work is to propose a method capable of fully automatically segmenting two types of cardiac adipose tissues that stand apart from each other by the pericardium on CT images obtained by the standard acquisition protocol used for coronary calcium scoring. Much effort was devoted to promote minimal user intervention and ease of reproducibility. The methodology proposed in this work consists of a registration, which will roughly adjust input images to a standard, an extraction of features related to pixels and their surrounding area and a segmentation step based on data mining classification algorithms that define if an incoming pixel is of a certain type. Experimentations showed that the achieved mean accuracy for the epicardial and mediastinal fats was 98.4% with a mean true positive rate of 96.2%. In average, the Dice similarity index was equal to 96.8%.

</details>


### [18] [SalsaAgent: A multimodal embodied language model for interactive dance generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29219)
*Payam Jome Yazdian,Zoe Stanley,Angelica Lim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SalsaAgent is an LLM-based system that generates expressive, full-body salsa dance motions reacting to a human leader and music by extending LLM vocabulary with motion tokens, pairwise relation tokens, and audio tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable socially aware robots and virtual agents that can interact with humans through bidirectional, nonverbal, and synchronized full-body dance motions.

Method: Formulates interaction as nonverbal motion token passing, extends LLM vocabulary with motion/relation tokens, fine-tunes LLM with auto-generated text descriptions for token grounding, and uses a two-stage token-to-diffusion pipeline.

Result: Significant improvements over baselines in motion quality, music/partner coordination, and consistent two-person spatial behavior, validated by subjective and objective evaluations.

Conclusion: The approach effectively enables LLMs to generate human-like interactive dance motions with spatial and rhythmic coordination.

Abstract: Interaction between humanoids involves bidirectional and nonverbal reactivity, coordination and synchrony. Toward socially aware robots and interactive virtual agents, we present SalsaAgent, a language model that generates expressive, full-body salsa dance motions in reaction to a human leader and against a contextual music backdrop. We formulate interaction as nonverbal motion token passing, extending the vocabulary of a large language model (LLM) to process discrete motion tokens, pairwise relation tokens, and audio. Our contributions include new tokens for full-body and motion relations, LLM fine-tuning using automatically derived text descriptions of skeleton dynamics for token grounding, and a two-stage token-to-diffusion pipeline. Subjective and objective evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in terms of motion quality, music and partner coordination, and consistent two-person spatial behavior, with significant improvements over baselines.

</details>


### [19] [Motion-guided sparse correction enables expert-quality point tracking across diverse microscopy regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29220)
*Leonidas Zimianitis,Pasindu Thenahandi,Kai Buckhalter,Dineth Jayakody,Julian O. Kimura,Xinyue Liang,Karen Cunningham,Azeem Ahmad,Balpreet S. Ahluwalia,Sampath Jayarathna,Nikos Chrisochoides,Brandon Weissbourd,Dushan N. Wadduwage*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RIPPLE is a semi-automated tracking tool that requires only sparse manual corrections (click start, fix drift points) to produce gold-standard trajectories, reducing manual clicks by 3-25x while matching exhaustive annotation quality across challenging microscopy datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exhaustive manual annotation for tracking non-canonical biological systems in microscopy videos does not scale, and both classical and learning-based trackers require expert-reviewed data that is costly to produce.

Method: RIPPLE recasts annotation as sparse correction: user clicks a starting point, the system proposes a full trajectory, and the user intervenes only where the trajectory drifts, then RIPPLE refines the path.

Result: Tested on five challenging microscopy datasets (four from Clytia hemisphaerica jellyfish, one from sperm tracking). RIPPLE matched exhaustive manual annotation quality while reducing manual clicks by 3 to 25 times across datasets.

Conclusion: RIPPLE fills a missing layer between manual annotation and fully automated tracking, enabling immediate quantification, benchmarking, and production of gold-standard data for future trackers.

Abstract: Tracking the dynamics of non-canonical biological systems in microscopy videos remains a persistent challenge. Both classical and learning-based trackers depend on expert-reviewed data to be evaluated and adapted, yet exhaustive manual annotation rarely scales to the videos where these tools are needed most. We developed RIPPLE (Refinement Interpolation Platform for Point Location Estimation), which recasts annotation as sparse correction: a user clicks a starting point, RIPPLE proposes a full trajectory, and the user intervenes only where the trajectory drifts. We tested RIPPLE on five challenging microscopy datasets from our laboratories, four from the transparent jellyfish Clytia hemisphaerica and one tracking landmarks on rapidly moving sperm. Across these, RIPPLE matched the quality of exhaustive manual annotation while reducing manual clicks by 3 to 25 times across datasets. RIPPLE thereby fills a missing layer between manual annotation and fully automated tracking, enabling immediate quantification of biological dynamics, method benchmarking, and the production of the gold-standard data needed to adapt future automated microscopy trackers.

</details>


### [20] [An Approach for Thyroid Nodule Analysis Using Thermographic Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29221)
*J. R. González,É. O. Rodrigues,C. P. Damião,C. A. P. Fontes,A. C. Silva,A. C. Paiva,H. Li,C. Du,A. Conci*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a pilot project for thyroid cancer detection using thermography, proposing acquisition protocols, autonomous image registration, feature extraction, and classification methods to support early diagnosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Thyroid cancer is projected to become the second most common cancer in women and third in men by 2030. Early detection improves survival, but physicians face information overload in medical diagnosis. Thermography offers a non-invasive diagnostic tool, but standardized protocols and automated analysis for thyroid thermograms are lacking.

Method: The authors conduct a general review of thermography focused on thyroid analysis, propose image acquisition protocols, develop an autonomous registration method for thyroid thermograms, perform feature extraction and image processing, and outline a classification approach to differentiate healthy from unhealthy patients.

Result: This is a pilot project description; results are not yet reported. The work establishes protocols and methodologies for a future study to be submitted for ethics committee approval at HUAP-UFF and the Brazilian Ministry of Health.

Conclusion: This pilot project aims to support preventive medical actions in endocrinology by evaluating thermography's importance for diagnosing thyroid nodules, pending ethics approval and future adjustments.

Abstract: Thyroid cancer is said to be the second most common type of cancer in female individuals and the third in males by 2030, according to projections. In general, detecting cancer in its early stages improves the chance of survival of the individual. Thermography is a diagnostic tool that has been increasingly used to detect cancer and abnormalities, including that of thyroid. Various methods to segment and detect hot regions in thermograms and, consequently, to detect suspicious tissues present in these images have been proposed. It is well known that medical diagnosis yields a great deal of information. Thus, physicians have to comprehensively analyse and evaluate this information in a short period of time, which is infeasible in most cases. In this work, we perform a general review of thermography , focusing on the thyroid analysis. We propose protocols for image acquisiton and an autonomous registration for thyroid images. We also perform analyses of the image data, which include feature extraction, image processing, and a possible approach for classification of healthy or unhealthy patients. In summary, this work presents a pilot project for detection of tumors in our university hospital, which is part of an effort to support preventive medical actions in our endocrinology department. Under some future adjustments, this project will be submitted for approval by the ethics and research committee of Hospital Universitário Antonio Pedro at Universidade Federal Fluminense (HUAP-UFF) and to the Brazilian Ministry of Health Ethical committee under the name: Evaluation of the importance of thermography to aid diagnosis of thyroid nodules of patients in HUAP-UFF (in Portuguese: Avaliação da importância da termografia no auxílio à investigação diagnóstica de nódulos tireoidianos em pacientes acompanhados no HUAP-UFF).

</details>


### [21] [Toward Ethical Facial Age Estimation: A Generalized Zero-Shot Benchmark Without Training on Children's Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29230)
*Caio Petrucci,Leo Sampaio Ferraz Ribeiro,Sandra Avila*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a generalized zero-shot benchmark for facial age estimation that excludes children's data from training while still evaluating on younger populations, revealing that all nine tested methods fail to generalize to unseen age groups with up to 52.8% performance degradation due to seen-class bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Age estimation from facial images typically requires training data including minors, raising serious ethical, legal, and privacy concerns. The authors aim to address this by formalizing a benchmark that excludes children's data during training while still assessing model performance on younger populations.

Method: The authors revisit six datasets and introduce standardized splits with strict age-group separation: ages 18-59 for training/validation/testing, under 18 exclusively for zero-shot evaluation, and 60+ as unseen validation set. Subject-exclusive splits prevent identity leakage. They evaluate nine state-of-the-art age estimation methods under this protocol.

Result: All evaluated methods consistently fail to generalize to unseen age groups, suffering on average 46.4% and up to 52.8% performance degradation relative to supervised baseline. Models systematically anchor predictions for unseen ages to nearby seen classes, exhibiting seen-class bias.

Conclusion: This work highlights a critical gap between current modeling practices and real-world ethical constraints, providing a principled benchmark for evaluating models under restricted data regimes and encouraging development of methods robust to distribution shift and aligned with responsible data use.

Abstract: Age estimation from facial images typically relies on training data that includes images of minors, a practice that raises serious ethical, legal, and privacy concerns. In this work, we propose a generalized zero-shot benchmark for facial age estimation that explicitly excludes children's data during training while still assessing model performance on younger populations. We revisit six widely used datasets and introduce standardized splits with strict age-group separation: samples aged 18-59 for training, validation, and testing; samples under 18 reserved exclusively for zero-shot evaluation; and samples 60+ as an unseen validation set for model selection under distribution shift. For datasets with identity annotations, subject-exclusive splits prevent identity leakage and better reflect real-world deployment conditions. Evaluating nine state-of-the-art age estimation methods under this protocol reveals that all evaluated methods consistently fail to generalize to unseen age groups, suffering substantial performance degradation -- on average 46.4%, and up to 52.8% -- relative to the supervised baseline. Moreover, models do not simply degrade: they systematically anchor predictions for unseen ages to nearby seen classes, a manifestation of the well-known seen-class bias in generalized zero-shot learning. By formalizing age estimation without children's data as a generalized zero-shot benchmark on existing datasets, this work highlights a critical gap between current modeling practices and real-world ethical constraints. Our benchmark provides a principled basis for evaluating models under restricted data regimes and encourages the development of methods that are robust to distribution shift and aligned with responsible data use.

</details>


### [22] [Deep Psychovisual Image Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29260)
*Wendi Ma,Aryaman Sharma,Wei Dai,Shekhar S. Chandra*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Deep Visual Coding, a frequency-domain representation inspired by 1990s psychovisual models, which together with complex-valued representations enables a psychovisual-based deep learning framework that produces more interpretable and less depth-dependent vision models than standard CNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard deep learning vision models use homogeneous spatial layers that are opaque and difficult to interpret, contrasting with psychovisual models where low-level features are decoupled from higher cognition via intermediate abstractions.

Method: Learned frequency-domain representation (Deep Visual Coding) inspired by 1990s image codes that quantise perceptually salient frequencies, combined with complex-valued image representations and data-driven spectral filters to encode task-relevant semantic structures within distinct frequency sub-bands.

Result: Psychovisual models extract highly interpretable object parts compared to the amorphous regions from CNNs, and are less depth-dependent than CNNs because complex-valued representations and learned abstractions subsume the role of deep spatial layers.

Conclusion: Psychovisual coding offers a promising path toward more efficient and transparent vision models.

Abstract: Psychovisual models suggest human vision decouples low-level feature extraction from higher cognition by first forming intermediate abstractions. In contrast, deep learning-based vision models routinely extract and aggregate features using homogeneous stacks of spatial layers, rendering their decision-making processes opaque. In this paper, we propose Deep Visual Coding, a learned frequency-domain representation inspired by 1990s image codes that quantised perceptually salient frequencies, which together with complex-valued image representations produces psychovisual-style abstractions. This approach enables the first psychovisual-based deep learning framework, utilizing data-driven spectral filters that learn to encode task-relevant semantic structures within distinct frequency sub-bands. Salience analyses reveal that our psychovisual models extract highly interpretable object parts compared to the amorphous regions produced by regular Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). Furthermore, we find that our models are less depth dependent than CNNs for model scaling, since our complex-valued representations and learned abstractions subsume the role of the deep spatial layers. Together, these findings demonstrate that psychovisual coding provides a promising path toward more efficient and transparent vision models.

</details>


### [23] [Turbulence-Robust Dynamic Object Segmentation with Multi-Signal Priors and SAM2 Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29292)
*Bolian Peng,Ying Tang,Xu Liu,Long Sun,Xiaoqiang Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free, multi-signal segmentation pipeline combining RAFT, DINOv2, ViBe, and SAM2 achieves 0.425 mIoU and 0.457 mDice on CVPR 2026 UG2+ DOST challenge for dynamic object segmentation under atmospheric turbulence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Atmospheric turbulence causes pseudo-motion, blur, and intermittent target visibility, making single motion cues unreliable for object segmentation. A training-free approach is needed to handle the DOST setting without task-specific fine-tuning.

Method: A training-free multi-signal segmentation pipeline combining: RAFT for dense motion responses, DINOv2 for semantic objectness priors, ViBe for background anomaly modeling, manual proposal fusion calibration, and SAM2 for box-prompt mask refinement.

Result: Achieved 0.425041 mIoU and 0.457206 mDice on the official leaderboard.

Conclusion: The training-free pipeline is effective for turbulence-degraded video segmentation, but stronger learned temporal association, adaptive proposal selection, or task-specific adaptation could further improve performance.

Abstract: This technical report presents our solution for the CVPR 2026 UG2+ Challenge Track 3: Dynamic Object Segmentation in Turbulence (DOST). We design a training-free multi-signal segmentation pipeline that combines pretrained motion estimation, self-supervised semantic priors, background anomaly modeling, manually calibrated proposal fusion, and SAM2-based mask refinement. The method uses RAFT for dense motion responses, DINOv2 for semantic objectness priors, ViBe for training-free background modeling, and pretrained SAM2 for box-prompt mask refinement.
  Instead of optimizing an end-to-end segmentation network, our system operates entirely in inference mode. This design is suitable for the DOST setting, where severe atmospheric turbulence produces pseudo-motion, blur, and intermittent target visibility, making a single motion cue unreliable. The final submitted masks are evaluated by the official leaderboard, which reports 0.425041 mIoU and 0.457206 mDice. Since no task-specific model training or fine-tuning is performed, stronger learned temporal association, adaptive proposal selection, or task-specific adaptation may further improve the system.

</details>


### [24] [Pocket-Dentist: On-Device Dental Image Understanding via Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29299)
*Kai Bian,Xucheng Guo,Bin Chen,Lingyan Ruan,Yiran Shen,Ting Dang,Hong Jia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pocket-Dentist is an efficiency-aware benchmark showing that compact 2B VLMs outperform larger models in dental image understanding accuracy while requiring far less computational cost, enabling real-time local deployment on an iPhone for privacy-preserving dental screening.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of dental VLMs are fragmented across datasets, tasks, and metrics, and ignore computational cost, which hinders practical deployment in non-specialist settings where timely inference, limited hardware, and privacy-preserving local processing are critical.

Method: The authors construct an efficiency-aware benchmark (Pocket-Dentist) using three datasets (~1,159 patients), five task types, and seven metrics, then evaluate 14 VLMs (ranging from 2B to 7B parameters) and finetune a compact model deployed locally on an iPhone 17 Pro.

Result: Compact 2B VLMs achieve higher accuracy than larger 7B models with substantially lower computational costs. The finetuned Pocket-Dentist-2B on iPhone 17 Pro achieves 4.31s per sample, with 4.9x lower latency and 2.3x lower memory use compared to a 7B baseline.

Conclusion: Compact VLMs are more suitable than larger models for practical, privacy-preserving dental prescreening on local devices, enabling efficient deployment outside specialist centres.

Abstract: Evaluations of dental vision-language models remain fragmented across datasets, task definitions and metrics, and often ignore their computational cost. This limits their widespread deployment for dental screening outside specialist centres, where timely inference, limited hardware, and local handling of patient images are vital for practical, privacy-preserving clinical prescreening. Here we present Pocket-Dentist, an efficiency-aware benchmark for dental multimodal question answering that brings together three datasets spanning approximately 1,159 patients, five task types and seven metrics. Across typical 14 VLMs, our results reveals an interesting observation: compact VLMs (e.g., 2B-parameter models) outperform larger VLMs in accuracy while requiring substantially lower computational costs in dental image understanding. Deployed locally on an iPhone 17 Pro, our finetuned compact VLM Pocket-Dentist-2B processed each sample in 4.31 s, reducing latency by 4.9-fold and memory use by 2.3-fold compared with a 7B baseline.

</details>


### [25] [ViASNet: A Video Ad Saliency Network for Predicting Dynamic Saliency and Viewer Engagement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29302)
*Jianping Ye,Michel Wedel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces ViASNet, a 3D U-Net-based deep saliency prediction model for short-form video advertising that incorporates audio and semantic scene information, evaluated on 151 ads with eye-tracking data from ~20 viewers each, and demonstrates its utility for ad design and testing through entropy-based diagnostic analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the growing importance of short-form video advertising across digital platforms by developing a deep saliency prediction model that can understand and predict viewer attention patterns, ultimately enabling faster and more effective ad design and testing.

Method: The proposed ViASNet model is built on a 3D U-Net architecture, integrates audio features and semantic scene understanding, and is evaluated on 151 video ads with eye-tracking data from approximately 20 viewers each. Ablation experiments identify critical factors influencing performance, and frame-by-frame entropy of predicted saliency maps serves as a diagnostic tool for detecting unengaging ads/scenes.

Result: ViASNet effectively predicts dynamic saliency for video ads; ablation experiments reveal key architectural and input factors; entropy-based diagnostics successfully identify ads and scenes that fail to engage viewers when tested on 15 unseen ads.

Conclusion: Deep saliency models like ViASNet can significantly accelerate ad design and testing processes through automated analysis, providing actionable insights into viewer engagement and content optimization.

Abstract: The digital media landscape has seen a pervasive shift toward short-form video advertising on TV, social media and e-commerce platforms. The present study focuses on deep saliency prediction for short-form video advertising. Deep saliency models have been used to generate predictions of human eye fixation patterns with the purpose of enhancing user interaction with digital technology and optimizing its design. For video ads, dynamic saliency maps capture where and when viewers are looking, revealing why video ads are effective, and how their content should be optimized. We develop and test a new deep dynamic saliency prediction model called ViASNet (Video Ad Saliency Network), which has an architecture founded on the 3D U-Net, and accommodates the influence of audio and the semantic meaning of scenes. We assess the model's performance on 151 video ads, each seen by about 20 viewers wile their eye movements were tracked, and explore the critical factors influencing model performance through ablation experiments. We calculate the entropy of the predicted saliency maps frame-by-frame as a diagnostic tool to identify ads and scenes that fail to engage viewers, and illustrate its use on test data of 15 unseen ads. Our study reveals that ad design and testing can be sped up considerably through automated systems built on deep saliency models such as ViASNet.

</details>


### [26] [CapTalk: Text-Guided Stylization and Speech-Driven 3D Head Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29316)
*Xuangeng Chu,Yuan Gan,Ziteng Cui,Shuhong Liu,Jian Wang,Bing Zhou,Tatsuya Harada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a talking head generation framework that enables separate control over speaking style and emotion in audio-driven 3D facial animation, supporting dynamic emotion changes during speech.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing audio-driven facial animation methods rely on predefined identity/style latent features, limiting users' control over speaking styles, and fail to adapt facial animation styles to the emotional content of the audio.

Method: The authors revisit style-emotion entanglement, construct a large-scale dataset with textual descriptions of both style and emotion, and propose a framework that takes textual descriptions of speaking style, character emotion, and driving audio as input for real-time generation.

Result: The model achieves highly synchronized lip movements and facial expressions that match provided style and emotion descriptions, and supports dynamic emotion control during inference for changing emotions throughout speech.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully enables separate and dynamic control over style and emotion in audio-driven facial animation, addressing the limitations of fixed identity/style approaches.

Abstract: Audio-driven 3D facial animation aims to generate synchronized lip movements and vivid facial expressions from arbitrary audio clips. While existing methods can produce synchronized lip motions, they often rely on predefined identity or style latent features, which limits users' ability to freely control speaking styles. Moreover, applying a fixed style or identity to an entire audio segment typically results in facial animation styles that do not adapt to the emotional content of the audio. To address these challenges, we revisit the entanglement between style and emotion, construct a large-scale dataset with textual descriptions of both style and emotion, and propose a novel talking head generation framework that enables separate control over style and emotion. Our model takes as input both textual descriptions of speaking style and character emotion, as well as the driving audio stream, enabling real-time generation of highly synchronized lip movements and facial expressions that match the provided descriptions. Furthermore, our model supports dynamic emotion control during inference, allowing it to handle scenarios where the target emotion changes throughout the speech.

</details>


### [27] [Multi-Stage VLM Pipeline for Zero-Shot Traffic Accident Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29325)
*Fumiya Tatematsu,Fumihiko Takahashi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A three-stage zero-shot accident prediction pipeline using frozen Qwen3-VL-32B (and 235B MoE blend) achieves 1st place in the ACCIDENT challenge, beating the baseline by +0.21 on two leaderboard metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the ACCIDENT challenge's zero-shot accident prediction task (timing, impact centroid, collision type) from CCTV footage, outperforming existing VLM baselines.

Method: Three-stage pipeline on frozen Qwen3-VL-32B: (1) full-video joint prediction, (2) time refinement, (3) single-frame grounding of impact centroid; then same pipeline on a 235B MoE model, blend outputs 9:1, snap points to nearest vehicle detection.

Result: Public LB 0.55469 / Private LB 0.57080, roughly +0.21 over the strongest host baseline (Molmo-7B, 0.358), winning the challenge.

Conclusion: The approach is effective for zero-shot accident prediction; ablation studies and released code support reproducibility and future work.

Abstract: We present the 1st-place solution to the ACCIDENT challenge at the CVPR 2026 AUTOPILOT Workshop, which asks for zero-shot prediction of accident timing, impact centroid, and collision type from CCTV footage. On a frozen Qwen3-VL-32B-Instruct checkpoint we build a three-stage pipeline (full-video joint prediction, time refinement, and single-frame grounding of the impact centroid), run the same pipeline a second time on a 235B Mixture-of-Experts sibling, blend the two outputs 9:1, and finally snap each predicted point onto the nearest vehicle detection. The final system reaches Public LB 0.55469 / Private LB 0.57080, roughly +0.21 over the strongest host baseline (Molmo-7B, 0.358) and wins the challenge. We ablate each component, report the negative results that shaped the final design, and release the code at https://github.com/fuumin621/cvpr2026-accident-1st-place-solution.

</details>


### [28] [EarthShift: a benchmark for measuring robustness to real-world distribution shifts in Earth observation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29330)
*Kelsey Doerksen,Hannah Kerner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EarthShift is the first public testbed for benchmarking distributional robustness in remote sensing, showing geospatial foundation models consistently perform 15-20% worse out-of-distribution across 8 models, 11 tasks, and 5 shift types, highlighting the need for future research to improve robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Earth observation benchmarks focus on in-distribution generalization, but real-world deployment requires models to handle out-of-distribution scenarios (new time periods, geographies, scales, sensors). There is no existing testbed to systematically measure robustness across these realistic distribution shifts in remote sensing.

Method: The authors introduce EarthShift, a public testbed using paired datasets across different sources, temporal windows, geographic locations, and sensors. They evaluate 8 geospatial foundation models (GFMs) and 11 tasks covering 5 shift types, comparing performance in-distribution vs. out-of-distribution.

Result: Geospatial foundation models consistently perform 15-20% worse out-of-distribution on average, regardless of model architecture, size, pre-training or fine-tuning strategy. GFM robustness is similar to generic vision foundation models and fully-supervised models.

Conclusion: There is a significant need for future research to strive for improvements in distributional robustness, not just performance. EarthShift provides a standardized testbed to benchmark and guide such efforts toward creating robust and reliable foundation models for real-world applications.

Abstract: Current Earth observation benchmarks focus on measuring performance on diverse tasks and applications, typically measuring generalization in-distribution. But when models are deployed, they must generalize to myriad out-of-distribution scenarios, such as new time periods, geographies, scales, and sensors. We introduce EarthShift: the first public testbed for benchmarking robustness across multiple realistic distribution shifts encountered in remote sensing. EarthShift enables users to measure distributional robustness by comparing performance in- and out-of-distribution using datasets from paired datasets from different sources, temporal windows, geographic locations, and sensors. Our experiments on 8 geospatial foundation models (GFMs) and 11 tasks covering 5 shift types show that GFMs consistently perform 15-20% worse out-of-distribution on average regardless of model architecture, size, pre-training or fine-tuning strategy. We show that GFM robustness is similar to that of generic vision foundation models, and even fully-supervised models.
