However, existing diffusion-based methods still require task-specific fine-tuning and large-scale audiovisual datasets, resulting in high computational costs that hinder scalability and accessibility of diffusion-based approaches across the research community. To address this, we propose a finetuning-free paradigm that directly performs talking face generation using the pretrained weights of Stable Diffusion and IP-Adapter. This backbone leverages the visual embedding capability of IP-Adapter to mine lip-related semantics from the pretrained Stable Diffusion. To address the challenges of identity drift, synchronization errors, and temporal instability, we also design three trainable-parameterfree components: (1) the Structurist, which explicitly disentangles and reassembles lip and appearance features to mitigate identity drift and appearance distortion; (2) the Structure Controller, which adaptively refines embeddings based on quasi-monotonic motion trends for precise lip synchronization; and (3) the Noise Sensor, which introduces Gaussian prior to detect and suppress flicker and jitter artifacts and enhance temporal consistency. Experimental results show that our method outperforms existing SOTA approaches in both lip-sync accuracy (at least 0.16 gain in PCLD) and visual fidelity (at least 0.7 improvement in FID), establishing a novel fine-tuning-free diffusion framework for talking face generation.

</details>


### [112] [Beyond 3D VQAs: Injecting 3D Spatial Priors into Vision-Language Models for Enhanced Geometric Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30231)
*Chun-Hsiao Yeh,Shengyi Qian,Manchen Wang,Yi Ma,Joseph Tighe,Fanyi Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GASP injects geometric priors into VLMs via a correspondence head and dual-objective training (contrastive + depth consistency) on video scenes, boosting spatial reasoning accuracy without any 3D VQA fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs lack robust 3D spatial reasoning; existing approaches (fine-tuning on 3D VQA data or using specialized 3D encoders) are prone to overfitting or are inflexible. The authors argue that genuine spatial understanding should come from learning fundamental geometric priors rather than high-level VQA supervision alone.

Method: GASP injects geometric priors into LLM transformer layers via a small correspondence head applied as deep supervision signal across all layers. Trained with dual objective: (1) contrastive loss on ground-truth 2D point correspondences for view-invariance, (2) depth consistency supervision for resolving 3D ambiguities, using large-scale video scenes with ground-truth geometry.

Result: Standard VLMs have internal correspondence matching accuracy below 5%; GASP boosts it to over 70% (peak layer-wise) and over 85% temporal robustness (baselines remain below 5%). Downstream gains: +18.2% on All-Angles Bench and +29.0% on VSI-Bench, without any 3D VQA training data.

Conclusion: Learning from fundamental geometric priors is a promising, generalizable pathway toward VLMs with more reliable 3D spatial reasoning.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often struggle with robust 3D spatial reasoning. Prevailing methods that rely on fine-tuning with 3D visual question-answering (VQA) datasets may overfit dataset-specific biases, while integrating specialized 3D visual encoders is often inflexible and cumbersome. In this paper, we argue that genuine spatial understanding should emerge from learning fundamental geometric priors, not only from high-level VQA supervision. We propose GASP (Geometric-Aware Spatial Priors), a framework that injects these priors directly into the LLM's transformer layers. GASP employs a small correspondence head, applied as a deep supervision signal across all layers, and is trained with a dual objective leveraging ground-truth geometry from large-scale video scenes: a contrastive loss on ground-truth point correspondences enforces 2D view-invariance, while a depth consistency supervision resolves 3D geometric ambiguities. Our analysis first provides a diagnostic showing that standard VLMs' internal correspondence matching accuracy is very low (often below 5%). We then demonstrate that our training substantially improves this behavior, boosting peak layer-wise correspondence to over 70% and maintaining over 85% temporal robustness while baselines remain below 5%. These internal improvements translate to significant gains on downstream spatial benchmarks including +18.2% on All-Angles Bench and +29.0% on VSI-Bench, all without training on any 3D VQA data. Our findings indicate that learning from fundamental geometric priors is a promising and generalizable pathway towards VLMs with more reliable 3D spatial reasoning.

</details>


### [113] [BullingerDB: A Dataset for Handwritten Text Recognition and Writer Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30235)
*Marco Peer,Anna-Scius Bertrand,Patricia Scheurer,Andreas Fischer*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BullingerDB is a large-scale historical document benchmark with 20,898 pages from 796 writers (16th century), featuring multilingual text and metadata. TrOCR achieves 9.1% CER for recognition, while writer retrieval shows mAP of 78.3% with temporal challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a large-scale, well-annotated benchmark dataset for historical document analysis that includes multilingual content, writer diversity, and temporal metadata—addressing the lack of standardized evaluation resources in this domain.

Method: The dataset comprises 20,898 pages and 499,222 text lines from Heinrich Bullinger's correspondence (1540s-1560s), with 796 identified writers. The authors evaluate text recognition using TrOCR and writer retrieval using a proposed temporal nDCG metric alongside mAP.

Result: TrOCR achieves 9.1% CER for text recognition. Writer retrieval yields mAP of 78.3%, with temporal nDCG indicating some success in time-aware retrieval but challenges remain due to long-term stylistic variation across writers.

Conclusion: BullingerDB establishes a new benchmark for multilingual historical text recognition and temporally-aware writer analysis, highlighting both achievements and remaining challenges in handling stylistic variation over decades.

Abstract: We present BullingerDB, a large-scale benchmark dataset for historical document analysis based on the correspondence of Heinrich Bullinger (1504-1575). The corpus comprises 20,898 pages and 499,222 text lines written by 796 writers over six decades, featuring stylistic variation, multilingual content (mostly Latin and Early New High German) as well as meta-information such as writer identity and time. We evaluate BullingerDB on text recognition and writer retrieval. TrOCR, the best performing model, achieves a CER of 9.1%. For writer retrieval, we introduce a temporal nDCG metric to assess time-aware retrieval. While temporally coherent retrieval is achievable, mAP (78.3%) scores indicate challenges due to long-term stylistic variation. With BullingerDB, we aim to establish a new benchmark for multilingual historical text recognition and temporally-aware writer analysis.

</details>


### [114] [SAM3D-Phys: Towards Multi-Object Interactive Simulation in Real World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30239)
*Xin Dong,Weijian Deng,Lihan Zhang,Tianru Dai,Wenfeng Deng,Yansong Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAM3D-Phys is a framework that recovers complete, physics-simulatable 3D object geometry from reconstructed real-world scenes by combining multi-view reconstruction with generative 3D priors (SAM3D), plus physics-constrained spatial optimization and mask-guided appearance distillation to ensure scene consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Objects in reconstructed real-world scenes are often incomplete due to occlusions and limited observations, making them unsuitable for physics simulation. The goal is to enable physically consistent interactive simulation with multiple objects within a scene.

Method: (1) Reconstruct scene from multi-view images to get partial object geometry; (2) Use SAM3D generative priors to infer complete object geometry from partial observations; (3) Apply physics-constrained spatial optimization to align recovered objects to original positions; (4) Use mask-guided appearance distillation to refine texture fidelity.

Result: Produces clean, complete object representations suitable for physics-based simulation, enabling simultaneous and physically consistent multi-object interaction within reconstructed scenes.

Conclusion: SAM3D-Phys effectively bridges scene reconstruction and physics simulation by recovering complete, scene-consistent object geometry and appearance, unlocking interactive physics-based manipulation of objects in reconstructed real-world scenes.

Abstract: This work addresses the problem of recovering complete, simulatable object geometry from reconstructed real-world scenes, enabling physics-based interaction with objects embedded in the scene. While modern multi-view reconstruction methods can produce visually accurate environments, objects are often incomplete due to occlusions and limited observations, making them unsuitable for physics simulation. To address this limitation, we propose SAM3D-Phys, a framework that integrates scene reconstruction with generative 3D priors of SAM3D to recover physically simulatable objects. Our approach first reconstructs the scene from multi-view images to obtain scene geometry and partial observations of objects. We then leverage SAM3D to infer complete object geometry from these partial observations. To ensure that the recovered objects remain consistent with the reconstructed scene, we restore scene-consistent object states through two complementary strategies: a physics-constrained spatial optimization algorithm that iteratively aligns the recovered object to its original location, and a mask-guided appearance distillation module that refines texture fidelity based on the observed images. By recovering complete object geometry and restoring its pose and appearance within the scene, SAM3D-Phys produces clean object representations suitable for physics-based simulation, enabling simultaneous and physically consistent interactive simulation of multiple objects within a reconstructed scene. Project page: https://chnxindong.github.io/sam3d-phys/

</details>


### [115] [Reinforcement Learning with Robust Rubric Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30244)
*Ya-Qi Yu,Hao Wang,Fangyu Hong,Xiangyang Qu,Gaojie Wu,Qiaoyu Luo,Nuo Xu,Huixin Wang,Wuheng Xu,Yongxin Liao,Zihao Chen,Haonan Li,Ziming Li,Dezhi Peng,Minghui Liao,Jihao Wu,Haoyu Ren,Dandan Tu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RLR³ extends RLVR from task-level to criterion-level verification using rubrics with two execution paths (LLM-as-extractor + deterministic verifier, or LLM-as-Judge), minimal exposure strategy, and hierarchical aggregation, achieving 4.7-point improvement over base models on 15 vision-language benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many vision-language tasks are only partially verifiable, requiring multi-criteria supervision (perceptual details, reasoning steps, constraints). Rubrics offer a natural interface but depend on execution accuracy during online RL.

Method: RLR³ routes instance-specific rubrics through two paths: LLM-as-extractor + deterministic verifier for verifiable criteria, or LLM-as-Judge for non-verifiable ones. It uses minimal exposure strategy (masking ground truths from extractors, images from judges) and hierarchical aggregation to prioritize essential criteria while mitigating score saturation.

Result: Evaluated on Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B across 15 benchmarks, RLR³ consistently outperforms RLVR, yielding 4.7-point improvement over base model and exceeding the instruct-to-thinking gap. Controlled audits confirm deterministic verification and minimal exposure significantly reduce exploitable false positives.

Conclusion: RLR³ effectively extends RLVR to partially verifiable vision-language tasks by providing robust rubric-based criterion-level verification, with significant improvements and reduced reward hacking.

Abstract: While Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) is effective for deterministically checkable tasks, many vision-language tasks are partially verifiable, demanding multi-criteria supervision (e.g., perceptual details, reasoning steps, and constraints). Rubrics provide a natural interface for this fine-grained supervision, but their effectiveness depends on the execution accuracy during online RL. We propose Reinforcement Learning with Robust Rubric Rewards ($\text{RLR}^3$), extending RLVR from task-level verification to criterion-level verification. $\text{RLR}^3$ routes instance-specific rubrics through two execution paths: an LLM-as-an-extractor paired with a deterministic verifier, or an LLM-as-a-Judge for non-verifiable criteria. To ensure faithful scoring, $\text{RLR}^3$ introduce a minimal exposure strategy that masks ground truths from extractors and images from judges. Furthermore, $\text{RLR}^3$ employs hierarchical aggregation to prioritize essential criteria over additional criteria, and mitigates score saturation within rollout groups. Evaluated on Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B across 15 benchmarks, $\text{RLR}^3$ consistently outperforms RLVR, yielding a 4.7-point improvement over the base model and exceeding the official instruct-to-thinking model gap. Controlled audits confirm our deterministic verification and minimal exposure significantly reduce exploitable false positives.

</details>


### [116] [GenClaw: Code-Driven Agentic Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30248)
*Junyan Ye,Jun He,Zilong Huang,Dongzhi Jiang,Xuan Yang,Rui Chen,Weijia Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GenClaw introduces a code-driven agentic image generation paradigm that mimics human artistic creation through three stages: conceptualization, sketching (via code like SVG/HTML/Three.js), and coloring (via image generation models), enabling precise control and interpretability beyond black-box text-to-image models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current image generation agents rely on black-box models with no direct canvas manipulation, only repetitive prompt rewriting. LLMs' potential as a genuine "brush" for precise visual construction remains untapped.

Method: GenClaw uses a three-stage pipeline: (1) conceptualization via search and reasoning, (2) rendering executable visual sketches using code (SVG, HTML, Three.js), and (3) supplementing textures, materials, and photorealism via image generation models. Code acts as a controllable intermediate canvas.

Result: The paradigm transforms image generation from black-box to interpretable staged process, offering enhanced controllability and interpretability.

Conclusion: GenClaw represents a step toward highly controllable and interpretable visual generation systems by mimicking human artistic creation workflow.

Abstract: Image generation models have evolved from text-conditioned pixel synthesis toward multimodal agents endowed with visual comprehension and tool invocation capabilities. Yet, existing agents remain at the mercy of underlying black-box image models. Their workflow is trapped in a repetitive cycle of prompt rewriting for generation refinement, leaving them with no mechanism to directly manipulate the canvas. In essence, the potential of LLMs to serve as a genuine "brush" for precise visual construction remains largely untapped. In this paper, we propose GenClaw, a code-driven agentic image generation paradigm that empowers the agent to create like a human artist: first conceptualizing, then sketching, and finally coloring. Specifically, the agent first constructs the conceptual knowledge and context through search and reasoning. It then utilizes code (e.g., SVG, HTML, Three.js) to render executable visual sketches. Finally, it employs an image generation model to supplement textures, materials, and photorealism. In this workflow, code serves as a controllable intermediate canvas bridging linguistic reasoning and pixel synthesis, seamlessly integrating programmatic logic with the visual expressiveness of generative models. By transforming image generation from a black-box paradigm into a staged process akin to authentic human creation, GenClaw offers a step toward for highly controllable and interpretable visual generation systems.

</details>


### [117] [Ambient-robust Inverse Rendering using Active RGB-NIR Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30250)
*Hoon-Gyu Chung,Jinnyeong Kim,Hyunwoo Kang,Seung-Hwan Baek*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces an ambient-robust inverse rendering method that uses active RGB-NIR imaging with NIR flash illumination to reconstruct accurate geometry and reflectance from multi-view images, outperforming prior methods across varying lighting conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inverse rendering methods produce inaccurate reconstructions sensitive to ambient illumination conditions.

Method: Uses active RGB-NIR imaging with NIR flash (imperceptible, stable point-light shading) and a three-stage inverse rendering pipeline that combines multi-view RGB (ambient-lit) and NIR (flash-lit) images.

Result: The method achieves accurate geometry and reflectance estimation across multiple ambient lighting scenarios, outperforming prior approaches.

Conclusion: Leveraging complementary RGB and NIR imaging with active flash illumination enables robust inverse rendering under varying ambient conditions.

Abstract: Inverse rendering aims to reconstruct geometry and reflectance of objects from images. Despite recent progress, existing methods often produces inaccurate reconstructions that are sensitive to ambient illumination conditions. Here we introduce an ambient-robust inverse rendering method enabled by active RGB-NIR imaging. Our key insight is to leverage near-infrared (NIR) flash illumination-imperceptible to human observers-to obtain stable point-light shading that is largely invariant to ambient illumination. By using multi-view RGB images illuminated by ambient light and NIR images acquired with active NIR flash illumination, we reconstruct accurate geometry and reflectance by exploiting the complementary benefits of RGB and NIR images via a three-stage inverse rendering method. To enable dense multi-view acquisition, we develop an active imaging system equipped with a RGB-NIR camera and a NIR flash mounted on a mobile base. Using this system, we collect the first multi-view RGB-NIR inverse rendering dataset captured under multiple ambient illumination conditions. Experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms prior approaches, achieving accurate geometry and reflectance estimation across multiple ambient lighting scenarios.

</details>


### [118] [VideoFDB: Evaluating Full-Duplex Vision-Speech Capabilities in Conversational Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30256)
*Amrita Mazumdar,Seonwook Park,Rajarshi Roy,Nikhil Srihari,Shengze Wang,Yuhao Zhou,Julia Wang,Koki Nagano,Shalini De Mello*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VideoFDB is the first benchmark for evaluating full-duplex audio-visual-to-audio-visual (AV2AV) conversational agents, using 237 dyadic clips with 11 nonverbal dynamics, revealing that current systems fail at joint audiovisual grounding and cannot produce full-duplex nonverbal cues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Natural human conversation is full-duplex and audio-visual (speaking, listening, nonverbal cues), yet existing full-duplex benchmarks only evaluate speech, not the integrated audiovisual modality needed for successful human-agent interaction.

Method: The benchmark includes (i) 237 dyadic clips covering 11 nonverbal conversational dynamics from real video calls, (ii) a taxonomy separating perception and generation behaviors, and (iii) a rubric-based LM-as-judge evaluation framework for assessing conversational quality on nonverbal dynamics.

Result: Vision-speech agents show captioning collapse, visual-stream ignorance, and exploit vision only for explicit VQA but not for streaming joint audiovisual grounding; cascaded speech-to-avatar systems fundamentally cannot produce full-duplex nonverbal cues due to architectural limitations.

Conclusion: VideoFDB establishes a foundation for systematically evaluating full-duplex AV2AV interaction and aims to accelerate the development of next-generation multimodal conversational agents.

Abstract: Natural human conversation is full-duplex and audio-visual: people simultaneously speak and listen while continuously interpreting and producing nonverbal cues, such as nods, smiles, and gestures. To support successful human-agent interaction, agents must model full-duplex audiovisual conversation; however, existing full-duplex benchmarks evaluate only speech. In this work, we present VideoFDB, the first benchmark to evaluate full-duplex audio-visual-to-audio-visual (AV2AV) conversational agents. VideoFDB contributes (i) 237 dyadic clips spanning 11 nonverbal conversational dynamics from real-world video calls, (ii) a taxonomy separating perception from generation behaviors, and (iii) a rubric-based LM-as-judge evaluation framework with interpretable axes for assessing conversational quality with respect to nonverbal conversational dynamics. Across open- and closed-source vision-speech agents, we find systematic failure modes: captioning collapse and visual-stream ignorance, and we show that current systems exploit vision for explicit visual question answering but not for the streaming joint audiovisual grounding required in natural conversation. We further evaluate cascaded speech-to-avatar systems and find that their architecture fundamentally precludes the production of full-duplex nonverbal cues. As the first benchmark for full-duplex AV2AV interaction, VideoFDB establishes a foundation for systematic evaluation and, we hope, will accelerate the advancement and development of next-generation multimodal conversational agents.

</details>


### [119] [Stable-Layers: Fine-Tuning Image Layer Decomposition Models with VLM-Scored Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30257)
*Ciara Rowles,Reshinth Adithyan,Nikhil Pinnaparaju,Vikram Voleti,Mark Boss*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Stable-Layers is a reinforcement learning framework that fine-tunes a pretrained layer decomposition model using VLM feedback instead of paired supervision, employing a two-stage evaluation pipeline to address reward signal challenges with Flow-GRPO and LoRA adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To eliminate the need for paired supervision in layer decomposition by leveraging VLM feedback, while overcoming the challenge of low within-group variance in reward signals when VLMs score samples in isolation.

Method: Applies Flow-GRPO with LoRA adaptation to Qwen-Image-Layered, sampling multiple candidate decompositions per image, scoring them with a VLM using a two-stage evaluation pipeline: structured per-sample scoring across five edit-centric criteria plus a grid-based calibration step for side-by-side re-scoring.

Result: Achieves stronger layer separation, fewer blank or artifact-heavy layers, and lower per-layer reconstruction error on the Crello dataset compared to the base model.

Conclusion: The proposed two-stage VLM evaluation pipeline effectively addresses reward signal compression issues in GRPO-based fine-tuning, enabling improved layer decomposition without paired supervision.

Abstract: We present Stable-Layers, a reinforcement learning framework that eliminates the need for paired supervision by fine-tuning a pretrained layer decomposition model using only feedback from a vision-language model (VLM). Starting from Qwen-Image-Layered, we apply Flow-GRPO with LoRA adaptation, sampling multiple candidate decompositions per image, scoring them with a VLM, and optimising the policy from group-relative advantages. The key challenge lies in designing a reliable reward signal: VLMs scoring samples in isolation tend to compress their judgements into a narrow band, leaving GRPO with little within-group variance to learn from. We address this with a two-stage evaluation pipeline that pairs structured per-sample scoring across five edit-centric criteria with a grid-based calibration step in which the VLM re-scores all candidates side-by-side. Stable-Layers produces decompositions with stronger layer separation, fewer blank or artifact-heavy layers, and lower per-layer reconstruction error on the Crello dataset compared to the base model.

</details>


### [120] [minWM: A Full-Stack Open-Source Framework for Real-Time Interactive Video World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30263)
*Min Zhao,Hongzhou Zhu,Bokai Yan,Zihan Zhou,Yimin Chen,Wenqiang Sun,Kaiwen Zheng,Guande He,Xiao Yang,Chongxuan Li,Fan Bao,Jun Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: minWM is a full-stack open-source framework that converts bidirectional video diffusion models into real-time, camera-controllable autoregressive world models for interactive video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Turning video diffusion foundation models into real-time interactive video world models is challenging due to requirements of controllability, causality, and low-latency rollout; existing models lack an integrated pipeline to achieve this.

Method: minWM provides an end-to-end pipeline: (1) fine-tune bidirectional video diffusion model with camera control, (2) apply Causal Forcing/Causal Forcing++ pipeline (AR diffusion training, causal ODE/consistency distillation, asymmetric DMD) to distill into few-step autoregressive generator for low-latency rollout. Supports both cross-attention and MMDiT architectures.

Result: Instantiated on Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B and HY1.5-TI2V-8B backbones; supports adapting existing world models like HY-WorldPlay; provides practical ablations on camera trajectory quality, controllability training steps, and batch-size requirements.

Conclusion: minWM serves as a reproducible, extensible, full-stack recipe for building and adapting real-time interactive video world models, with open-source release of scripts, checkpoints, documentation, and inference code.

Abstract: Recent video diffusion foundation models have achieved remarkable progress in high-quality video generation, yet turning them into real-time interactive video world models remains challenging. Interactive world models require controllable, causal, and low-latency rollout, which in practice demands a full pipeline spanning data construction, controllable fine-tuning, autoregressive training, few-step distillation, and streaming inference. In this work, we present minWM, a full-stack open-source framework for building real-time interactive video world models. minWM provides an end-to-end pipeline that converts existing bidirectional T2V/TI2V video foundation models into camera-controllable few-step autoregressive world models. Specifically, minWM first fine-tunes a bidirectional video diffusion model with camera control, and then applies the Causal Forcing / Causal Forcing++ pipeline, including AR diffusion training, causal ODE or causal consistency distillation, and asymmetric DMD, to distill it into a few-step autoregressive generator for low-latency rollout. The framework is modular and architecture-extensible: we instantiate it on representative open backbones, including Wan2.1-T2V-1.3B and HY1.5-TI2V-8B, covering both cross-attention-based condition injection and MMDiT-style architectures. minWM also supports adapting existing video world models, such as HY-WorldPlay, to new data distributions, training recipes, and latency targets. Beyond releasing runnable scripts, checkpoints, documentation, and inference code, we provide practical ablations on camera trajectory quality, controllability training steps, and minimal batch-size requirements. We hope minWM serves as a reproducible and extensible recipe for building and adapting real-time interactive video world models.
  Project Page: [https://github.com/shengshu-ai/minWM](https://github.com/shengshu-ai/minWM)

</details>


### [121] [LoMo: Local Modality Substitution for Deeper Vision-Language Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30265)
*Feng Han,Zhixiong Zhang,Zheming Liang,Yibin Wang,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLMs suffer from "carrier sensitivity" - significant performance drops when text is replaced by images with same semantics. The authors propose LoMo, a data curation method that creates interleaved text-image-text sequences to train cross-modal representational invariance, achieving consistent gains across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs exhibit dramatic performance degradation when textual questions are replaced with semantically equivalent image counterparts, attributed to biased training data where text and images have asymmetric roles (text as queries, images as references).

Method: LoMo (Local Modality Substitution) dynamically selects text spans and recasts them as rendered images within interleaved "text, visual, text" sequences, providing supervision for cross-modal representational invariance without changing model architecture.

Result: LoMo improves over standard SFT by 2.67 points on LLaVA-OneVision-1.5-8B and 2.82 points on Qwen3.5-9B across 13 diverse multimodal benchmarks.

Conclusion: LoMo is a lightweight, architecture-agnostic data curation paradigm that effectively addresses the carrier sensitivity issue in VLMs by enforcing cross-modal representational alignment, leading to more robust multimodal reasoning.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved substantial progress across a wide range of understanding and reasoning tasks, driven by large-scale image-text training aimed at multimodal fusion. Ideally, replacing a textual question with its rendered-image counterpart should leave model performance essentially unaffected. In practice, however, such modality substitution induces dramatic performance degradation. We attribute this "carrier sensitivity" issue to an inherent bias in current training corpora. Across prevalent datasets such as image captioning, VQA, OCR, and web-sourced interleaved data, text and images are typically organized into distinct and asymmetric roles, with text serving as linguistic queries and images as visual references. Such data bias leads VLMs to exhibit distinct preferences for information acquisition across different modalities. Consequently, VLMs fail to align representations of semantically equivalent content across textual and visual carriers, making model reasoning fragile under modality substitution. To address this, we propose Local Modality Substitution (LoMo), a lightweight, architecture-agnostic data curation paradigm designed to provide supervision for cross-modal representational invariance between semantically equivalent text and image carriers. LoMo achieves this by reformulating single-modality prompts into seamlessly interleaved multimodal sequences. It dynamically selects target text spans and recasts them as rendered images, thereby preserving the same semantics across "text, visual, text" carriers. Extensive experiments across 13 diverse multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that LoMo significantly improves overall multimodal reasoning and yields deeper cross-modal fusion. Specifically, it delivers consistent gains across foundational models, improving over standard SFT by 2.67 points on LLaVA-OneVision-1.5-8B and 2.82 points on Qwen3.5-9B.

</details>


### [122] [PhyGenHOI: Physically-Aware 4D Generation of Dynamic Human-Object Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30268)
*Omer Benishu,Gal Fiebelman,Sagie Benaim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PhyGenHOI generates physically accurate 4D Human-Object Interactions by coupling generative human motion (via Motion Diffusion Model) with explicit physical object simulation (via Material Point Method), using 3D Gaussian Splats as a unified representation with three supervision mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To generate physically accurate and visually faithful 4D Human-Object Interactions from static 3D human/object inputs and text descriptions, addressing the gap between generative motion models and realistic physics simulation.

Method: PhyGenHOI uses a dual-agent framework: human as semantic agent (Motion Diffusion Model) + object as physical agent (Material Point Method), unified via 3D Gaussian representation. Three supervision mechanisms: (1) Windowed Attraction Loss for temporal synchronization, (2) Contact-Driven Re-simulation for momentum transfer, (3) Masked Video-SDS for contact fidelity enhancement.

Result: Outperforms baselines in generating physically consistent 4D HOI across diverse actions, humans, and objects.

Conclusion: The hybrid approach of coupling generative motion with explicit physics simulation effectively produces realistic 4D HOI while maintaining physical consistency.

Abstract: We address the task of generating physically accurate and visually faithful 4D Human-Object Interaction (HOI). Given a static 3D human and target object represented as 3D Gaussian Splats (3DGS), our goal is to synthesize dynamic scenes where the human actively engages with the object through actions, such as punching or kicking, in accordance with a given input text. To this end, we introduce PhyGenHOI, a novel framework that couples generative human motion with an explicit physical object simulation. We model the human as a semantic agent driven by a Motion Diffusion Model (MDM) and the object as a physical agent simulated via the Material Point Method (MPM), utilizing 3D Gaussians as a unified, differentiable representation. We supervise their interaction through three coupled mechanisms: (1) A Windowed Attraction Loss that temporally synchronizes generative motion to intercept the object; (2) A Contact-Driven Re-simulation step that triggers physically consistent momentum transfer upon impact; and (3) A Masked Video-SDS objective that injects video-based priors to enhance contact fidelity. Experiments show PhyGenHOI generates physically consistent 4D HOI across diverse actions, humans, and objects, outperforming baselines. Project page and videos: https://omerbenishu.github.io/PhyGenHOI/

</details>


### [123] [Grounded 3D-Aware Spatial Vision-Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30307)
*An-Chieh Cheng,Yang Fu,Yatai Ji,Ligeng Zhu,Guanqi Zhan,Zhuoyang Zhang,Zhaojing Yang,Song Han,Yao Lu,Pavlo Molchanov,Vidya Nariyambut Murali,Jan Kautz,Xiaolong Wang,Hongxu Yin,Sifei Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GR3D is a spatial VLM that integrates explicit 2D, implicit 2D, and monocular 3D grounding in one framework, using implicit grounding to insert region tokens during generation and monocular 3D grounding with intrinsic-aware normalization for 3D box prediction, achieving consistent gains on spatial benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance spatial understanding in VLMs by introducing grounding as an inductive bias, enabling models to decompose complex spatial problems into grounded 2D perception followed by 3D inference.

Method: GR3D combines (1) implicit grounding that inserts region tokens into the text stream during generation for on-the-fly visual reference, and (2) region-prompted monocular 3D grounding that predicts 3D bounding boxes from grounded region queries using intrinsic-aware normalization and dense geometric supervision.

Result: Consistent improvements across both grounded and non-grounded spatial benchmarks, demonstrating that grounding serves as an effective inductive bias for spatial understanding.

Conclusion: Grounding capabilities collectively enhance general spatial understanding beyond the grounding task itself, validating the approach as a strong inductive bias for spatial VLMs.

Abstract: We present GR3D, a spatial vision language model equipped with three complementary grounding capabilities--explicit 2D grounding, implicit 2D grounding, and monocular 3D grounding--within a single framework. GR3D introduces an implicit grounding mechanism that identifies entity mentions during generation and inserts the corresponding region tokens into the text stream, allowing the model to reference visual evidence on the fly when producing spatial chain-of-thought responses. In parallel, a region-prompted monocular 3D grounding design predicts 3D bounding boxes in the camera view from grounded region queries, supported by intrinsic-aware normalization and dense geometric supervision. Together, these grounding capabilities enable GR3D to decompose complex spatial understanding problems into grounded 2D perception followed by 3D inference. GR3D achieves consistent improvements across grounded and non-grounded spatial benchmarks, demonstrating grounding as an effective inductive bias for strengthening spatial understanding in VLMs. These grounding capabilities collectively enhance general spatial understanding beyond the grounding task itself.

</details>


### [124] [City-Mesh3R: Simulation-Ready City-Scale 3D Mesh Reconstruction from Multi-View Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30310)
*Sayan Paul,Sourav Ghosh,Siddharth Katageri,Soumyadip Maity,Sanjana Sinha,Brojeshwar Bhowmick*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: City-Mesh3R is a scalable, end-to-end framework for reconstructing watertight 3D surface meshes from large unordered city-scale images, using a divide-and-conquer strategy with topological image clustering, geometry-aware camera selection, and curvature-aware remeshing to produce high-fidelity, regular meshes suitable for simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing city-scale 3D reconstruction methods (NeRF, Gaussian Splatting) fail to produce watertight, simulation-ready meshes due to incomplete geometry and noisy surfaces, while small-scale methods are computationally infeasible for large urban scenes.

Method: The method follows a divide-and-conquer approach: (1) topological image clustering for sparse city map reconstruction, (2) cluster-wise independent sparse SfM and map merging without exhaustive feature matching, (3) spatial partitioning with geometry-aware camera selection, (4) dense surface reconstruction with curvature-aware adaptive vertex density remeshing, and (5) stitching partition meshes into a global watertight mesh.

Result: The framework produces high-fidelity watertight 3D meshes with regular geometry and fine surface details, and scales to arbitrarily large scenes in a distributed setting.

Conclusion: City-Mesh3R effectively addresses city-scale 3D mesh reconstruction challenges, delivering simulation-ready, watertight surfaces suitable for arbitrarily large urban scenes.

Abstract: City-scale 3D surface reconstruction from multiview images for downstream 3D simulation, poses highly challenging problems due to the scale and complexity of urban scenes. Existing city-scale 3D reconstruction methods based on NeRF, Gaussian Splatting etc. often fail to recover 3D meshes ready for simulation due to incomplete/missing geometry and irregular, noisy surfaces. Scaling existing small-scale 3D reconstruction methods to arbitrarily large urban scenes is highly infeasible due to their computational complexity. We present City-Mesh3R, a scalable framework for reconstructing watertight surface meshes directly from large unordered image collections. Unlike recent methods which use global sparse SfM point-cloud initialization followed by a distributed 3D dense reconstruction of large-scale scenes, our method follows an end-to-end images-to-mesh 3D reconstruction approach using a divide-and-conquer strategy. The sparse city map is reconstructed via topological image clustering, cluster-wise independent sparse SfM and map merging, without need for exhaustive image feature matching. Then this map is partitioned spatially to perform geometry-aware camera selection, followed by dense surface reconstruction and surface refinement using curvature-aware adaptive vertex density remeshing. These partition meshes are then stitched together to produce the global mesh of the city. The proposed end-to-end framework is evaluated on city-scale reconstruction datasets. As demonstrated by our qualitative and quantitative results, our proposed method yields high-fidelity watertight 3D meshes with regular geometry, capturing fine surface details, and is suitable for scaling to arbitrarily large scenes owing to the end-to-end processing in a distributed setting.

</details>


### [125] [Archon: A Unified Multimodal Model for Holistic Digital Human Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30311)
*Chong Bao,Shichen Liu,Lijun Yu,David Futschik,Stylianos Moschoglou,Shefali Srivastava,Ziqian Bai,Feitong Tan,Guofeng Zhang,Zhaopeng Cui,Sean Fanello,Yinda Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Archon is a unified multimodal model for holistic digital human generation that integrates seven modalities (text, audio, motion, visual, etc.) using modality-specific tokenizers and autoregressive pretraining, featuring a semantic video reparameterization for 4x token reduction and a "Thinking in Modality" reasoning approach to enhance fidelity and controllability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Creating a unified model that handles holistic modalities (text, audio, motion, visual content) for digital human generation remains an open challenge, as existing approaches lack a single framework capable of modeling joint distributions across all these modalities.

Method: (1) Modality-specific tokenizers for seven modalities; (2) A native autoregressive unified multimodal model pretrained on synchronized modalities and 72 tasks; (3) Memory-efficient semantic video reparameterization achieving 4x token reduction; (4) Semantic-driven video diffusion decoder; (5) "Thinking in Modality" — decomposing ambiguous cross-modal tasks into stepwise reasoning in an alternative chain of modality.

Result: Archon achieves superior or comparable performance across diverse digital human generation tasks, validated by extensive experiments.

Conclusion: A fully pretrained, human-centric unified multimodal framework is effective for holistic avatar generation, with proposed techniques (token reduction, semantic video decoder, chain-of-modality reasoning) significantly improving fidelity and controllability.

Abstract: Digital humans are fundamental to immersive interaction, yet creating a unified model for holistic modalities, including text, audio, motion, and visual content, remains an open challenge. In this paper, we present Archon, a fully pretrained, human-centric unified multimodal model for holistic avatar generation. Archon unifies seven modalities with modality-specific tokenizers, and a native autoregressive unified multimodal model pretrained on synchronized modalities and 72 diverse tasks to model holistic joint distributions. To address the token explosion challenge in high-fidelity talking videos, we introduce a memory-efficient semantic video reparameterization, achieving 4x token reduction while preserving fine-grained dynamics, coupled with a semantic-driven video diffusion decoder. We further propose a "Thinking in Modality" that decomposes ambiguous cross-modal tasks into stepwise thinking in an alternative chain of modality, progressively enhancing fidelity and controllability. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Archon achieves superior or comparable performance across diverse digital human generation tasks, validating the effectiveness of our unified framework. Project page: https://zju3dv.github.io/archon/.

</details>


### [126] [VPG: Visual Prefix Guidance for Autoregressive Image and Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30317)
*Xinyao Liao,Qiyuan He,Yicong Li,Jiayin Zhu,Xiaoye Qu,Wei Wei,Angela Yao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Visual Prefix Guidance (VPG) is a training-free inference-time method that improves autoregressive image and video generation by contrasting outputs under generated vs. corrupted prefixes to strengthen posterior support, reducing FID by 0.36 on VAR and improving benchmarks across multiple tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive generators suffer from exposure bias and prefix drift because they are trained with teacher-forced histories but must sample from their own generated prefixes at inference time, and existing remedies focus on external semantic conditions rather than the posterior support of the generated prefix itself.

Method: VPG contrasts the model's output under the generated prefix with its output under a corrupted prefix, then extrapolates logits toward candidates that strengthen the posterior support of the generated prefix, all without additional training.

Result: Across class-conditional image generation (VAR), text-to-image (Infinity), and text-to-video (InfinityStar), VPG improves generation quality, reducing FID on VAR by 0.36 on average and improving benchmark performance on both image and video generation.

Conclusion: VPG provides an effective training-free solution to exposure bias and prefix drift in autoregressive visual generation, consistently improving quality across diverse tasks and models without retraining.

Abstract: Autoregressive image and video generators are trained with teacher-forced histories but must sample from their own generated prefixes at inference time, making them vulnerable to exposure bias and prefix drift. Existing remedies either modify training or apply sampling-time guidance aimed primarily at external semantic conditions, such as class labels or text prompts, rather than testing whether a next-step prediction provides strong posterior support for the generated prefix itself. We propose Visual Prefix Guidance (VPG), a training-free inference-time guidance method for autoregressive image and video generation. VPG improves next-step prediction by contrasting the model's output under the generated prefix with its output under a corrupted prefix, then extrapolating logits toward candidates that strengthen the posterior support of the generated prefix. Across class-conditional image generation with VAR, text-to-image generation with Infinity, and text-to-video generation with InfinityStar, VPG improves generation quality without retraining the base model, reducing FID on VAR by 0.36 on average and improving benchmark performance on both image and video generation.

</details>


### [127] [MonoPhysics: Estimating Geometry, Appearance, and Physical Parameters from Monocular Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30320)
*Daniel Rho,Jun Myeong Choi,Matthew Thornton,Biswadip Dey,Roni Sengupta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MonoPhysics jointly optimizes geometry, appearance, and physical parameters of deformable objects from monocular video using differentiable MPM simulation and 3D Gaussian Splatting, achieving multi-view-level performance with a single camera.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inverse physics methods rely on multi-view videos for geometric constraints to resolve scale and 3D structure. In monocular settings, the absence of such constraints leads to scale ambiguity, inaccurate geometry, and weak coupling between appearance and physics.

Method: Proposes three visual-physical bridges: global scale alignment, physics-aware geometry refinement, and a differentiable position map, enabling joint optimization of geometry, appearance, and physical parameters from a single camera view using differentiable MPM simulation and 3D Gaussian Splatting.

Result: Outperforms existing monocular baselines and achieves performance comparable to multi-view methods on Vid2Sim and a new elastic/plastic object dataset.

Conclusion: Monocular inverse physics estimation of deformable objects is feasible with proper visual-physical bridging mechanisms, closing the gap to multi-view approaches.

Abstract: Existing inverse physics methods recover physical parameters from multi-view videos, where geometric constraints across views resolve scale and 3D structure. In monocular settings, however, such constraints are absent, leading to severe scale ambiguity, inaccurate geometry, and weak coupling between appearance optimization and physical simulation. We propose MonoPhysics, a framework for monocular inverse physics estimation of deformable objects using differentiable MPM simulation and 3D Gaussian Splatting, which jointly optimizes geometry, appearance, and physical parameters from a single camera view. We address these challenges through three visual-physical bridges: global scale alignment, physics-aware geometry refinement, and a differentiable position map, which together enable accurate optimization from monocular observations alone. We evaluate on Vid2Sim and our new dataset of elastic and plastic objects, showing that MonoPhysics outperforms existing baselines in monocular settings and achieves performance comparable to multi-view baselines using only a single camera. Our project page is available at https://daniel03c1.github.io/MonoPhysics/

</details>


### [128] [Veda: Scalable Video Diffusion via Distilled Sparse Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30325)
*Shihao Han,Hao Yang,Xinting Hu,Xiaofeng Mei,Yi Jiang,Xiaojuan Qi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Veda is a distilled sparse attention framework that accelerates diffusion transformers for high-resolution long video generation by aligning sparse masks with full attention geometry, achieving 5.1× end-to-end speedup with no quality degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling diffusion transformers to high-resolution long videos is bottlenecked by the quadratic cost of self-attention. Existing sparse attention methods degrade under high sparsity, limiting their practical acceleration.

Method: Veda formulates tile selection as a reconstruction problem from full attention, using statistics-aware tile scoring and head-aware tiling to enable aggressive sparsity. A hardware-efficient tile-skipping kernel converts theoretical sparsity into actual speedup.

Result: On Waver-T2V-12B (720P 10-second videos), Veda achieves 5.1× end-to-end speedup and 10.5× self-attention speedup, reducing attention overhead from 92% to 50%. Gains increase with sequence length.

Conclusion: Generation quality depends not on sparsity ratio alone but on how well the sparse mask aligns with full attention's tile-wise geometry. Veda scales favorably with spatiotemporal resolution, enabling practical acceleration for large video diffusion models.

Abstract: Scaling Diffusion Transformers to generate high-resolution, long videos is constrained by the quadratic cost of self-attention, and existing sparse attention methods degrade under high sparsity. We show empirically that generation quality is determined not by the sparsity ratio itself, but by how well the sparse mask aligns with the tile-wise geometry of full attention. Based on this insight, we propose Veda, a distilled sparse attention framework that formulates tile selection as an explicit reconstruction problem from full attention. Veda integrates statistics-aware tile scoring with head-aware tiling to reduce estimation error and structural mismatch, enabling aggressive sparsity. A hardware-efficient tile-skipping kernel converts theoretical sparsity into practical wall-clock speedups. Experiments on large video diffusion models, including Waver and Wan2.1, demonstrate substantial acceleration with no noticeable degradation in generation quality. To generate 720P 10-second videos on Waver-T2V-12B, Veda achieves a 5.1$\times$ end-to-end speedup and a 10.5$\times$ self-attention speedup, reducing attention overhead from 92% to 50%. Notably, the gains increase with sequence length, indicating that Veda scales favorably with spatiotemporal resolution across models.

</details>


### [129] [Supercharging Thermal Gaussian Splatting with Depth Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30328)
*Manoj Biswanath,Chenxin Cai,Hannah Schieber,Daniel Roth,Benjamin Busam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes Thermal-to-Depth Gaussian Splatting (TDg), a single-modality method using only thermal infrared images and depth estimation for 3D scene representation, eliminating the need for multimodal data fusion. It outperforms the MSMG baseline in rendering quality metrics (LPIPS, SSIM, PSNR) and reduces training time by 55% (12 min 47 sec).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the challenges of multimodal data fusion (slower processing, additional complexity) in 3D scene representation by using a single modality—thermal infrared domain—thus removing reliance on visible light and achieving faster, more efficient performance.

Method: Thermal-to-Depth Gaussian Splatting (TDg) uses only thermal images and depth estimation to derive radiance fields, avoiding multimodal data fusion. It is based on a single-modality architecture leveraging thermal infrared data.

Result: TDg outperforms the MSMG baseline on RGBT-Scenes and ThermalMix datasets: LPIPS improves by 1.12%, SSIM by 0.034%, PSNR by 0.01%. Training time is reduced by 12 min 47 sec (55% improvement).

Conclusion: The method successfully derives thermal radiance fields with improved efficiency and competitive quality, with applications in surveillance, search/rescue, and industrial inspections.

Abstract: Efficient and robust 3D scene representation is crucial in autonomous driving, robotics, and related fields. While RGB images provide valuable content for 3D reconstruction, other modalities like thermal or depth can enable additional information on the environment. Lately, novel view synthesis methods like 3D Gaussian Splatting have started using multiple modalities to further boost their performance. But fusing or combining multimodal data can make the process slower and can bring in additional challenges. Therefore, our project aims to use single modality based on thermal infrared domain, by removing the reliance on visible light as much as possible. This single modality can be expected to be faster as it does not rely on multimodal data. We propose a method, Thermal-to-Depth Gaussian Splatting (TDg), that uses only thermal images and depth estimation in its architecture to derive the radiance fields. Our TDg method outperforms the MSMG (Multiple Single-Modal Gaussians) baseline in most cases on our test datasets, RGBT-Scenes and ThermalMix. On average, the rendering quality metrics such as learned perceptual image patch similarity (LPIPS), structural similarity index measure (SSIM), and peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) of TDg are 1.12%, 0.034%, and 0.01% better than the baseline MSMG values. It also reduces the training time significantly, by 12 mins 47 secs (55% improvement). Overall, our method is successful in deriving these thermal radiance fields, which can ultimately have several applications, such as identifying heat sources critical in surveillance, search or rescue operations, and industrial inspections where temperature is widely used to monitor machines.

</details>


### [130] [Colored Noise Diffusion Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30332)
*Hadar Davidson,Noam Issachar,Sagie Benaim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper identifies that diffusion models exhibit spectral bias (low frequencies first, high frequencies later) and shows that standard SDE solvers waste energy by injecting uniform white noise throughout. The authors propose Colored Noise Sampling (CNS), a training-free sampler that dynamically allocates noise energy to frequency bands still needing refinement, achieving significant FID improvements across multiple architectures (SiT, JiT, FLUX) on ImageNet.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SDE solvers for diffusion models inject uniform white noise throughout the entire generative process, failing to account for the model's spectral bias where low-frequency structures are resolved early and high-frequency details come later. This naive approach misuses the finite energy budget.

Method: The authors establish a mathematical framework viewing SDE inference as targeted, frequency-decoupled energy transfer. They propose Colored Noise Sampling (CNS), a training-free stochastic solver that uses a dynamic, timestep- and frequency-dependent noise schedule to allocate injected energy toward structurally unresolved frequency bands, exploiting the model's inherent spectral bias.

Result: CNS achieves substantial unguided FID improvements on ImageNet-256: SiT-XL/2 (8.26→6.27), JiT-B/16 (32.39→26.69), JiT-H/16 (11.88→8.31). Consistent relative improvements are also shown with Classifier-Free Guidance across SiT, JiT, and FLUX architectures.

Conclusion: CNS significantly outperforms standard ODE and SDE baselines as a plug-and-play, inference-time sampler substitution across diverse architectures, demonstrating the value of frequency-aware noise scheduling in diffusion model sampling.

Abstract: Diffusion models achieve state-of-the-art image synthesis, with their generative trajectories fundamentally exhibiting a spectral bias, resolving low-frequency global structures early and high-frequency fine details later. Conventional stochastic differential equation (SDE) solvers fail to account for this dynamic, naively injecting uniform white noise throughout the entire process and misusing the finite energy budget. In this work, we establish a mathematical framework that reconsiders SDE inference as a targeted, frequency-decoupled energy transfer. Leveraging this framework, we introduce Colored Noise Sampling (CNS), a novel, training-free stochastic solver. Rather than injecting uniform white noise, CNS utilizes a dynamic, timestep- and frequency-dependent schedule that more efficiently allocates injected energy toward structurally unresolved frequency bands. By actively exploiting the model's inherent spectral bias, CNS systematically steers the generated distribution toward the true data manifold. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CNS significantly outperforms standard ODE and SDE baselines as a strictly plug-and-play, inference-time sampler substitution across diverse architectures (SiT, JiT, FLUX). Compared to standard sampling on ImageNet-256, CNS achieves substantial unguided FID reductions, improving from 8.26 to 6.27 on SiT-XL/2, 32.39 to 26.69 on JiT-B/16, and 11.88 to 8.31 on JiT-H/16, while yielding consistent relative FID improvements with Classifier-Free Guidance. Project page is available at https://hadardavidson.github.io/CNS/.

</details>


### [131] [REST3D: Reconstructing Physically Stable 3D Scenes from a Single Image](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30338)
*Xiaoxuan Ma,Jiashun Wang,Nicolas Ugrinovic,Yehonathan Litman,Kris Kitani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: REST3D reconstructs physically stable 3D scenes from single RGB images by combining agentic physical scene understanding (using a scene-tree representation to capture object states and gravity-support relationships) with physics-constrained refinement, significantly reducing floating/penetration artifacts and improving simulation stability for VR applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing single-image 3D reconstruction methods produce geometrically plausible but physically inconsistent scenes (object floating, penetration), leading to unstable physics simulations. Image-conditioned scene generation methods improve physical plausibility but rely on strong priors that yield inaccurate object arrangements that don't match the input image.

Method: (1) Agentic physical scene understanding: constructs a scene-tree representation capturing object physical states and inter-object relationships from a gravity-support perspective. (2) Scene initialization using image-to-3D models. (3) Scene-tree-guided alignment + physics-constrained optimization to resolve physical violations while preserving visual consistency.

Result: Significantly reduces physical errors and improves simulation stability on both synthetic and real-world datasets while maintaining strong reconstruction quality. Demonstrated in VR-based human-object interaction.

Conclusion: REST3D effectively bridges the gap between geometric plausibility and physical stability in single-image 3D reconstruction, enabling the creation of simulation-ready digital assets for immersive applications like VR.

Abstract: Reconstructing physically stable 3D scenes from a single RGB image enables casual images to be converted into simulation-ready digital assets for applications such as immersive interaction and content creation. However, existing single-image reconstruction methods fall short in capturing the physical structure of a scene. As a result, they often produce geometrically plausible but physically inconsistent results, including object floating and penetration, which lead to unstable behavior in physics simulations. Image-conditioned scene generation methods improve physical plausibility but often rely on strong scene priors, yielding plausible yet inaccurate object arrangements that fail to match the input image. We propose REST3D, a single-image reconstruction framework that can reconstruct physically stable 3D scenes by integrating physical scene understanding with physics-constrained refinement. We first introduce an agentic physical scene understanding technique that constructs a scene-tree representation capturing object physical states and inter-object relationships from a gravity-support perspective, providing a structural prior for reconstruction. Leveraging this structure, we initialize the scene using image-to-3D models, followed by scene-tree-guided alignment and physics-constrained optimization to resolve physical violations while preserving visual consistency with the input image. Experiments show that our method significantly reduces physical errors and improves simulation stability on both synthetic and real-world datasets while maintaining strong reconstruction quality. We further demonstrate the reconstructed scenes in VR-based human-object interaction, showing their potential for immersive applications.

</details>


### [132] [Benchmarking Single-Factor Physical Video-to-Audio Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30339)
*Tingle Li,Siddharth Gururani,Kevin J. Shih,Gantavya Bhatt,Sang-gil Lee,Zhifeng Kong,Arushi Goel,Gopala Anumanchipalli,Ming-Yu Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlatSounds is a benchmark that evaluates physical reasoning in video-to-audio models, revealing a trade-off where text captions improve physical accuracy but degrade temporal alignment, highlighting the need for models to learn physics directly from pixels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing V2A models focus on perceptual realism but overlook physical correctness under controlled interventions; there is no benchmark to audit whether generated audio reflects underlying physical processes.

Method: The benchmark uses 1) controlled counterfactual pairs varying a single physical factor, and 2) single-video pattern tests for internal consistency, then evaluates state-of-the-art V2A models on these physics-based tests.

Result: Models rely more on text captions than visual streams for physics/semantics; captions improve physical accuracy but paradoxically degrade temporal alignment; physics-based metrics correlate strongly with human preferences.

Conclusion: The field needs to move beyond audio quality toward learning physical processes directly from pixels rather than relying on text captions.

Abstract: Generative video-to-audio (V2A) models produce highly plausible soundtracks, but it remains unclear whether they capture the underlying physical processes. Existing evaluations emphasize perceptual realism and overlook physical correctness under controlled interventions. In this paper, we introduce FlatSounds, a benchmark that audits the physical reasoning of V2A models through: 1) controlled counterfactual pairs in which a single physical factor is varied, and 2) single-video pattern tests that probe internal consistency and directional trends. These settings test whether the generated audio correctly reflects specific physical properties and timings. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art models reveals a consistent trade-off: models rely more on text captions than the visual stream to infer physics and semantics. Captions generally improve physical and semantic accuracy, but paradoxically degrade temporal alignment. Our results highlight the need to move beyond audio quality toward learning physical processes directly from pixels. Finally, we find that our physics-based metrics correlate strongly with human preference tests on our own data. Project webpage: https://research.nvidia.com/labs/cosmos-lab/flatsounds/

</details>


### [133] [GPIC: A Giant Permissive Image Corpus for Visual Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30341)
*Keshigeyan Chandrasegaran,Kyle Sargent,Suchir Agarwal,Michael Jang,Michael Poli,Juan Carlos Niebles,Justin Johnson,Jiajun Wu,Li Fei-Fei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GPIC is a massive 28-trillion-pixel image dataset (100M training, 200K validation, 1M test) with permissive licenses, safety filtering, deduplication, and captions from a vision-language model, hosted on Hugging Face, with a benchmarking protocol and reference baseline for pixel-space flow matching.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide large-scale, accessible, stable, and permissively licensed image datasets for scalable visual generative modeling research.

Method: Curated ~28 trillion pixels from diverse internet images, captioned by a state-of-the-art vision-language model; applied safety filtering and deduplication; hosted on Hugging Face; established a benchmarking protocol and a pixel-space flow matching baseline.

Result: GPIC contains 100M training, 200K validation, and 1M test examples with permissive licenses; a reference baseline for pixel-space flow matching is provided.

Conclusion: GPIC enables scalable generative modeling research with open access, permissive licensing, and standardized benchmarking.

Abstract: Studying scalable methods for visual generative modeling requires large, accessible, and stable datasets. We introduce GPIC, a Giant Permissive Image Corpus of approximately 28 trillion pixels. GPIC comprises diverse internet images captioned by a state-of-the-art vision-language model, including 100M training, 200K validation, and 1M test examples. Moreover, all GPIC images are permissively licensed for both research and commercial use. GPIC is safety-filtered, deduplicated, and centrally hosted on Hugging Face. We provide a benchmarking protocol for generative modeling on GPIC. Finally, we provide a reference baseline for pixel-space flow matching on GPIC. Our dataset, benchmark, and models are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/stanford-vision-lab/gpic. Evaluation toolkit and code are available at https://gpic.stanford.edu

</details>


### [134] [YoCausal: How Far is Video Generation from World Model? A Causality Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30346)
*You-Zhe Xie,Yu-Hsuan Li,Jie-Ying Lee,Kaipeng Zhang,Yu-Lun Liu,Zhixiang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: YoCausal is a two-level benchmark using reversed real-world videos to evaluate whether video diffusion models understand causality or just statistical temporal patterns, revealing a significant gap between VDMs and human causal cognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks for evaluating causal understanding in VDMs rely on synthetic data, which suffers from sim-to-real gaps and fails to assess real-world generalization of causal reasoning.

Method: YoCausal uses the Violation of Expectation paradigm by temporally reversing real-world videos as natural counterfactuals. Level 1: Reverse Surprise Index quantifies arrow-of-time perception via denoising loss. Level 2: Causality Cognition Index uses a VLM to stratify datasets into causal/non-causal subsets to disentangle genuine causal reasoning from temporal bias.

Result: Evaluation of 13 state-of-the-art VDMs shows that perceiving the arrow of time does not imply understanding causality, and a significant gap remains compared to human-level causal cognition.

Conclusion: Current video diffusion models still lack genuine causal understanding despite their ability to model temporal statistics, highlighting the need for better causal reasoning benchmarks and architectures.

Abstract: As video diffusion models (VDMs) advance toward world models, a key question arises: do they truly understand causality, or merely overfit to statistical temporal patterns? Existing benchmarks mostly rely on synthetic data, limiting real-world generalization due to the sim-to-real gap. We present YoCausal, a two-level benchmark inspired by the Violation of Expectation (VoE) paradigm from cognitive science. By temporally reversing real-world videos at zero cost as natural counterfactual samples, YoCausal establishes an arbitrarily extensible evaluation protocol. Level 1 introduces the Reverse Surprise Index (RSI), quantifying arrow-of-time perception via denoising loss. Level 2 introduces the Causality Cognition Index (CCI), which leverages a VLM to stratify datasets into causal and non-causal subsets, disentangling genuine causal reasoning from temporal bias. Evaluation of 13 state-of-the-art VDMs reveals that perceiving the arrow of time does not imply understanding causality, and a significant gap persists relative to human-level causal cognition.

</details>


### [135] [NeuROK: Generative 4D Neural Object Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30347)
*Chen Geng,Guangzhao He,Yue Gao,Yunzhi Zhang,Shangzhe Wu,Jiajun Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NeuROK proposes a data-driven method using a transformer-based encoder-decoder to learn kinematic state representations from a large-scale 4D dataset, enabling realistic temporal deformations of 3D objects without predefined physical models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current approaches for generating 4D dynamics rely on predefined physical models and system identification, which limits them to specific object categories and small-scale datasets. The authors aim to overcome these restrictions by learning a data-driven kinematic state parameterization.

Method: Learn a latent space representing all possible object states and a decoder that maps sampled latents to deformed shapes. Use a transformer-based encoder-decoder trained on a curated large-scale 4D dataset. Dynamics are modeled in the low-dimensional latent space following Lagrangian mechanics.

Result: The neural simulation framework demonstrates effectiveness and generality across diverse dynamic object types, showing clear advantages over prior works.

Conclusion: Learning a data-driven kinematic state parameterization can overcome restrictions of predefined physical models, simplifying 4D dynamics generation by operating in a low-dimensional latent space informed by classical physics principles.

Abstract: Data-driven approaches have revolutionized 3D vision, enabling transformers to effectively reconstruct and generate static 3D objects. However, generating simulative 4D dynamics -- realistic temporal deformations of static objects under various physical conditions -- remains challenging and often ad hoc, despite its importance in building comprehensive 3D world models. Most existing methods assume a predefined physical model and use system identification to estimate parameters, restricting these methods to specific categories and small-scale datasets. We propose that these restrictions can be overcome by learning a data-driven kinematic state parameterization for object-centric physical systems. Specifically, we learn both a latent space representing all possible states of the object and a decoder that maps any sampled latent to a plausibly deformed shape of the object. We refer to this parameterization as Neural Object Kinematics (NeuROK), and learn a transformer-based encoder-decoder model on a curated large-scale 4D dataset. This formulation and the learned model significantly simplify the generation of simulative dynamics since we only need to consider the dynamics within a low-dimensional latent space from the Lagrangian mechanics' perspective in classical physics. We demonstrate the effectiveness and generality of this neural simulation framework across diverse dynamic object types, showing clear advantages over prior works. Project page: https://chen-geng.com/neurok

</details>


### [136] [AdaState: Self-Evolving Anchors for Streaming Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30349)
*Yusuf Dalva,Pinar Yanardag*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Autoregressive video diffusion models suffer from static anchoring to the first frame, suppressing dynamics. The authors propose replacing the fixed first-frame anchor with an adaptive latent state that evolves with generated content, enabling richer motion and natural scene progression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autoregressive video diffusion models are structurally anchored to the first frame, whose key-value representation draws disproportionate attention, suppressing video dynamics, camera movement, and scene progression in favor of static consistency.

Method: Replace the static first-frame anchor with an adaptive hidden latent that denoises alongside content but is never rendered. The model generates its own scene anchor at each step by attending to both the previous state and current content, treating time as relative with identical positional structure and state transition at every chunk, introducing recurrence into generation.

Result: The adaptive state substantially improves video dynamics, enabling richer motion and natural scene progression within generated videos.

Conclusion: The proposed adaptive state mechanism effectively overcomes the static anchor limitation, producing temporally deeper videos with more natural motion and scene evolution.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion models generate streaming video by producing frames sequentially, conditioning each chunk on previously generated content. These models are structurally anchored to the first frame: its key-value representation occupies a privileged position in the attention cache and serves as the primary scene reference throughout generation. As the cleanest and most error-free position in the cache, this anchor draws disproportionate attention, suppressing video dynamics, and locking scene composition to the initial viewpoint even as the scene naturally evolves. The result is a temporally shallow video in which motion, camera movement, and scene progression are dampened in favor of static consistency. To address this, we replace the static anchor with an adaptive state, a hidden latent that the model denoises alongside content at every chunk but never renders. Rather than referencing a frozen first frame, the model generates its own scene anchor at each step by attending to both the previous state and the current content, producing a reference that evolves with the generated content. Unlike standard video generation, which encodes an absolute notion of time, our formulation treats time as relative: every generation step sees the same positional structure regardless of how far generation has progressed, and the state transition is identical at every chunk. Together, these properties introduce a recurrence into the generation process, where denoising serves as the transition function, and the KV cache serves as the carrier, requiring no external module. Experiments demonstrate that the adaptive state substantially improves video dynamics, enabling richer motion and natural scene progression within generated videos.

</details>


### [137] [VideoMLA: Low-Rank Latent KV Cache for Minute-Scale Autoregressive Video Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30351)
*Hidir Yesiltepe,Jiazhen Hu,Tuna Han Salih Meral,Adil Kaan Akan,Kaan Oktay,Hoda Eldardiry,Pinar Yanardag*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VideoMLA introduces Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) to video diffusion, replacing per-head KV caches with a shared low-rank latent plus decoupled 3D-RoPE positional key, reducing KV memory by 92.7% per layer, matching baselines on short horizons, achieving best long-horizon scores on VBench, and improving throughput by 1.23x on a single B200.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The per-head KV cache layout in long-rollout causal video diffusion has remained largely unchanged despite being a dominant contributor to streaming memory and latency. The authors aim to reduce this memory bottleneck.

Method: Replace per-head keys and values with a shared low-rank content latent and a shared decoupled 3D-RoPE positional key, adapted from MLA in language models. The authors also investigate why MLA works in video diffusion despite the low-rank spectral assumption not holding—finding that the MLA bottleneck itself determines effective rank regardless of initialization.

Result: 92.7% reduction in per-token KV memory per layer; matches short-horizon baselines; achieves best overall score on long horizons on VBench; 1.23x throughput improvement on a single B200.

Conclusion: MLA is an effective and efficient alternative to fixed-size sliding-window KV caches for long-rollout causal video diffusion, with strong empirical performance and surprising robustness to the non-low-rank nature of video attention.

Abstract: Long-rollout causal video diffusion has converged on a fixed-size sliding-window KV cache, with recent progress innovating within this layout by changing which tokens occupy the window or how their positions are encoded. The per-head KV layout itself, a dominant contributor to streaming memory and latency, has been mostly left unchanged. In this paper, we present the first study of Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) in video diffusion. VideoMLA replaces per-head keys and values with a shared low-rank content latent and a shared decoupled 3D-RoPE positional key, reducing per-token KV memory by 92.7% at every cached layer. We further investigate why MLA succeeds in video diffusion even though the spectral assumption often used to motivate it in language models does not hold: pretrained video attention is not low-rank, with 99%-energy effective rank far above any practical latent dimension. VideoMLA retains quality at compression ratios where direct spectral approximation would predict large reconstruction error. We show that the MLA bottleneck, rather than the pretrained spectrum, determines the effective rank: both spectral and random initialization occupy nearly the full rank budget from initialization, and training preserves this budget while adapting within it. On VBench, VideoMLA matches short-horizon streaming video diffusion baselines, achieves the best overall score at long horizons among evaluated methods, and improves throughput by 1.23x on a single B200.

</details>


### [138] [GMOS: Grounding Moving Object Segmentation in 3D Space and Time](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30352)
*Junyu Xie,Tengda Han,Weidi Xie,Andrew Zisserman*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GMOS is a 3D-aware, temporally fine-grained framework for moving object segmentation that works directly on RGB video, outperforming prior methods while running faster and supporting online inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
