Overall, the results suggest that different post-hoc explainability approaches capture partially overlapping relevance structures in EEG-based deep learning models for depression detection. Although the observed attribution patterns are broadly consistent with several previous EEG studies of MDD, the analysis should be interpreted as exploratory rather than evidence of definitive neurophysiological biomarkers or clinical applicability. The study highlights both the usefulness and limitations of post-hoc explainability for interpreting black-box EEG classifiers in psychiatric applications.

</details>


### [166] [The Hamilton-Jacobi Theory of Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28983)
*Jose Marie Antonio Miñoza,Erika Fille T. Legara,Christopher P. Monterola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper establishes an exact correspondence between neural network training/inference and Hamilton-Jacobi PDEs, unifying residual networks, transformers, RNNs/LSTMs, and SSMs under a single viscous PDE framework controlled by parameter ε, with implications for generalization, robustness, backpropagation, and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify diverse neural network architectures (ResNets, Transformers, RNNs, LSTMs, SSMs) under a common mathematical framework—specifically, viscous Hamilton-Jacobi equations—revealing their shared PDE structure and enabling rigorous theoretical analysis of their properties.

Method: The paper identifies gradient descent as solving a sequence of Hamilton-Jacobi initial-value problems via the Hopf-Cole transform. It shows log-sum-exp layers correspond exactly (and broader architectures structurally) to discretizations of viscous Hamilton-Jacobi equations, with architecture-specific Hamiltonian and viscosity. A deformation parameter ε unifies network, tropical algebra, viscous PDE, and convex optimization perspectives in a commutative diagram.

Result: Minimax optimal generalization rate O(n^{-1/(d+2)}) for fixed t; adversarial robustness controlled by ε; backpropagation emerges as the co-state equation (Pontryagin Maximum Principle); scaling exponents tied to data intrinsic dimension via PDE quadrature; closed-form O(N) influence function (softmax attribution weights πj) whose entropy landscape undergoes fold bifurcations as ε increases.

Conclusion: Neural networks can be rigorously understood through the lens of viscous Hamilton-Jacobi PDEs, providing a unified theoretical foundation that yields practical insights into generalization, robustness, optimization dynamics, and interpretability across major architectures.

Abstract: In this paper, training a neural network is identified, exactly, as a search through Hamilton--Jacobi initial-value problems: each gradient step selects the initial data of a viscous Hamilton--Jacobi equation whose Hopf--Cole propagator best fits the observations; at inference, the input is the spatial point at which that solution is evaluated and the initial condition is already encoded in the weights. The correspondence is exact for log-sum-exp layers and structural for broader architectures: residual networks, transformers, and recurrent architectures (RNNs, LSTMs, SSMs) each discretize the same class of Hamilton--Jacobi equations, with architecture-dependent Hamiltonian and viscosity. A single deformation parameter $\varepsilon$ unifies all four perspectives (network, tropical algebra, viscous PDE, convex optimization) in a commutative diagram closed under Lipschitz conditions. Quantitative consequences include: the minimax optimal generalization rate $O(n^{-1/(d+2)})$ for fixed $t$; adversarial robustness controlled by $\varepsilon$; backpropagation as the co-state equation of the Hamiltonian system for residual networks (Pontryagin Maximum Principle); scaling exponents consistent with data intrinsic dimension via PDE quadrature; and a closed-form $O(N)$ influence function (softmax attribution weights $π_j$) whose entropy landscape undergoes fold bifurcations as $\varepsilon$ increases, each merging attribution basins.

</details>


### [167] [Learning Robust and Task-Invariant Functional Representation from fMRI through Siamese Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28990)
*Jiyao Wang,Peiyu Duan,Nicha C. Dvornek,Lawrence H. Staib,Denis Sukhodolsky,Pamela Ventola,James S. Duncan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BrainSimSiam is a lightweight self-supervised framework that learns robust, generalizable fMRI representations from positive-only data pairs, achieving strong downstream performance even with limited data and outperforming supervised baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: fMRI datasets are often small and have variable label quality due to high acquisition costs and subjective psychiatric ratings, combined with high dimensionality, leading to overfitting. Existing foundation models require prohibitive computational resources.

Method: BrainSimSiam is a lightweight self-supervised representation learning framework that uses positive-only data pairs to learn robust and generalizable features from fMRI data.

Result: The learned representations generalize across diverse downstream tasks, outperform fully supervised baselines, and approach the performance of large-scale models on multiple classification and regression tasks.

Conclusion: BrainSimSiam offers a data-efficient solution for neuroimaging applications with limited data, demonstrating that lightweight self-supervised learning can be highly effective for fMRI analysis.

Abstract: Functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) is a powerful tool for investigating human brain function. However, the high cost of data acquisition and the inherent subjectivity of psychiatric rating scales often lead to datasets with small sample sizes and variable label quality, especially when targeting a specific neurological condition. Combined with the inherently high dimensionality of fMRI data, these limitations substantially increase the risk of model overfitting. Recent years have seen growing interest in developing fMRI foundation models by combining multiple datasets; however, the computational resources needed for pretraining and fine-tuning are often prohibitive. We show that a lightweight self-supervised framework yields representations that generalize across diverse downstream tasks, outperforming fully supervised baselines and approaching the performance of large-scale models. We introduce BrainSimSiam, a data-efficient self-supervised representation learning framework that leverages positive-only data pairs to learn robust and generalizable features. We demonstrate that the learned representations achieve strong performance across multiple downstream classification and regression tasks, highlighting the potential of BrainSimSiam for data-limited neuroimaging applications.

</details>


### [168] [FormInv: A Measurement Protocol for Semantic Invariance in Mathematical Reasoning Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29001)
*Nishal Thomas,Noel Thomas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A paraphrase-quality audit reveals that benchmark rankings are fragile: removing just 4 flawed paraphrases shifts leaderboard positions; cross-model consensus can detect such errors cheaply; a deeper invariance gap (SCR vs. accuracy) shows benchmarks fail to measure semantic robustness; a no-free-lunch theorem proves any ranking can be realized by weighting paraphrase families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard benchmarks may produce unreliable model rankings due to undetected errors in paraphrases and a fundamental insensitivity to semantic consistency, motivating the need for principled invariance auditing.

Method: FormInv protocol uses cross-model unanimity (≥3/4 or ≥6/9 models) to flag semantically incorrect paraphrases automatically; introduces Semantic Consistency Rate (SCR) and per-theorem Cochran's Q as invariance measures; proves a No-Free-Benchmark corollary showing rankings are sensitive to paraphrase-family weighting.

Result: Found that 3.1% of MathCheck paraphrases are incorrect; removing them drops GPT-4o from rank 2 to rank 4. In own data, 47% of auto-generated connective-variation paraphrases were incorrect. SCR ranges 50-82% vs. accuracy 86-96%, a 32-point gap invisible to standard benchmarks.

Conclusion: Benchmark designers' choice of paraphrase families implicitly determines which model wins; FormInv provides a practical protocol to audit paraphrase quality and measure semantic invariance.

Abstract: A paraphrase-quality audit of MathCheck (ICLR 2025) detected 4 semantically incorrect paraphrases in 129 groups (3.1%); removing them drops GPT-4o from rank 2 to rank 4 and elevates Claude Haiku and DeepSeek V3 above it; these ranking changes are invisible to any single-model evaluation. Cross-model unanimity found these errors automatically (>= 3/4 models for MathCheck; >= 6/9 for our primary evaluation) for under $10; in our own dataset the same protocol found that 47% of auto-generated connective-variation paraphrases were semantically incorrect. That flaw compounds a deeper measurement gap: Claude Haiku 4.5 achieves 86% accuracy yet SCR=50%, meaning half its theorems are answered differently under semantically equivalent restatements, while aggregate accuracy across 9 models spans only 86-96% yet Semantic Consistency Rates (SCR) span 50-82% -- a 32-point gap invisible to standard benchmarks. Formally, for any target ranking over 9 frontier models there exists a weighting over paraphrase families that realizes it (No-Free-Benchmark corollary), because no model Pareto-dominates all families -- so benchmark designers who select families are implicitly choosing which model wins. FormInv supplies the audit protocol (replicated on external benchmarks at 100% recall), SCR and per-theorem Cochran's Q as primary invariance measures evaluated on 9 models across 366-811 items (on Lean4-verified theorems), and FormInvSelector for regime-aware model selection.

</details>


### [169] [FedQHD: Closed-Form Function-Space Federated Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29002)
*Yuchen Hou,Yongshan Chen,Zhuowen Zou,Calvin Yeung,Mohsen Imani,Tian Lan,Mahdi Imani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedQHD is a federated Q-learning method using hyperdimensional (random-feature) encoders with linear readouts, enabling closed-form aggregation. It addresses function-space inconsistency in FedAvg-style parameter averaging. Theoretical analysis of the "federation gap" and empirical results show improved performance with less computation on continuous-state control benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FedAvg-style federated reinforcement learning suffers from function-space inconsistency: averaging parameters does not correspond to averaging value functions, especially when clients use heterogeneous or nonlinear encoders.

Method: Uses hyperdimensional (random-feature) state encoders with linear readout layers. With shared encoders, aggregates local readout matrices via weighted averaging. With heterogeneous encoders, uses a shared anchor-state set for teacher-student style distillation via ridge projection.

Result: Matches or outperforms FedAvg and distillation baselines on four continuous-state control benchmarks with less computation. Empirical federation gap aligns with theoretical analysis.

Conclusion: The federation gap decomposes into subspace misalignment, anchor-set conditioning, and regularization bias. With sufficient anchor-to-dimension ratio (m ≥ D_i), the gap reduces to encoder heterogeneity floor.

Abstract: Federated reinforcement learning enables decentralized agents to collaboratively improve policies or value estimates without exchanging raw trajectories. However, FedAvg-style parameter averaging is not function-space consistent: when clients use heterogeneous encoders or even identical nonlinear networks, averaged parameters need not correspond to the weighted average of client value functions in any common function space. We propose FedQHD, a federated Q-learning method using hyperdimensional (random-feature) state encoders with a linear readout, so that Q-functions are nonlinear in state yet linear in trainable parameters. This linear structure enables closed-form aggregation. With a shared encoder, the function-space consensus update coincides exactly with weighted averaging of local readout matrices. With heterogeneous encoders, the server constructs a global teacher by averaging client Q-values on a shared anchor-state set, and each client compiles this teacher into its local representation via a single ridge projection. We formalize the federation gap -- the error incurred when compiling a federated teacher into a heterogeneous client representation -- relative to a client-specific oracle projection. We show that this gap decomposes into subspace misalignment, anchor-set conditioning, and regularization bias. We further identify the anchor-to-dimension ratio $m \geq D_i$ as the well-conditioned regime in which the gap reduces to a multiple of the encoder heterogeneity floor. On four continuous-state, discrete-action control benchmarks, FedQHD matches or outperforms FedAvg-style baselines and distillation-based alternatives while requiring substantially less computation, and the empirical dependence of the federation gap on encoder dimension matches our theoretical analysis.

</details>


### [170] [LoRe: Adaptive Interaction-Evaluation Routing with Per-Step Interaction Budgets for Iterative Graph Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29005)
*Jintao Li,Yong-Yi Wang,Zheng-An Wang,Heng Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRe is a training-free, inference-time wrapper that dynamically budgets interactions in diffusion-based neural solvers for combinatorial optimization, achieving significant speedups (8-15x) and memory reductions (12-44x) while preserving solution quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based neural solvers for combinatorial optimization are expensive due to repeatedly evaluating dense edge/factor interactions, making inference slow and memory-bound at scale.

Method: LoRe enforces per-step interaction-evaluation budgeting by dynamically routing computation to high-conflict or high-uncertainty interactions, evaluating only a fixed fraction of interactions each iteration, inspired by many-body physics computational methods.

Result: On MIS: 3x beyond baseline's memory limit, ~8x speedup, ~12x peak-memory reduction. On TSP (n=1000): ~15x speedup, 44x memory reduction with competitive tour quality. Also demonstrates zero-shot robustness to topology shifts.

Conclusion: LoRe is a simple, training-free drop-in wrapper that substantially improves scalability of diffusion-based combinatorial optimization solvers without sacrificing solution quality.

Abstract: Diffusion-based neural solvers for combinatorial optimization repeatedly re-evaluate dense edge/factor interactions, making inference expensive in wall-clock time and often memory-bound at scale. Inspired by the computational methodologies of many-body physics, we introduce LoRe, a training-free, inference-time drop-in wrapper that enforces per-step interaction-evaluation budgeting: at each iteration, it evaluates only a fixed fraction of interactions by dynamically routing computation to high-conflict or high-uncertainty interactions, instead of using a fixed sparsification (e.g., static kNN graphs or static masks). Under fully inclusive end-to-end wall-clock accounting, LoRe substantially improves scalability on the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem, extending feasible inference more than $3\times$ beyond the baseline's out-of-memory limit, delivering a $\sim 8\times$ speedup and a $\sim 12\times$ peak-memory reduction, with solution quality preserved in this regime. Demonstrating cross-task generality on the large-scale Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) and zero-shot robustness to topology shifts, LoRe achieves a $\sim 15\times$ speedup at $n=1000$ with a $44\times$ memory reduction and competitive tour quality.

</details>


### [171] [Causal Intelligence for Constraint-Aware Intervention Design to Induce State Transitions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29008)
*Zixuan Song,Uwe Mueller,Dimitris V. Manatakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: COAST is a causal-intelligence framework that learns causal graphs from data and uses constraint-aware optimization to design interventions that drive systems from a source state to a target state, demonstrated on synthetic and biological benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing predictive models lack mechanistic insight and principled decision-making frameworks for driving state transitions in complex systems.

Method: COAST integrates feature selection, causal discovery, structural causal modeling, mechanism-level attribution of distribution shifts, and a constraint-aware multi-objective optimization balancing efficacy, complexity, and stability.

Result: On synthetic and real biological datasets, COAST recovers key causal drivers and identifies robust single- and multi-target intervention strategies that achieve desired state transitions.

Conclusion: COAST provides a modular, domain-agnostic framework with transparent mechanistic rationales to guide experimental validation of intervention strategies.

Abstract: Driving a system from one state to another through targeted interventions is a fundamental challenge in science, yet most predictive models offer limited mechanistic insight and no principled framework for decision-making. Here we present COAST (Causally Optimal Actions for State Transitions), a causal-intelligence approach for the in-silico design of constrained interventions that induce user-defined state transitions. Given data characterizing source and target states, COAST learns context-specific causal graphs and structural causal models, attributes observed distributional shifts to mechanism-level causal drivers, and introduces a novel constraint-aware multi-objective optimization formulation that balances transition efficacy, intervention complexity, and target-state stability. The approach is modular and domain-agnostic, integrating feature selection, causal discovery, causal modeling, and intervention identification and evaluation through interchangeable components. Across synthetic benchmarks and real biological datasets, COAST recovers key causal drivers and identifies robust single- and multi-target intervention strategies that achieve desired state transitions, accompanied by transparent mechanistic rationales to guide experimental validation.

</details>


### [172] [Label-Free Reinforcement Learning via Cross-Model Entropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29009)
*Matt Gorbett,Hossein Shirazi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-Model Entropy (CME) uses a separate verifier model's log-likelihood of a generator's responses as a label-free reward signal for RL post-training, enabling open-ended instruction following without ground-truth rewards or human labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL post-training approaches are bottlenecked by reward signals: ground-truth verifiable rewards restrict training to domains with automatic correctness checks, while human preference labels are expensive and prone to reward hacking. Self-referential signals (majority voting, token entropy) risk reinforcing a model's own errors.

Method: Proposes Cross-Model Entropy (CME) — the mean log-likelihood of a generator's response under a separate, independent verifier model — as a label-free, continuous, training-free reward signal. Integrated into GRPO without modifying the training loop.

Result: On open-ended instruction following (UltraFeedback prompts, AlpacaEval 2.0), CME rewards beat untrained baselines across four model families (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, OLMo) and three training regimes (pretrained, SFT, instruction-tuned), with tie-adjusted win rates from 52.5% to 71.4%.

Conclusion: CME extends label-free RL to open-ended instruction following where self-referential signals are inapplicable, providing a grounded and game-resistant reward signal via a separate verifier model.

Abstract: Post-training large language models with reinforcement learning is bottlenecked by the reward signal. Existing approaches require either ground-truth verifiable rewards, restricting training to domains with automatic correctness checks (e.g., mathematics, code execution), or human preference labels, which are expensive to collect and prone to reward hacking. Recent label-free methods replace ground-truth verifiers with self-referential signals like majority voting or token entropy over a model's own outputs, but risk reinforcing a model's own errors. In this work we propose Cross-Model Entropy (CME), the mean log-likelihood of a generator's response under a separate verifier model, as a label-free reward signal for RL post-training. CME is continuous, training-free, and grounded in the principle that responses a verifier finds unsurprising are likely correct or high quality. Because the verifier is independent of the generator, the signal cannot be gamed through self-consistency. We integrate CME into GRPO with no other changes to the training loop, extending label-free RL to open-ended instruction following -- a regime where self-referential signals are inapplicable or poorly suited. On open-ended instruction following (UltraFeedback prompts, evaluated on AlpacaEval 2.0), CME rewards beat the untrained base in head-to-head LLM-as-Judge comparisons across four model families (Qwen, Llama, Gemma, OLMo) and three training regimes (pretrained, SFT, and instruction-tuned), with tie-adjusted win rates ranging from 52.5% to 71.4%. Code will be released upon publication.

</details>


### [173] [Designing Active Tether-Net Systems for Space Debris Capture with Graph-Learning-Aided Mixed-Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29021)
*Feng Liu,Achira Boonrath,Gishnu Madhu,Eleonora M. Botta,Souma Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based optimization approach to solve a Mixed Combinatorial Nonlinear Programming (MCNLP) problem for designing active tether-net systems for space debris capture. The GNN recommends candidate combinations, reducing the MCNLP to a standard NLP solvable by Particle Swarm Optimization, achieving faster convergence to similar optimal solutions compared to direct MCNLP solving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Active tether-net systems are promising for capturing space debris, but systematic exploration of their design and control choices is limited due to the complex MCNLP problem involving continuous, integer, and categorical variables (net connectivity, component choices). Classical binary encoding and integer coding methods are ineffective for such nonlinear, multimodal problems.

Method: A GNN is trained to score and recommend candidate combinations (represented as graph nodes) given continuous variable vectors as input, effectively reducing the MCNLP to a standard NLP. The NLP is then solved using a Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with gradient-based fine-tuning.

Result: On the tether-net system design problem (net morphology, MU mass/thrusters, aiming points), the GNN-based recommender provides significantly faster convergence to similar optimal solutions compared to direct MCNLP solving.

Conclusion: The GNN-aided reduction approach is an effective and solver-agnostic method for tackling MCNLPs in engineering design, demonstrated on the tether-net system for space debris capture.

Abstract: Active tether-net systems are a promising solution for capturing large non-cooperative targets, such as space debris, by deploying a flexible net manipulated by maneuverable units (MUs). However, concurrent systematic explorations of design and control choices of the tether-net system to understand its full potential remain limited, partly due to the complex, constrained, nonlinear optimization problem that it presents -- one that involves a mixture of continuous, integer and categorical variables, with the latter two arising from net connectivity and component choices, respectively. Classical binary encoding methods are often ineffective for solving highly nonlinear and multimodal Mixed Combinatorial Nonlinear Programmings (MCNLPs) in engineering design, while integer coding approaches can introduce spurious relations among combinations. Given the graph-structured characteristics of the combinatorial space, this paper adopts and extends a new graph-learning-aided optimization approach to solve this MCNLP problem. Here, a Graph Neural Network (GNN) is trained to score (as output) and thereof recommend candidate combinations represented as nodes in a graph, with the continuous variable vector portion of a candidate design given as input. As a result, the MCNLP optimization reduces to an NLP, which can be solved using standard solvers. While this reduction approach is agnostic to the choice of the NLP solver, here a state-of-the-art Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm with gradient-based fine-tuning is used as the solver. Demonstrated on the problem of concurrently designing the morphology of the net, choice of mass and thrusters in the MUs and aiming points used by the controller of the tether-net system, the GNN-based recommender is shown to provide significantly faster convergence to similar optimal solutions, compared to direct solution of the MCNLP problem.

</details>


### [174] [Return-to-Go Is More Than a Number: Q-Guided Alignment for Return-Conditioned Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29028)
*Yuxiao Yang,Weitong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-ALIGN DT improves conditioned sequence models by aligning RTG with actual policy Q-values, achieving better controllability and performance in offline RL tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CSMs treat RTGs as simple numerical inputs without ensuring alignment between the RTG and the actual performance of the learned policy, leading to poor controllability.

Method: Proposes Q-ALIGN DT which uses a Q-function to provide dense guidance to CSMs and fine-tunes it with RTG-perturbation, ensuring that higher RTGs map to higher expected returns.

Result: Achieves superior controllability and performance across D4RL benchmark; generalizes to velocity-tracking tasks where prior methods fail.

Conclusion: Theoretically near-optimal with sufficiently high RTG; empirically demonstrates precise alignment and structured policy family learning.

Abstract: Conditioned Sequence Models (CSMs) learn policies by treating return-to-go (RTG) as a control signal. However, existing CSMs often treat the RTGs as simple numerical inputs rather than aligning them with the performance of their policies. In this paper, we propose Q-ALIGN DT, a framework that enforces this alignment by ensuring the $Q$-value of the output policy is consistent with the input RTG. By leveraging a $Q$ function to provide dense guidance to CSMs and further fine-tuning it using an RTG-perturbation technique with the CSM, our method ensures that higher RTGs are consistently mapped to trajectories with higher expected returns. Theoretically, we show that Q-ALIGN DT can efficiently learn the desired policy and output a near-optimal one when the RTG is sufficiently high. Empirically, we demonstrate through extensive experiments that Q-ALIGN DT achieves superior controllability and performance across the D4RL benchmark. Remarkably, our model effectively learns a structured family of policies that maintains precise alignment and generalizes to tasks like velocity-tracking where prior methods fail.

</details>


### [175] [Theoretical Foundations and Effective Algorithms for Policy-Aware Simulator Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29032)
*Christoph Dann,Yishay Mansour,Mehryar Mohri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a minimax game framework for MBRL where the model learns to be strategically robust to adversarial policies, rather than merely predictively accurate, with theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements of 1.5-2.2x in strategic prediction error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MBRL agents suffer from a "reality gap" where policies exploit minor model inaccuracies, succeeding in simulation but failing in the real world. The authors argue this stems from optimizing for predictive accuracy rather than strategic robustness.

Method: Formulates simulator learning as a zero-sum minimax game between a model player and an adversarial policy player. Provides theoretical analysis including online learning guarantees, critic-based simplification, and Error-MDP duality that converts the problem into a standard RL problem.

Result: Reduces prediction error in strategically important regions by 1.5-2.2x and enables simulation-trained policies to match near-optimal real-world performance on continuous control tasks.

Conclusion: Strategic robustness is a better objective than predictive accuracy for learning world models, and the proposed minimax formulation with theoretical guarantees effectively bridges the simulation-to-real gap.

Abstract: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents typically learn world models by minimizing predictive loss. However, powerful RL optimizers inevitably exploit minor model inaccuracies, leading to simulator exploitation and a reality gap where policies succeed in simulation but fail in the real world. We propose that the objective for learning simulators should be strategic robustness rather than predictive accuracy, and formulate this as a zero-sum minimax game between a model player and an adversarial policy player. We provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis: (1) an online learning guarantee showing the game is learnable with sublinear regret bounds; (2) a tractable critic-based simplification bounding the global policy-value gap by the local critic's loss; and (3) an Error-MDP duality, proving that finding the worst-case policy is formally dual to a standard RL problem where the reward is the one-step critic error. This duality yields a provably convergent active data selection algorithm. Experiments on continuous control tasks demonstrate that our approach reduces prediction error in strategically important regions by $1.5$-$2.2\times$ and enables policies trained purely in simulation to match near-optimal real-world performance.

</details>


### [176] [Momentum Based Reward Design for Low Emission Traffic Signal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29693)
*Chinmay Mundane,Amith Manoharan,Arun Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Momentum-Based Reward Function (MBRF) for DRL-based adaptive traffic signal control, which encourages continuous vehicle movement rather than just penalizing congestion, achieving better throughput-emission trade-offs and more stable learning compared to traditional reward functions and classical controllers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban traffic congestion causes long commute times and pollution; traditional traffic signal controls fail to adapt dynamically; existing DRL-based methods using delay/queue-based rewards produce short-sighted or unstable policies.

Method: Proposes a Momentum-Based Reward Function (MBRF) that incentivizes vehicles to keep moving rather than only penalizing congestion; evaluated using SUMO simulation under standard traffic metrics.

Result: MBRF achieves better throughput-emission trade-offs and more stable learning behavior than delay/queue-based rewards and classical controllers (Max Pressure, LQF).

Conclusion: MBRF is an effective reward design for DRL-based traffic signal control, improving both efficiency and environmental impact without requiring infrastructure changes.

Abstract: Urban traffic congestion is a growing global issue contributing significantly to long commute times and environmental pollution. Traditional traffic signal control systems often fail to adapt to dynamic traffic conditions. Adaptive traffic signal control can improve urban traffic without changing road infrastructure. Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has shown strong performance for this task, but existing delay and queue-based rewards often produce short-sighted or unstable policies. This paper proposes a Momentum-Based Reward Function (MBRF) that encourages vehicles to keep moving rather than penalizing congestion alone. The method is evaluated in SUMO (Simulation of Urban MObility) using standard traffic metrics such as waiting time, queue length, throughput, and CO2 emissions. Results show that the proposed reward produces better throughput-emission trade-offs and more stable learning behavior than delay or queue-based rewards, as well as classical controllers such as Max Pressure and LQF.

</details>


### [177] [Moment Matching Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29033)
*Yiyan,Liang,Sifei Liu,Weitong Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoMa QL uses Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) to match all moment statistics between distributions, accelerating inference in score-based/flow-based generative models for RL while maintaining competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Score-based and flow-based generative models have high inference latency due to iterative sampling, creating a computational bottleneck in reinforcement learning applications.

Method: Proposes Moment Matching Q-Learning (MoMa QL) using Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) to match all orders of statistics between original and target distributions, enforcing strong regularization on moment statistics for stable convergence of conditional score functions.

Result: MoMa QL is more computationally efficient with comparable/competitive performance on D4RL tasks, and demonstrates superior performance in offline-to-online RL by accelerating action sampling for flow-based policies.

Conclusion: Moment matching via MMD effectively reduces inference latency in generative model-based RL, enabling faster adaptability for online fine-tuning.

Abstract: Score-based and flow-based generative models exhibit remarkable expressive capacity in capturing complex distributions, and have been extensively deployed in tasks ranging from image generation to reinforcement learning. Nevertheless, these models suffer from prolonged inference latency, which imposes a significant computational bottleneck in RL with iterative sampling. To overcome this limitation, we propose a new framework named Moment Matching Q-Learning (MoMa QL), which utilizes a technique from statistical hypothesis testing known as maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) that intend to match all orders of statistics between the original and target distribution. By enforcing strong regularization on all moment statistics, this algorithm guarantees distribution-level convergence for conditional score function and remains stable under various hyperparameters. Empirically, we show that our method MoMa QL is more computationally efficient with a comparable if not competitive performance in various D4RL tasks. Remarkably, by accelerating the action sampling process for flow-based policies, MoMa QL demonstrates superior performance in offline-to-online RL tasks because of faster and stronger adaptability for online interactive finetuning.

</details>


### [178] [Parallel Adaptive Multi-Objective Evolutionary Learning of Discretized Bayesian Network Classifiers for Clinical Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29058)
*Damy M. F. Ha,Tanja Alderliesten,Peter A. N. Bosman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper improves Baymex, an evolutionary algorithm for learning Bayesian Networks, by adding parallelization (up to 54x speedup on 16-core CPU) and an anti-overfitting mechanism. It extends Baymex to classification tasks using multi-objective optimization of cross-entropy loss and BIC complexity, showing statistically comparable or better performance than clinical baselines on real-world datasets while producing compact, inspectable networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Baymex previously required high computation time and was only tested on synthetic data. The authors aim to improve scalability and evaluate Baymex on real-world clinical classification tasks to demonstrate practical utility.

Method: Introduced parallelization strategy and adaptive steering to reduce overfitting. Reconfigured Baymex for BN classification via multi-objective optimization of cross-entropy loss and BIC complexity. Evaluated on three clinical datasets (RADCURE, SUPPORT, in-house) against decision trees, logistic regression, naive Bayes, and random forests.

Result: Achieved up to 54x speedup on 16-core CPU. Baymex obtained statistically similar or better predictive performance compared to clinical baselines while producing compact, clinically inspectable BNs with predictors consistent with established clinical factors.

Conclusion: Baymex is now scalable for real-world clinical classification, offering a practical trade-off between predictive performance and model interpretability, making it suitable for clinical decision support.

Abstract: Bayesian Networks (BNs) are of interest from an explainable AI viewpoint, offering transparent probabilistic models for decision support. Baymex is a recently introduced multi-objective evolutionary algorithm for learning discretized BNs, enabling experts to trade-off different objectives of interest, such as likelihood, model complexity, and prior beliefs. While Baymex has been shown to outperform state-of-the-art BN learning approaches, Baymex still 1) requires a lot of computation time and 2) has only been evaluated on synthetic data. To improve scalability, we introduce a parallelization strategy as well as a mechanism that enables adaptively steering optimization toward networks that overfit less. We furthermore reconfigure Baymex to train a BN classifier through multi-objective optimization of cross-entropy loss and the BIC complexity term so as to evaluate its performance on real-world clinical classification tasks. Besides observing speedups up to over 54 times on a 16-core CPU, comparisons against clinically familiar baselines (decision trees, logistic regression, naive Bayes, and random forests) on two open-source (RADCURE and SUPPORT) and one in-house dataset, show that Baymex obtains statistically similar or better predictive performance while producing compact, clinically inspectable BNs. Importantly, Baymex finds multiple plausible BN classifiers that contain predictors consistent with established clinical factors.

</details>


### [179] [Ensemble Score Filtering for Real-Data Energy Consumption Forecast Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29072)
*Ruoyu Hu,Dahai Yu,Feng Bao,Guang Wang,Guannan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper applies the Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) to correct forecasts from a pretrained black-box spatiotemporal energy forecasting model using partial/noisy observations, showing EnSF outperforms open-loop propagation and Ensemble Kalman Filter in nonlinear settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Energy consumption measurements are often incomplete, noisy, or delayed, making accurate state estimation and forecasting challenging. There is a need for methods that can assimilate partial observations to correct imperfect forecasting model outputs.

Method: Uses a pretrained black-box spatiotemporal forecasting model as the state propagator, then applies the Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) — a score-based diffusion model with closed-form score representation and Monte Carlo approximation that avoids retraining — to assimilate partial/noisy observations and correct forecasts.

Result: Open-loop propagation of the forecasting model becomes unreliable over long horizons. EnSF-based correction substantially improves state estimation. EnSF outperforms Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) under nonlinear observation settings.

Conclusion: The EnSF is an effective data assimilation method for high-dimensional energy consumption forecasting, providing robust forecast correction without requiring neural network retraining during assimilation, especially under nonlinear observation conditions.

Abstract: Accurate estimation and forecasting of energy consumption are important for power-system operation, planning, and demand-side management. In practice, however, complete and timely measurements may not always be available, and the observed data can be partial, noisy, or delayed. This motivates the use of learned forecasting models for predicting the evolving consumption state, together with data assimilation methods for sequential forecast correction. In this work, we study a high-dimensional data assimilation problem for real energy-consumption data. \modeltext{The forward prediction is supplied by a pretrained black-box spatio-temporal forecasting model, which is treated as the state propagator in the filtering procedure.} We employ the Ensemble Score Filter (EnSF) to assimilate partial and noisy observations and to correct the forecast trajectory over time. The EnSF uses score-based diffusion models to approximate filtering distributions and avoids retraining neural-network score models during assimilation by using a closed-form score representation and Monte Carlo approximation. Numerical experiments demonstrate that open-loop propagation of the learned forecasting model can become unreliable over long horizons, while EnSF-based correction substantially improves state estimation. Comparisons with the Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) further show that EnSF provides stronger correction under the nonlinear observation setting considered in this work.

</details>


### [180] [Knowledge Offloading: Decomposing LLMs into Sparse Backbones and Memory Modules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29075)
*Karim Galliamov,Rochelle Choenni,Ivan Titov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KOFF decomposes pretrained LLMs into a sparse shared backbone and domain-specific external memory modules (LoRA adapters + learned KV caches), achieving ~12% sparsity while preserving performance, with specialized neurons preferentially moved to memories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether the capacity of LLMs (which mix general capabilities and domain-specific knowledge in a single parameter set) can be reorganized by keeping general computation in a shared backbone while offloading specialized knowledge to external memory modules.

Method: Starting from a frozen base model, KOFF jointly learns (1) a structured pruning mask and (2) lightweight recovery modules implemented as LoRA adapters and learned key-value caches, to decompose models into sparse backbones and domain-specific memories.

Result: At ~12% global sparsity, KOFF preserves unpruned model performance while pruning without memories degrades sharply. LoRA and learned KV memories are complementary. Language-specific neurons are preferentially removed while language-general neurons remain in the backbone.

Conclusion: Knowledge can be meaningfully reallocated between a shared core and swappable external memories, suggesting a modular architecture where specialized knowledge is externalized from a general-purpose backbone.

Abstract: LLMs encode both general capabilities and domain-specific knowledge in a single set of parameters. We ask whether this capacity can be reorganized: keeping broadly useful computation in a shared backbone, while moving specialized knowledge into external memory modules. We propose \emph{knowledge offloading} (KOFF), a framework for decomposing a pretrained LLM into a sparse shared backbone and domain-specific memories. Starting from a frozen base model, we jointly learn a structured pruning mask and lightweight recovery modules, implemented as LoRA adapters and learned key-value caches. Across Llama and Qwen models from 3B to 8B, we find that non-trivial capacity can be moved out of the shared backbone without a large loss in model ability. At around 12\% global sparsity, KOFF preserves much of the unpruned model's performance, while pruning the same frozen model without memories degrades sharply. Ablations show that LoRA and learned KV memories are complementary, and specialization analyses suggest that the learned decomposition is meaningful: language-specific neurons are preferentially removed while language-general neurons largely remain in the backbone. These results suggest that knowledge can be reallocated between a shared core and swappable external memories.

</details>


### [181] [OISD: On-Policy Internal Self-Distillation of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29089)
*Xinyu Liu,Darryl Cherian Jacob,Yang Zhou,Jindong Wang,Pan He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OISD is a new RL post-training framework that improves reasoning by distilling predictive signals from the final layer to intermediate layers through logit and attention alignment, achieving consistent improvements over baselines on math reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL post-training approaches focus on optimizing final outputs with sparse outcome-level rewards, ignoring the rich predictive signals encoded in intermediate representations during training.

Method: OISD transfers on-policy predictive signals from the final layer (acting as both policy and detached internal teacher) to intermediate layers via two mechanisms: logit alignment (transferring high-level reasoning behaviors) and attention alignment (enforcing consistent attention patterns), combined with GRPO using signed advantage-weighted Jensen-Shannon alignment.

Result: OISD achieves substantial and consistent improvements over strong reasoning RL baselines across four mathematical reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: On-policy internal self-distillation is an effective paradigm for enhancing reasoning by leveraging intermediate representations during RL post-training, without requiring external privileged information.

Abstract: Recent reinforcement learning (RL) post-training approaches primarily optimize the final output policy using sparse outcome-level rewards, while largely overlooking predictive signals encoded in intermediate representations. In this paper, we introduce a new paradigm called on-policy internal self-distillation and propose the OISD framework, which improves reasoning by transferring on-policy predictive signals from the final layer to intermediate representations. During rollout and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) optimization, the final layer acts as both the policy and a detached internal teacher for selected intermediate layers, which are guided to align with it through two complementary mechanisms: logit alignment, which transfers high-level reasoning behaviors (how to think), and attention alignment, which enforces consistent attention patterns (where to look) from the final layer to the selected intermediate layer, both without requiring external privileged information. Our OISD, together with GRPO, employs signed advantage-weighted Jensen--Shannon alignment to distill informative intermediate representations while preserving policy consistency under a unified acting policy. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of OISD, with substantial and consistent improvements over strong reasoning RL baselines across four mathematical reasoning tasks. The code will be released at https://github.com/THE-MALT-LAB/OISD

</details>


### [182] [Bridging Chemists and AI: An Expert-Augmented Framework for Interpretable Route Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29108)
*Yujia Guo,Mikhail Kabeshov,Tat Hong Duong Le,Samuel Genheden,Marco V. Mijangos,Varvara Voinarvoska,Giulia Bergonzini,Ola Engkvist,Samuel Kaski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A DeepSets-based scoring framework integrating expert knowledge and machine learning improves multi-step route assessment in organic synthesis, achieving 0.78 Spearman correlation and 60.2% top-1 ranking accuracy, outperforming prior baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data-driven route assessment systems oversimplify the multi-objective nature of synthesis design and rely on imperfect proxy datasets (e.g., patent routes), lacking universal criteria for evaluating multi-step synthetic routes.

Method: A DeepSets-based model trained on tree edit distance between reference and machine-generated routes, then fine-tuned with expert evaluations to produce quantitative scores and qualitative categories (Good, Plausible, Bad).

Result: Spearman correlation of 0.78, Pearson correlation of 0.77 for category prediction, and 60.2% top-1 ranking accuracy for score prediction, significantly outperforming the 17.5% baseline.

Conclusion: Integrating expert domain knowledge with machine learning yields both more accurate and interpretable route assessment, offering a practical framework for synthesis planning in medicinal and process chemistry.

Abstract: Selecting efficient multi-step synthetic routes is a central challenge in organic synthesis, particularly in medicinal and process chemistry, where route choice directly impacts feasibility, cost, and development efficiency. Data-driven assessment systems often oversimplify the multi-objective nature of synthesis design and rely on proxy datasets, such as patent routes, rather than universally grounded criteria. To address this, we introduce an expert-augmented, data-driven scoring framework that integrates machine learning with chemists' domain knowledge for both numerical and explainable route assessment. A DeepSets-based model is trained using tree edit distance between reference and machine-generated routes, and then fine-tuned with expert evaluations to produce both quantitative scores and interpretable qualitative categories: Good, Plausible, and Bad. The resulting system achieves a Spearman correlation coefficient of 0.78 and a Pearson correlation of 0.77 for category assessment prediction, and 60.2% top-1 ranking accuracy for score prediction, substantially outperforming the previous baseline of 17.5%.

</details>


### [183] [When and How Long? The Readout-Mediator Angle in Temporal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29126)
*Shreyas Fadnavis,Praitayini Kanakaraj,Felix Wyss*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Linear probes can decode interpretable features from model activations that are completely orthogonal to the model's actual causal computation, as demonstrated on calendar-date reasoning where a sin/cos probe recovers day-of-year but ablating its direction has no effect, while a DAS-found subspace collapses performance. This readout-mediator orthogonality replicates across scales, families, and domains, undermining probe-based interpretability for safety monitoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the assumption that what linear probes decode from neural network activations necessarily reflects the model's actual causal reasoning process, and to demonstrate that probe-readout directions can be entirely orthogonal to the model's true computation.

Method: Using calendar-date duration reasoning as a testbed, the authors compare a sin/cos linear probe (recovering day-of-year) against Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) subspaces; measure the angle between the two subspaces against a Haar-uniform null; perform causal ablation experiments; reverse-engineer the circuit (attention heads with QK offsets at ±30 and ±61 days, MLPs converting absolute date to duration); and validate with sparse autoencoder decomposition across four model scales (1.5B-9B) and two families, with preliminary tests on spatial displacement and symbolic arithmetic.

Result: The probe-recovered direction is statistically indistinguishable from a random subspace relative to the causally relevant DAS subspace (readout-mediator angle equals Haar-uniform null). Ablating the probe direction has no effect on model answers, while ablating the DAS subspace collapses performance. The dissociation replicates across scales and families, and preliminary evidence suggests it generalizes to spatial displacement and symbolic arithmetic domains.

Conclusion: Readout-mediator orthogonality is a general failure mode of probe-based interpretability, directly undermining proposals to use probes as runtime safety monitors: a probe can report high confidence on a direction the model has silently abandoned.

Abstract: A linear probe can decode a representation almost perfectly and yet be completely irrelevant to how the model uses it. On calendar-date duration reasoning in language models, a $\sin$/$\cos$ probe recovers day-of-year from a layer's activations, yet ablating its direction has no effect on the model's answers -- while ablating a four-dimensional subspace found by Distributed Alignment Search (DAS) at the same layer collapses performance entirely. We measure the angle between these two subspaces -- the \emph{readout-mediator angle} -- and find it indistinguishable from the angle between two random subspaces (the Haar-uniform null), meaning the probe has learned a direction orthogonal to the model's actual computation. Reverse-engineering the circuit reveals why: attention heads route month-grained context through learned QK offsets at ${\pm}30$ and ${\pm}61$ days, and MLPs then convert \emph{when} (absolute date) into \emph{how long} (duration) -- all downstream of the causal subspace the probe never touches. Sparse-autoencoder decomposition confirms the split: probe-aligned and DAS-aligned features encode semantically disjoint concepts with negligible causal overlap. The dissociation replicates across four scales ($1.5$-$9\,$B) and two model families, with preliminary evidence on two further domains (spatial displacement, symbolic arithmetic), suggesting that readout-mediator orthogonality is a general failure mode of probe-based interpretability. This directly undermines proposals to deploy probes as runtime safety monitors: the probe can report high confidence on a direction the model has silently abandoned.

</details>


### [184] [Apertus LLM Family Expansion via Distillation and Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29128)
*Andrei Panferov,Davit Melikidze,Martin Jaggi,Dan Alistarh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Apertus-v1.1, a distilled family of LLMs (up to 4B parameters) derived from the 8B Apertus model using distillation and quantization, showing cost-effective expansion across different hardware constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are used widely but must satisfy varying budget and hardware constraints. Releasing model families at different sizes helps meet diverse requirements, but creating new models from scratch for each size is expensive.

Method: The authors validate distillation and quantization as cost-effective methods. They produce Apertus-v1.1 from the Apertus 8B LLM, a distilled family with up to 4B parameters trained on 1.7T permissive license tokens.

Result: The approach demonstrates cost-efficiency and strong accuracy performance across a wide range of hardware and system requirements.

Conclusion: Distillation and quantization are validated as effective strategies for expanding LLM families to new sizes and hardware formats without prohibitive costs.

Abstract: The wide adoption of LLMs has led to their use in great variety of applications and scenarios, such as chatbot assistants and data annotation, creating the need for the models to satisfy certain budget and hardware constraints. This has led to the trend of LLMs being released in batches consisting of similar models of various sizes for the family of models to adhere to as wide of a range of constraints as possible. In this paper, we validate distillation and quantization as a cost-effective way to expand model families to new sizes and hardware formats. Based on the open-recipe Apertus 8B LLM, we produce Apertus-v1.1 - a distilled family of models with up to 4B parameters trained on 1.7T permissive license tokens. We demonstrate cost-efficiency and strong accuracy performance of our approach for covering large ranges of hardware and systems requirements.

</details>


### [185] [Optimal Gap-Dependent Regret for Private Stochastic Decision-Theoretic Online Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29148)
*Tommaso Cesari,Roberto Colomboni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper resolves an open problem in differentially private online learning by proving a matching upper bound of O(log K/Δ_min + log K/ε) for stochastic decision-theoretic learning with pure event-level DP, matching the known lower bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine the optimal gap-dependent regret rate for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning under pure event-level differential privacy, which was posed as an open problem by Hu and Mehta at COLT.

Method: The algorithm partitions time into blocks of exponentially increasing size, plays a single action throughout each block, and selects the next action via an exponential mechanism applied to a data-independent random prefix of the previous block. A entropy-potential argument controls all privacy-dominated large-gap actions at cost log K/ε.

Result: Achieves explicit regret bound Reg_T ≤ 1000 · (log K/Δ_min + log K/ε) for every horizon T, matching the known lower bound up to constants.

Conclusion: The optimal gap-dependent regret rate under pure event-level DP is Θ(log K/Δ_min + log K/ε), resolving the COLT open problem. The result is horizon-free and matches the lower bound.

Abstract: We study stochastic decision-theoretic online learning with full information and event-level pure differential privacy. A COLT open problem of Hu and Mehta asks to determine the optimal gap-dependent regret rate for stochastic decision-theoretic online learning under pure event-level differential privacy. For $K$ actions, losses in $[0,1]$, and a unique best action separated from the second-best action by gap $Δ_{\min}$, the known lower bound is of order $
  \frac{\log K}{\min\{Δ_{\min},\varepsilon\}}, $
or equivalently, up to universal constants, of order \[
  \frac{\log K}{Δ_{\min}}+\frac{\log K}{\varepsilon}. \] We give a horizon-free pure-DP algorithm and prove the explicit regret bound \[
  \operatorname{Reg}_T
  \le
  1000 \cdot \left(\frac{\log K}{Δ_{\min}}+\frac{\log K}{\varepsilon}\right) \] for every horizon $T$. The numerical constant is not optimized. The algorithm partitions time into blocks of exponentially increasing size, plays a single action throughout each block, and chooses the next action by an exponential mechanism applied to a data-independent random prefix of the previous block. The random prefix converts block regret into a sum, over all prefix lengths, of softmax selection errors. A single entropy-potential argument controls all privacy-dominated large-gap actions at cost $\log K/\varepsilon$.

</details>


### [186] [Do Deep Networks Forget Initialization? A Forgetting-Time View of Practical Inductive Bias](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29152)
*Mohua Das,Pierfrancesco Beneventano,Shibshankar Dey,Gareth H. McKinkey,Tomaso Poggio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates how much of a neural network's random initialization bias persists after training, introducing "initialization memory" as a metric. Experiments show that low-learning-rate SGD can preserve initialization memory (causing 26.5% test accuracy variation despite >99.5% training accuracy), while Adam-family methods, larger learning rates, and explicit regularization erase it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To measure how much of the random initialization prior survives the training process, and to understand the conditions under which initialization memory is preserved or forgotten.

Method: The authors introduce "initialization memory" - measuring how the validation-selected predictor depends on the scale of random initialization. They perform controlled CIFAR-10 experiments on ResNets, varying initialization scales, learning rates, batch sizes, optimizers (SGD vs Adam-family), and training duration.

Result: (1) Low-learning-rate SGD can interpolate while still remembering initialization (26.5% test accuracy spread across scales on ResNet-9). (2) Extending training to 5,000 epochs doesn't reduce this spread. (3) Adam-family methods largely erase initialization dependence. (4) Larger learning rates with explicit L2 norm control cause forgetting.

Conclusion: The practical inductive bias of a trained network is the architectural prior filtered by the forgetting dynamics of the training pipeline. Regularizers that improve generalization are precisely those that erase initialization memory.

Abstract: Randomly initialized neural networks induce a prior over functions, but the predictor used in practice is produced only after training. We ask how much of this initial bias survives the training pipeline. To make the question measurable, we introduce initialization memory: the dependence of the validation-selected predictor on the scale of the random initialization. We perform controlled CIFAR-10 experiments on ResNets where initialization memory already sharply separates training regimes. Low-learning-rate SGD can interpolate while still remembering its initialization: on ResNet-9 with batch size $b=128$, test accuracy varies by $26.5$ percentage points across initialization scales despite $\ge99.5\%$ training accuracy. This is not undertraining: extending the same low-learning-rate regime to $5{,}000$ epochs leaves the spread essentially unchanged. In contrast, Adam-family methods largely erase the dependence. SGD can also be made to forget when larger learning rates are paired with explicit $L_2$ norm control. We interpret these findings in terms of the time scale of forgetting: gradient-flow-like dynamics can preserve initialization memory, whereas stochastic finite-step effects, explicit norm decay, and adaptive preconditioning erase it on scales governed by the size of explicit or implicit regularization. The practical inductive bias of a trained network is therefore not the architectural prior alone, but the architectural prior after being filtered by the forgetting dynamics of the training pipeline; and the same regularizers that improve generalization are precisely those that erase memory of initialization.

</details>


### [187] [Unveiling Multi-regime Patterns in SciML: Distinct Failure Modes and Regime-specific Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29153)
*Yuxin Wang,Yuanzhe Hu,Xiaokun Zhong,Xiaopeng Wang,Haiquan Lu,Tianyu Pang,Michael W. Mahoney,Yujun Yan,Pu Ren,Yaoqing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies that neural networks in scientific machine learning (SciML) exhibit distinct training regimes depending on hyperparameter settings, revealing a consistent three-regime structure across various models. The authors propose a regime-aware diagnostic framework to analyze performance, training dynamics, and loss-landscape geometry, finding that optimization effectiveness is regime-specific and that SciML models can have fine-grained failure modes challenging standard interpretations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and characterize the multi-regime behavior of SciML models (PINNs, neural operators, neural ODEs) under different hyperparameter settings, moving beyond single-regime analyses to provide a unified perspective on failure modes and improve model robustness.

Method: A regime-aware diagnostic framework jointly analyzing performance, training dynamics, and loss-landscape geometry across different hyperparameter regimes, validated on multiple SciML architectures with various constraint enforcements and optimizer designs.

Result: (i) A consistent three-regime structure emerges across many standard SciML models; (ii) no single optimization method works best across all regimes; (iii) SciML models exhibit fine-grained failure modes that challenge conventional interpretations of standard loss-landscape metrics.

Conclusion: The findings enable a unified, task-oblivious perspective on failure modes in SciML and provide regime-aware guidance for improving model robustness.

Abstract: Neural networks trained under different hyperparameter settings can fall into distinct training "regimes," with consistent behavior within regimes and qualitative differences across regimes. In this paper, we study such multi-regime behavior in scientific machine learning (SciML) models through a regime-aware diagnostic framework that jointly analyzes performance, training dynamics, and loss-landscape geometry. We identify three key findings: (i) a consistent three-regime structure emerges across many standard SciML models, different constraint enforcements, and various optimizer designs; (ii) optimization effectiveness is regime-specific, with no single method performing well across all regimes; and (iii) SciML models can exhibit fine-grained failure modes that can challenge conventional interpretations of standard loss-landscape metrics. Our results provide an approach to establish a unified, task-oblivious perspective on failure modes in SciML and to inform regime-aware guidance for improving robustness. We validate these findings across widely-used SciML models, including physics-informed neural networks, neural operators, and neural ordinary differential equations, on benchmarks spanning representative ordinary and partial differential equations.

</details>


### [188] [RUBRIC-ARROW: Alternating Pointwise Rubric Reward Modeling for LLM Post-training in Non-verifiable Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29156)
*Haoxiang Jiang,Zihan Dong,Tianci Liu,Wanying Wang,Ran Xu,Tony Yu,Linjun Zhang,Haoyu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RUBRIC-ARROW jointly trains a rubric generator and rubric-conditioned judge using pairwise preference data and alternating GRPO, achieving competitive reward modeling accuracy and downstream policy gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pointwise reward modeling struggles with absolute scoring in subjective, non-verifiable settings; existing rubric-based methods rely on frontier LLMs and suffer from ties due to hard Boolean aggregation.

Method: An alternating framework that jointly trains a rubric generator and a rubric-conditioned judge. Uses probability-based scoring to reduce ties, phase-specific preference-based rewards, and alternating GRPO scheme, trained solely on pairwise preference data during the RL stage.

Result: Achieves competitive reward-modeling accuracy and yields consistent gains for downstream policy post-training.

Conclusion: RUBRIC-ARROW effectively addresses the tie problem and reliance on frontier LLMs in rubric-based evaluation, offering a practical alternative for pointwise reward modeling in subjective settings.

Abstract: Pointwise reward modeling offers critical signals for LLM post-training, yet struggles with absolute scoring in subjective, non-verifiable settings. Rubric-based methods address this by decomposing evaluation into explicit criteria, but existing approaches typically depend on frontier LLMs and suffer from ties caused by hard Boolean aggregation. We present RUBRIC-ARROW, an alternating framework that jointly trains a rubric generator and a rubric-conditioned judge, with its RL stage using only pairwise preference data. Our method couples a probability-based scoring rule that reduces ties with phase-specific preference-based rewards and an alternating GRPO scheme that together train the pointwise evaluator. Extensive experiments show that RUBRIC-ARROW achieves competitive reward-modeling accuracy and yields consistent gains for downstream policy post-training.

</details>


### [189] [Parallax: Parameterized Local Linear Attention for Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29157)
*Yifei Zuo,Dhruv Pai,Zhichen Zeng,Alec Dewulf,Shuming Hu,Zhaoran Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Parallax is a parameterized Local Linear Attention mechanism that scales to LLMs by replacing softmax attention's local constant estimate with a local linear estimate, achieving Pareto improvements in perplexity and downstream performance across 0.6B and 1.7B pretraining scales, with hardware-efficient kernels matching FlashAttention 2/3.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Softmax attention relies on a local constant estimate, which has suboptimal bias-variance tradeoffs for associative memory. Prior Local Linear Attention (LLA) was not scalable for LLM pretraining due to computational and numerical stability issues.

Method: Parallax eliminates the numerical solver in LLA, learns an extra query-like projector to probe the KV covariance, and introduces a hardware-aware algorithm that increases arithmetic intensity over FlashAttention. It is placed within a family of attention mechanisms connected by bandwidth, probe construction, and affine structure.

Result: Parallax achieves consistent perplexity improvements at 0.6B and 1.7B scales that transfer to downstream benchmarks, with gains under both parameter-matched and compute-matched controls (Pareto improvement). The decode kernel matches or outperforms FlashAttention 2/3. Muon optimizer unlocks Parallax's capacity.

Conclusion: Parallax demonstrates the first empirical evidence of strong architecture-optimizer codesign for attention mechanisms, showing that upgrading from local constant to local linear estimation is viable and beneficial for LLM pretraining at scale.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have become the central paradigm in artificial intelligence, yet the core computational primitive of attention has remained structurally unchanged. Local Linear Attention (LLA) is an attention mechanism derived from nonparametric statistics in the test-time regression framework. In contrast to prior research on efficient attention variants, LLA upgrades the local constant estimate in softmax attention to a local linear estimate, yielding provably superior bias-variance tradeoffs for associative memory. However, LLA has not been scaled in LLM pretraining due to computational and numerical stability concerns. We introduce Parallax, a parameterized Local Linear Attention that is scalable for LLMs. Parallax eliminates the numerical solver in LLA and learns an extra query-like projector that probes the KV covariance. We place Parallax within a family of attention mechanisms connected by the bandwidth, the probe construction and the affine structure. We propose a hardware-aware algorithm that increases the arithmetic intensity over FlashAttention, shifting attention into a more compute bound regime. Our prototype decode kernel matches or outperforms FlashAttention 2/3 across diverse batch sizes and context lengths. We pretrain Parallax at 0.6B and 1.7B scales and find consistent perplexity improvements throughout pretraining with gains that transfer to downstream benchmarks. The advantage persists under both parameter-matched and compute-matched controls, demonstrating a Pareto improvement. We perform careful pretraining ablations and identify a novel phenomenon whereby Muon unlocks the capacity of Parallax. To our knowledge, this is the first empirical demonstration of strong architecture-optimizer codesign for attention mechanisms in the architecture research literature.

</details>


### [190] [PROTOCOL: Late Interaction Retrieval for Protein Homolog Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29158)
*Gabrielle Cohn,Rohan Gumaste,Minh Hoang,Vihan Lakshman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProtoCol uses ColBERT-style late interaction with residue-level protein embeddings to improve remote homology search in the "twilight zone," outperforming pooled PLM, alignment-based, and single-vector baselines on SCOPe and Pfam benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protein homology search in the "twilight zone" (weak sequence similarity) remains challenging because classical alignment methods lose sensitivity and prior protein embedding pipelines pool representations into single vectors, obscuring local motifs, domains, or conserved residues critical for detecting remote homology.

Method: ProtoCol represents proteins as sets of residue embeddings, encodes them independently (keeping candidates pre-computable), and uses ColBERT-style late interaction with MaxSim over residue embeddings to score candidate matches.

Result: On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines for remote homology search.

Conclusion: Late interaction (residue-level comparison) serves as an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search, offering better sensitivity than pooled representations or classical alignment methods.

Abstract: Protein homology search underlies function annotation, structure prediction, and evolutionary analysis, but remains challenging in the "twilight zone," where global sequence similarity is weak and classical alignment methods lose sensitivity. Protein language models provide context-aware representations that could improve alignment sensitivity in this regime. However, prior protein embedding-based retrieval pipelines often pool these representations into a single vector, potentially obscuring local motifs, domains, or conserved residues that reveal remote homology. We introduce ProtoCol, a model which represents proteins as sets of residue embeddings and uses ColBERT-style late interaction to test whether residue-level comparison improves homolog retrieval. ProtoCol encodes proteins independently, keeps candidate representations pre-computable, and scores candidates with MaxSim over residue embeddings. On SCOPe superfamily and Pfam clan benchmarks, ProtoCol outperforms sequence-composition, alignment-based, pooled PLM, and trained single-vector baselines, supporting late interaction as an effective retrieval layer for remote homology search.

</details>


### [191] [Evolutionary Refinement of Generative Graph Topologies: A Hybrid WGAN-GA Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29161)
*James Sargant,Seyedeh Ava Razi Razavi,Renata Dividino,Sheridan Houghten*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper improves GAN-based graph generation by applying a Genetic Algorithm to refine edges, reducing structural deviations (e.g., degree/spectral distributions) and lowering combined MMD compared to baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GAN-based graph generators still produce noticeable structural deviations (degree/spectral distributions) from real graphs, limiting their realism and utility.

Method: Start with a GAN-based graph generator (generator produces node features + connectivity; GNN critic evaluates realism/class consistency). Then apply a Genetic Algorithm to refine the edges of generated graphs, guiding them toward real data while preserving diversity.

Result: GA refinement consistently lowers combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) compared to the base GAN model, producing graphs that better match real structural patterns.

Conclusion: Evolutionary refinement (GA) is an effective and flexible post-processing method to correct residual structural deviations in GAN-based graph generators, improving suitability for realistic graph synthesis and data augmentation.

Abstract: Generating realistic graph-structured data is challenging due to discrete connectivity, varying graph sizes, and class-specific structural patterns. Recent Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN)-based graph generation methods improve edge modelling by learning connectivity and matching class-specific density distributions. However these models still exhibit noticeable deviations such as in degree and spectral distribution when compared to real graphs, indicating that important structural properties are not fully preserved. This work aims to reduce these deviations by refining the graphs produced by an existing GAN-based graph generator framework with a Genetic Algorithm (GA). In the GAN framework, the generator produces both node features and connectivity patterns, while a GNN-based critic evaluates graph realism and class consistency to ensure global structural and class alignment. Building on this foundation, we apply a GA to refine the edges of generated graphs. The refinement process guides synthetic graphs toward closer agreement with real data, while preserving diversity and novelty. Experimental results show that the GA refinement consistently lowers combined Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) compared to the base model, leading to graphs that more closely match real structural patterns. This demonstrates that evolutionary refinement is an effective and flexible way to correct residual structural deviations in GAN-based graph generators, improving their suitability for realistic graph synthesis and data augmentation.

</details>


### [192] [Probabilistic bias adjustment of seasonal forecasts using generative machine learning: A case study of Arctic sea ice predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29172)
*Parsa Gooya,Reinel Sospedra-Alfonso*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper improves a cVAE-based probabilistic post-processing framework for seasonal Arctic sea ice forecasts by replacing the Gaussian decoder with a generator, using CRPS instead of MSE loss, and leveraging higher-resolution target data to reduce blurriness and improve sharpness, calibration, and spectral power.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Seasonal climate forecasts from ensembles suffer from drift, biases, and errors that grow with lead time. Standard cVAE post-processing produces blurry, low-resolution outputs. The authors aim to address this loss of fine-scale energy and improve the sharpness and spectral power of bias-adjusted predictions.

Method: Replace the Gaussian-parameterized decoder in the cVAE with a generator; use Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) instead of Mean Square Error (MSE) as the objective function; employ a higher-resolution target dataset compared to the raw forecast.

Result: Adjusted forecasts are better calibrated, more consistent with observational distribution, exhibit smaller errors than benchmarks, enhance resolution of raw forecasts, and improve sharpness and spectral power relative to the standard cVAE.

Conclusion: The proposed modifications to the cVAE framework effectively overcome the blurriness problem while maintaining calibration and bias correction benefits, yielding sharper, more realistic seasonal sea ice predictions.

Abstract: Seasonal climate predictions support planning and risk management by offering early information of the most likely-to-occur climate conditions in the coming months, and associated uncertainties. Ensemble forecasts enable this by simulating many plausible outcomes, allowing predictions to be expressed as usable probabilities. Large ensembles and high-resolution forecasts strengthen this guidance by better sampling uncertainty and capturing finer-scale processes but come with significant computational cost. Moreover, forecast ensembles drift and exhibit systematic biases and spatio-temporal errors that grow with lead time, requiring careful post-processing and calibration.
