A probabilistic post-processing framework based on conditional Variational Autoencoders (cVAEs) was developed at the Canadian Center for Climate Modeling and Analysis to generate large ensembles of bias adjusted seasonal predictions of Arctic sea ice. The generative model was designed to learn the observational distribution conditioned on the biased model prediction. This enables generation of arbitrarily large ensembles of well-calibrated, bias corrected forecasts with improved skill. Here, we extend this framework to address the loss of fine-scale energy and the characteristic blurriness in predictions, a known limitation of standard cVAEs. Specifically, we employ a generator in place of the Gaussian parametrized decoder in the cVAE and use Continuous Ranked Probability Score in the objective function instead of the Mean Square Error. We further use a higher resolution target dataset compared to the raw forecast. We show that the adjusted forecasts are better calibrated, more consistent with the observational distribution, and exhibit smaller errors than benchmark predictions, while also enhancing the resolution of the raw forecasts and improving sharpness and spectral power relative to the standard cVAE.

</details>


### [193] [TIMEGATE: Sustainable Time-Boxed Promotion Gates for Continual ML Adaptation Under Resource Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29183)
*Abhijit Chakrabroty,Suddhasvatta Das,Kevin A. Gary,Yash Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TIMEGATE is a policy layer that budgets time, labeling, training, and evaluation for continual ML adaptation, achieving significant compute savings (up to 89% less energy) with no accuracy loss across tabular and LLM tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As ML systems undergo continual retraining, each cycle consumes compute, annotation, and energy resources, motivating the need for a management policy to budget these costs efficiently.

Method: TIMEGATE introduces a policy layer that emits a metric-availability signal M to decide between partial vs. full evaluation, based on budgeting time, labeling, training, and evaluation cycles.

Result: (i) Labeling outperforms training by 2.3x on Adult tabular; (ii) transfers to LLaMA-3.1-8B + QLoRA on SST-2 (accuracy 0.80→0.96; M=1 in 35/36 runs); (iii) M is informative with 28-cell sensitivity showing drop to 0.81 at tight thresholds; (iv) 100-cycle simulation achieves 66% evaluation-compute savings with no silent mis-promotions; (v) 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA uses 89% less wall-clock and energy on a single H200 (ratios agree to 0.2%).

Conclusion: TIMEGATE effectively manages adaptation budgets, dramatically reducing compute and energy costs while maintaining accuracy, and demonstrates strong transferability from tabular to large language model settings.

Abstract: As machine learning(ML) systems evolve to continual adaptation, each re-training cycle uses compute, annotation, and energy. We introduce TIMEGATE, a policy layer managing adaptation by budgeting time, labeling, training, and evaluation. TIMEGATE emits a metric-availability signal M for partial vs. full-evaluation decisions. We validate: (i) labeling outperforms training by 2.3x on Adult tabular; (ii) it transfers to LLaMA-3.1-8B + QLoRA on SST-2 (accuracy 0.80 to 0.96; M =1 in 35/36 runs); (iii) M is informative, 28-cell sensitivity shows M drops to 0.81 at tight thresholds; (iv) 100-cycle simulation achieves 66% evaluation-compute savings with no silent mis-promotions; (v) 10%-slice evaluation on LLaMA uses 89% less wall-clock and energy on a single H200 (ratios agree to 0.2%).

</details>


### [194] [Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression: Scientific Discovery via LLM-Driven Equation Search with Granular Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29184)
*Evgeny S. Saveliev,Samuel Holt,Nabeel Seedat,David L. Bentley,Jim Weatherall,Mihaela van der Schaar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IGSR enhances symbolic regression by using LLMs to generate candidate terms evaluated via granular influence scores within a Monte Carlo Tree Search, enabling more efficient equation discovery and demonstrated on benchmarks and a real biological case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based symbolic regression methods rely on coarse scalar metrics (e.g., global MSE) that fail to pinpoint which parts of an equation cause errors, leading to inefficient search and poor feedback.

Method: IGSR iteratively combines LLM-generated candidate basis functions with influence scores Δⱼ that quantify each term's marginal contribution, using these scores for pruning within a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to balance exploration and exploitation.

Result: Outperforms baselines on LLM-SRBench, PKPD models, epidemiology simulations, and real genomic data; discovered a novel methylation-Pol II pausing relationship validated by wet-lab experiments.

Conclusion: Influence-guided feedback enables LLMs to perform more effective symbolic regression, with demonstrated potential for genuine scientific discovery validated experimentally.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising avenue for scientific discovery, yet their application to symbolic regression is often constrained by inefficient search strategies and coarse feedback signals. Current methods typically guide LLMs using scalar metrics (e.g., global Mean Squared Error), which fail to identify which components of a proposed equation are driving performance or causing error. We introduce \textit{Influence-Guided Symbolic Regression} (IGSR), a method that frames equation discovery as an iterative two-step process combining diverse term generation with rigorous selection: an LLM generates candidate basis functions $ψ_j(\mathbf{x})$ for a linear model, which are then evaluated using granular influence scores $Δ_j$. These scores quantify each term's marginal contribution to generalization accuracy, enabling an influence-guided pruning process that systematically refines the model structure. Integrating this mechanism into a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) enables navigating the combinatorial search space while balancing exploration of novel functional forms with exploitation of high-influence components. We demonstrate IGSR's effectiveness on a diverse suite of benchmarks, including LLM-SRBench, pharmacological PKPD models, an epidemiological simulation, and real-world genomic data. Notably, we validate the framework's capacity for genuine discovery in a case study using a high-dimensional biological dataset, in which IGSR identified a novel relationship between DNA methylation and RNA Polymerase II pausing; a hypothesis that was subsequently supported via wet-lab experimentation.

</details>


### [195] [When RL Suppresses Its Own Vocabulary: Recovering Reasoning Diversity in Puzzle-to-Math Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29190)
*Mayug Maniparambil,Arjun Karuvally,Terrence Sejnowski,Fergal Reid*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR training on constraint-satisfaction puzzles improves LLM math reasoning (from 16% to 36% on OlymMATH-Hard). The paper introduces a primitive-level framework to trace how SFT builds a reasoning vocabulary, RL composes primitives into longer verify chains, and a novelty bonus restores suppressed exploratory primitives like hypothesize and backtrack.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why and under what conditions RLVR-based reasoning transfer across domains (puzzles to math) occurs, and to analyze the evolution of reasoning primitives during post-training.

Method: Introduced a reasoning primitive-level framework with a 9-class span classifier and motif extraction to segment chain-of-thought traces. Used only constraint-satisfaction puzzles for SFT and RL (GSPO), plus a novelty bonus (perplexity-based diversity reward) to restore exploratory primitives.

Result: Puzzle SFT yields +7pp pass@32 on OlymMATH-Hard; vanilla GSPO adds +6pp; novelty bonus adds another +7pp. Overall capability improves from 16.0% to 36.0% on hard math without any math in post-training data.

Conclusion: Cross-domain reasoning transfer is achievable through RLVR on non-math puzzles, and is driven by the acquisition, composition, and preservation of reasoning primitives. The novelty bonus effectively counters RL-induced suppression of exploratory reasoning behaviors.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning using verifiable rewards (RLVR) improves LLM reasoning, but the conditions under which it transfers across domains -- and why it does so -- remain under-explored. We study cross-domain transfer in a 7B model whose SFT and RL post-training stages use only constraint-satisfaction puzzles, with no mathematics problems in the post-training data. To analyze how transfer emerges, we introduce a reasoning primitive-level framework that combines a 9-class span classifier with motif extraction, allowing us to segment chain-of-thought traces into primitive motifs and track their evolution across training stages and domains. We find that puzzle SFT induces a reasoning-primitive vocabulary, yielding a $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} gain on OlymMATH-Hard. Vanilla GSPO then composes these primitives into longer compute-verify chains, adding a further $+6$pp. However, this RL stage also suppresses exploratory primitives such as \textit{hypothesize} and \textit{backtrack}. To address this, we introduce a novelty bonus that rewards diverse correct rollouts, using perplexity under the reference model as a signal. This restores recovery primitives during RL and adds a further $+7$pp \texttt{pass@32} relative to vanilla GSPO. Finally, the end-to-end recipe raises the hard-math capability ceiling from $16.0\%$ at the OLMo3-7B-Instruct-SFT base to $36.0\%$, without adding any mathematics problems during the SFT or RL stages.

</details>


### [196] [Stochastic Lifting for Generating Trajectories of Stochastic Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29194)
*Jules Berman,Tobias Blickhan,Benjamin Peherstorfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Stochastic Lifting is a method that uses high-dimensional random labels to model stochastic transitions in physical systems, enabling diverse trajectory generation without mode collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of modeling stochastic physical systems where multiple plausible next states exist from similar current states, and to avoid mode collapse to mean predictions in finite-sample settings.

Method: Attaches independent, high-dimensional random labels to each state transition; fits a transition map from current state + label to next state using regression loss; at inference, samples fresh labels per time step for autoregressive rollouts.

Result: Enables generation of diverse trajectories with a single network evaluation per time step, avoiding collapse to mean prediction.

Conclusion: Stochastic Lifting effectively models smooth stochastic dynamics by leveraging random auxiliary labels, offering a simple yet powerful approach for trajectory generation.

Abstract: Many stochastic physical systems evolve smoothly over time in the sense that the distribution of states changes regularly across time steps. The transition from current state to the next state can often be modeled as the combination of a smooth map and an explicit source of randomness. Stochastic Lifting exploits this structure by attaching an independent, high-dimensional random label to each state transition in the training data and fitting a transition map from the current state and label to the next state using a standard regression loss. The labels act as auxiliary coordinates that let the model represent multiple plausible next states from similar current states, avoiding collapse to a mean prediction in the finite-sample size regime. At inference, fresh labels are sampled at each time step and the learned map is rolled forward autoregressively, generating diverse trajectories with a single network evaluation per time step.

</details>


### [197] [Auditing Training Data in Generative Music Models via Black-Box Membership Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29202)
*Yi Chen Liu,Jiawei Yu,Kexin Cao,Syed Irfan Ali Meerza,Trishika Movva,Jian Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a black-box membership inference method for text-to-music generative models, achieving up to 98.6% accuracy by auditing the semantic alignment between candidate audio and caption-conditioned generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing concerns about data provenance, consent, and training transparency in text-to-music generation, as current models train on large-scale corpora with little disclosure and no practical mechanism to verify training data inclusion.

Method: The approach queries the target model with a caption, measures alignment between candidate audio and generated output in a learned feature space, trains a music auditor on paired examples (track + caption-conditioned generation) from shadow models to classify membership.

Result: Achieves up to 98.6% accuracy across multiple state-of-the-art music generators, with false-positive rate as low as 1.9% and false-negative rate as low as 1.0%.

Conclusion: Reliable training-data auditing is feasible in realistic black-box deployment scenarios without access to model parameters or training metadata.

Abstract: Recent advances in text-to-music generation enable high-fidelity synthesis of structured musical audio, raising growing concerns about data provenance, consent, and training transparency. These models are typically trained on large-scale corpora with little disclosure, leaving no practical mechanism to verify whether a particular audio sample was included in training. In this paper, we investigate black-box membership inference for generative music models, aiming to determine whether a candidate music sample was used during training, given only query access to the deployed system. Our key insight is that training membership induces systematically stronger semantic and structural alignment between a candidate sample and the model's generation conditioned on its caption. We query the target model with the associated caption and measure the relationship between the candidate audio and the generated output in a learned feature space. To capture features that separate members from non-members, we construct paired examples consisting of each track and its caption-conditioned generation from shadow models, and train a music auditor to classify membership. The auditor captures alignment patterns characteristic of training membership and generalizes to unseen target models in a fully black-box setting without access to model parameters or training metadata. Across multiple state-of-the-art music generators, our method achieves up to 98.6% accuracy, with false-positive and false-negative rates as low as 1.9% and 1.0%, demonstrating that reliable training-data auditing is feasible in realistic deployment scenarios.

</details>


### [198] [Inferring the Size of Large Language Models From Popular Text Memorization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29223)
*Ivica Nikolic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a black-box method to infer lower bounds on LLM parameter counts using only text outputs, by measuring how accurately models predict next tokens across varying text fragment lengths from widely-circulated texts. Two complementary methods (pairwise statistical test and PCA-based scaling-law estimator) are validated on open-weight models and applied to closed-weight models, revealing industry parameter scaling strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM developers often withhold parameter counts, obscuring a key reference point for interpreting model capabilities and costs. The authors aim to provide a method to infer model size from text outputs alone, without requiring internal model information.

Method: The method uses popular texts (classical literature, religious texts, etc.) present in pretraining corpora. It measures next-token prediction accuracy across varying fragment lengths as a memorization signal, aggregates this into accuracy profile vectors, and builds two inference methods: a pairwise statistical test for comparing model sizes and a PCA-based scaling-law estimator that maps the signal to parameter counts.

Result: Validated on open-weight models, both methods produce accurate and reliable lower bounds. Applied to closed-weight models, the framework recovers internal product hierarchies and reveals divergent industry scaling strategies—some developers show large generational parameter growth while others operate under strict parameter ceilings.

Conclusion: Hidden design choices in LLMs can be systematically probed even under strict API limitations, and the method reveals clear differences in industry scaling strategies among developers.

Abstract: The parameter counts of the most widely used large language models (LLMs) are often withheld by their developers, leaving model size -- a primary reference point for interpreting capabilities and costs -- largely undisclosed. We propose a black-box method to infer conservative lower bounds on LLM size from generated text outputs alone, requiring nothing beyond the ability to submit text fragments and observe next-token predictions. Our approach is grounded in a key observation: popular, widely-circulated texts -- such as classical literature, religious texts, and foundational documents -- are present in virtually every large-scale pretraining corpus, and how accurately a model predicts the next word across text fragments of varying length is a reliable signal of how much it has memorized them, which in turn is fundamentally limited by its total parameter count. We aggregate this memorization signal across a diverse corpus of texts and fragment lengths into a single accuracy profile vector per model, and build two complementary inference methods on top of it: a pairwise statistical test that determines which of two models is larger, and a scaling-law estimator that extracts a one-dimensional latent index from these vectors via Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to map the aggregated signal to a parameter count. Validated on a broad set of open-weight models, both methods produce accurate and reliable lower bounds. When applied to popular closed-weight models, our framework recovers internal product hierarchies and reveals a clear divergence in industry scaling strategies: while some developers yield significantly higher bounds indicative of large generational parameter growth, others operate under strict parameter ceilings, demonstrating that hidden design choices can be systematically probed even under strict API limitations.

</details>


### [199] [Traditional machine learning vs. deep learning from dynamic graph representations of proteins' 3D folds in the task of protein structure classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29228)
*Aydin Wells,Francis A. Gatsi,Aaron Striegel,Tijana Milenković*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper compares traditional ML with deep learning for protein structure classification using dynamic protein structure networks, finding ML matches DL accuracy but is over 10x faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether automatic deep learning from dynamic PSNs improves upon traditional machine learning approaches for protein structure classification.

Method: Comparison of traditional ML (manual features + off-the-shelf classifier) vs. DL on 72 datasets (~44,000 dynamic PSNs labeled with CATH/SCOPe classes).

Result: Traditional ML and DL achieve near-tied accuracy on most datasets, but DL is on average 10+ times slower.

Conclusion: For dynamic PSN-based PSC, traditional ML is preferred over DL due to comparable accuracy and significantly faster speed.

Abstract: Protein structure classification (PSC) uses supervised learning to predict a protein's CATH/SCOP(e) class from the protein's sequence or 3D structural feature(s). We already modeled 3D structures as (static) protein structure networks (PSNs), demonstrating the competitiveness of PSN-based features to sequence or direct (i.e. non-network) 3D structural features in the PSC task. More recently, we demonstrated the power of features extracted from dynamic PSNs over features extracted from static PSNs (and thus by transitivity over sequence and direct 3D structural features) in the same task. That dynamic PSN approach used traditional machine learning (ML), combining manual (pre-engineered) features with an off-the-shelf classifier. Here, we evaluate whether automatic deep learning (DL) from the dynamic PSNs yields improvements. Our evaluation on 72 datasets spanning ~44,000 CATH- or SCOPe-labeled dynamic PSNs reveals that in terms of PSC accuracy, traditional ML and DL are (close to) tied for a large majority of the datasets, while DL is on average 10+ times slower. We are the first to evaluate traditional ML vs. DL in the dynamic PSN-based PSC task.

</details>


### [200] [BlockBatch: Multi-Scale Consensus Decoding for Efficient Diffusion Language Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29233)
*Xiaoyou Wu,Cheng-Jhih Shih,Binfei Ji,Yong Liu,Yingyan,Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BlockBatch is a training-free inference framework that accelerates diffusion language models by executing multiple branches with different block sizes in parallel, using confidence-gated token merging and synchronization to achieve 26.6% fewer denoising steps and 1.33× speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing block-wise dLLM inference faces a granularity trade-off: small blocks preserve local conditioning but need many denoising steps, while large blocks offer more parallelism but risk premature commitments and cache errors. Previous methods use a single block size per request, ignoring complementarity among block sizes.

Method: BlockBatch is a training-free online inference framework that runs multiple block-size branches for the same request in a batched forward pass. It coordinates branches via confidence-gated token merging, leader-based synchronization, and periodic full-sequence refreshes to maintain a globally consistent KV state.

Result: Across 3 representative dLLMs and 4 datasets, BlockBatch reduces denoising NFEs by 26.6% on average and achieves 1.33× average end-to-end speedup over Fast-dLLM while preserving accuracy.

Conclusion: Block-size diversity is a practical and previously underexplored axis for branch-parallel dLLM inference, offering effective acceleration without training.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (dLLMs) generate text by iteratively denoising multiple token positions in parallel, offering an attractive alternative to strictly autoregressive decoding. In practice, however, block-wise dLLM inference exposes a difficult granularity trade-off: small blocks preserve local conditioning but require many denoising steps, whereas large blocks expose more parallelism but can make premature commitments and accumulate cache error. Existing acceleration methods typically choose a single block size per request, leaving the complementarity among block sizes unused. We show that block size itself is a useful branching dimension. Different block sizes induce related but non-identical KV-cache trajectories: branches often share an initial prefix, bifurcate at semantically decisive positions, and later agree on syntactically lightweight tokens. Motivated by this structure, we propose BlockBatch, a training-free online inference framework that executes multiple block-size branches for the same request inside a batched forward pass. BlockBatch coordinates these branches through confidence-gated token merging, leader-based synchronization, and periodic full-sequence refreshes that re-anchor local block updates to a globally consistent KV state. Across 3 representative dLLMs and 4 datasets, BlockBatch reduces denoising NFEs by 26.6\% on average and achieves a 1.33$\times$ average end-to-end speedup over Fast-dLLM while preserving accuracy. These results identify block-size diversity as a practical and previously underexplored axis for branch-parallel dLLM inference.

</details>


### [201] [SigmaMedStat: Temporal Signal Modeling for ICU False Alarm Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29236)
*Arunkumar Ramachandran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SigmaMedStat uses temporal chunking (6×10s segments) with CWT scalograms + EfficientNet-B0 + LSTM to classify ICU alarm trustworthiness, achieving AUC 0.822 vs 0.641 baseline on the PhysioNet 2015 challenge dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Alarm fatigue in ICUs is a severe patient safety issue—72-99% of alarms are clinically irrelevant, leading to staff desensitization and missed true emergencies. A system to filter non-actionable alarms before clinical response is needed.

Method: Each 60s alarm recording is split into six 10s chunks. CWT scalograms are generated per chunk, encoded by a shared EfficientNet-B0, then passed through a two-layer LSTM for sequential modeling. Five-fold stratified cross-validation is used.

Result: Mean AUC 0.822±0.016 (95% CI: [0.790,0.853]), significantly outperforming the static EfficientNet baseline (0.641). Ablations confirm both temporal chunking and multi-channel fusion help. Ventricular Flutter AUC 0.820; Asystole AUC 0.722 (hardest).

Conclusion: Temporal modeling of ICU alarm signals via chunked CWT+LSTM effectively identifies clinically relevant alarms, addresses alarm fatigue, and the framework is open-source for reproducibility and further research.

Abstract: Alarm fatigue in intensive care units (ICUs) is a well documented patient safety crisis. Clinical monitors generate 350 or more alarms per patient per day, out of which 72-99% are clinically irrelevant. Staff desensitization to non-actionable alarms increases the risk of missed true emergencies. This paper presents SigmaMedStat, a machine learning system that evaluates the trustworthiness of physiological alarm signals before clinical action is taken. Four approaches were evaluated on the PhysioNet/Computing in Cardiology Challenge 2015 dataset of 498 four-channel ICU alarm recordings. Primary contribution is a temporal modeling framework that splits each 60 second recording into six consecutive 10-second chunks, and this in turn generates Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT) scalograms per chunk, encodes each chunk with a shared EfficientNet-B0 encoder, and passes the resulting feature sequence to a two-layer Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. Five-fold stratified cross-validation yields a mean AUC of 0.822 +/- 0.016 (95% CI: [0.790,0.853]), compared to 0.641 for a static EfficientNet baseline trained on the full 60-second window. Ablation studies confirm that temporal chunking and multi-channel signal fusion both contribute independently to classification performance. Per-alarm type analysis reveals that Ventricular Flutter is the most accurately classified alarm type (AUC 0.820) while Asystole remains the hardest (AUC 0.722). Error analysis identifies 65 false negatives and 85 high-confidence misclassifications as the primary failure modes. All code and results are publicly available at https://github.com/Arun-K-Ram/sigmamedstat.

</details>


### [202] [KLAS: Using Similarity to Stitch Neural Networks for Improved Accuracy-Efficiency Tradeoffs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29259)
*Debopam Sanyal,Anantharaman Iyer,Alind Khare,Trisha Jain,Akshay Jajoo,Myungjin Lee,Clayton Kerce,Alexey Tumanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KLAS is a framework that automates stitch selection between pretrained models using KL divergence, achieving better accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs (up to 1.21% higher top-1 accuracy or 1.33× FLOPs reduction) compared to heuristic-based stitching approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing stitching approaches rely on heuristics for selecting stitch configurations, leading to suboptimal accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs and limited generalizability across model families.

Method: KLAS uses KL divergence between intermediate representations of pretrained models to automatically identify the most promising binary stitches from O(k²n²) possibilities for k models of depth n.

Result: KLAS achieves up to 1.21% higher ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy at the same computational cost, or maintains accuracy with a 1.33× reduction in FLOPs.

Conclusion: Explicitly capturing and leveraging the similarity (via KL divergence) between pretrained models enables better stitch selection and improves the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff curve at the same finetuning cost as baselines.

Abstract: Given the wide range of deployment targets, flexible model selection is essential for optimizing performance within a given compute budget. Recent work demonstrates that stitching pretrained models within a model family enables cost-effective interpolation of the accuracy-efficiency tradeoff space. Stitching transforms intermediate activations from one pretrained model into another, producing a new interpolated stitched network. Such networks provide a pool of deployment options along the accuracy-efficiency spectrum. However, existing stitching approaches often yield suboptimal tradeoffs and lack generalizability, as they primarily rely on heuristics to select stitch configurations. We argue that constructing improved accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs requires explicitly capturing and leveraging the similarity between pretrained models being stitched. To this end, we introduce KLAS, a novel stitch selection framework that automates and generalizes stitch selection across model families by leveraging KL divergence between intermediate representations. KLAS identifies the most promising binary stitches from the $O(k^2n^2)$ possibilities for $k$ pretrained models of depth $n$. Through comprehensive experiments, we demonstrate that KLAS improves the accuracy-efficiency curve of stitched models at the same finetuning cost as baselines. KLAS achieves up to $1.21\%$ higher ImageNet-1K top-1 accuracy at the same computational cost, or maintains accuracy with a $1.33\times$ reduction in FLOPs.

</details>


### [203] [Robust Frequency-Calibrated Virtual EEG Channel Generation from Four Frontal Electrodes for Wearable EEG Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29263)
*Minghao Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FAVC-Net is a compact neural network that generates 13 unmeasured EEG channels from just 4 frontal electrodes, achieving significant spectral improvements over baselines while being robust to noisy wearable inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-channel wearable EEG (only 4 frontal electrodes) provides sparse and spatially biased views of distributed scalp activity, limiting its utility for long-term monitoring.

Method: FAVC-Net combines multi-scale source encoding, source-state embeddings, target-conditioned signed source-block mixing, GATv2 attention refinement, attention-consistent skip fusion, and weak Welch PSD calibration to generate 13 unmeasured channels from Fp1, Fp2, F7, and F8.

Result: On PRED+CT dataset, FAVC-Net achieved best joint waveform-spectral operating point; log-spectral distance reduced by 30.09% and PSD KL divergence by 37.98% vs. strongest baseline; preserved spectral fidelity under wearable-like perturbations.

Conclusion: Virtual EEG channel generation should be treated as a dual-domain (time+frequency) augmentation problem, with generated posterior/parietal channels interpreted as frequency-calibrated representations rather than independent physical recordings.

Abstract: Low-channel wearable electroencephalography (EEG) is attractive for long-term monitoring, but four frontal electrodes provide only a sparse and spatially biased view of distributed scalp activity. We present FAVC-Net, a compact frequency-calibrated virtual-channel network that generates 13 unmeasured EEG channels from Fp1, Fp2, F7, and F8. The model combines shared multi-scale source encoding, source-state embeddings, target-conditioned signed source-block mixing, GATv2-based attention refinement, attention-consistent skip fusion, and weak Welch power spectral density calibration. Rather than treating sparse-to-dense EEG generation as a purely waveform-matching task, the framework jointly emphasizes amplitude fidelity, spectral allocation, channel-frequency texture, and robustness to corrupted wearable inputs. On the PRED+CT dataset, FAVC-Net achieved the best joint waveform-spectral operating point among neural and interpolation baselines. Its time-domain gains were modest, whereas log-spectral distance and PSD KL divergence were reduced by 30.09% and 37.98% relative to the strongest non-FAVC comparator. Under wearable-like source perturbations, the model preserved spectral fidelity and resisted spectral collapse. These results support virtual EEG channel generation as a dual-domain augmentation problem, while emphasizing that generated posterior and parietal channels should be interpreted as frequency-calibrated representations derived from sparse frontal measurements rather than as independent physical recordings.

</details>


### [204] [Causal Label Recovery in Payment Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29272)
*Gaurav Dhama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes the Sequential Triply Robust (STR) estimator that achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound for fraud detection despite four systematic label biases (authorization gate, issuer reporting gate, delay gate, and label corruption), enabling training on much fresher data by decoupling model freshness from chargeback maturity cycles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fraud detection models train on chargeback labels that suffer from four systematic impairments: authorization filtering, issuer under-reporting, temporal delay, and label corruption (first-party fraud or misclassification). A companion paper proved these impose a minimax lower bound. This paper asks whether that bound can be achieved in practice.

Method: Formalizes the observation pipeline as a sequential missing-data problem with three propensity stages plus a corruption layer. Constructs the STR estimator which: (1) corrects for all four impairments simultaneously via sequential triple robustness; (2) uses noise-rate-adjusted pseudo-labels for corruption correction; (3) applies empirical Bayes shrinkage to stabilize weights for small issuers; (4) provides plug-in variance estimation and Bernstein concentration inequalities for finite-sample guarantees.

Result: STR achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound (no estimator can have lower asymptotic variance). It is sequentially triply robust (at each gate, consistency requires correct specification of either propensity model or outcome regression). Derives optimal training delay balancing label quality and model staleness. STR permits training on days-old rather than months-old data, and dominates naive chargeback training in MSE for any sample size.

Conclusion: The minimax lower bound from the companion paper is achievable. The STR estimator provides a practical, theoretically optimal solution for fraud detection under systematic label impairments, with concrete operational benefits including dramatically reduced training latency and provable superiority over standard approaches.

Abstract: Fraud detection models in payment networks train on chargeback labels that are systematically biased. Every label must survive three sequential gates: authorization (declined transactions generate no labels), issuer reporting (unreported fraud is invisible), and delay (pending chargebacks are missing at training time). Labels that do arrive may be corrupted by first-party misuse or issuer misclassification. A companion paper [arXiv:2605.27557] proved that these four impairments impose a minimax lower bound on detection performance. This paper asks: can that bound be achieved?
  We formalize the observation pipeline as a sequential missing-data problem with three propensity stages and a corruption layer, and construct the Sequential Triply Robust (STR) estimator. The STR corrects for all four impairments simultaneously and achieves the semiparametric efficiency bound -- no estimator can have lower asymptotic variance. It is sequentially triply robust: at each gate, consistency requires only that either the propensity model or the outcome regression is correctly specified, not both. We provide corruption correction via noise-rate-adjusted pseudo-labels, empirical Bayes shrinkage to stabilize inverse-propensity weights for small issuers, a plug-in variance estimator yielding valid confidence intervals, and a Bernstein concentration inequality for finite-sample guarantees.
  On the operational side, we derive the optimal training delay -- the maturity window that minimizes the sum of label-quality loss and model staleness -- and prove that the STR permits training on data that is days old rather than months old, decoupling model freshness from the chargeback maturity cycle. The STR provably dominates naive chargeback-based training in mean squared error for any sample size.

</details>


### [205] [A Theoretical and Experimental Study of a Novel Adaptive Learning Algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29273)
*Sakshi Kumari,Shyam Kumar M,Sushmitha P*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper reviews adaptive optimizers (Adam, AMSGrad) and proposes C-Adam, a new optimizer using a line-of-sight approach with convergence guarantees and experimental validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive learning rate optimizers like Adam are widely used but do not guarantee convergence. AMSGrad addressed non-convergence but has limitations. The authors aim to propose a better optimizer with guaranteed convergence.

Method: C-Adam optimizer based on the line of sight approach, with a theoretical convergence proof provided.

Result: Validated through real-life based numerical experiments showing improved performance over Adam and AMSGrad.

Conclusion: C-Adam achieves convergence guarantees while maintaining efficiency, addressing key limitations of existing adaptive optimizers.

Abstract: A crucial component of machine learning algorithms is minimizing loss functions with less computational cost and less oscillations. While adaptive learning rate-based optimizers have been widely used for real-world tasks, they do not guarantee convergence, which is why AMSGrad was later introduced to investigate the non-convergence behaviour of Adam. In this paper, popular adaptive optimization methods like Adam and AMSGrad are critically reviewed with an emphasis on their fundamental design concepts. To address limitations of the above mentioned optimizers, a new optimizer variant, C-Adam, is proposed based on the line of sight approach. A theoretical proof for convergence is also provided and the optimizer is validated through a number of real-life based numerical experiments.

</details>


### [206] [LoopFM: Learning frOm HistOrical RePresentations of Foundation Model for Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29280)
*Shali Jiang,Hua Zheng,Boyang Liu,Laming Chen,Kenny Lov,Chuanqi Xu,Lisang Ding,Qinghai Zhou,Can Cui,Xiaolong Liu,Xiaoyi Liu,Yasmine Badr,Xin Xu,Jiyan Yang,Ellie Dingqiao Wen,Gerard Jonathan Mugisha Akkerhuis,Chenxiao Guan,Rong Jin,Ruichao Qiu,Xian Chen,Shifu Xu,Zhehui Zhou,Ping Chen,Rui Yang,Haicheng Chen,Xiangge Meng,Song Zhou,Dharak Kharod,Shuyu Xu,Qiang Jin,Qiao Yang,Wankun Zhu,Qin Huang,Yuzhen Huang,Darren Liu,Parish Aggarwal,Hui Zhou,Erzhuo Wang,Shuo Chang,Xiaorui Gan,Wenlin Chen,Santanu Kolay,Huayu Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoopFM improves knowledge distillation by transferring rich intermediate representations from large foundation models to small vertical models as structured input features, doubling transfer ratio and achieving significant conversion improvements in industrial-scale systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional KD transfers only a single scalar, causing diminishing transfer ratio as rich intermediate knowledge from large FMs is lost; there is a need for a high-bandwidth transfer channel.

Method: LoopFM uses historical FM intermediate embeddings as structured input features for downstream VMs, avoiding real-time FM inference and architectural coupling, with theoretical gain decomposition and transfer-ratio analysis.

Result: 6%+ AUC improvement on TaobaoAd; in industrial systems (billions of examples, trillion-parameter FMs), LoopFM doubles transfer ratio over KD, achieving +0.5%, +1.03%, and +1.22% conversion improvements.

Conclusion: LoopFM effectively complements KD by enabling high-bandwidth knowledge transfer from FMs to VMs, significantly improving real-world conversion metrics.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers a single scalar prediction from a large foundation model (FM) to compact vertical models (VMs), suffering from diminishing transfer ratio -- the fraction of FM improvement captured by the VM -- as a single scalar cannot convey the rich intermediate knowledge that larger FMs learn. To address this bottleneck, we propose LoopFM (Learning frOm HistOrical ReP*resentations of FM), a framework that opens a high-bandwidth transfer channel by structuring FM intermediate embeddings as input features (e.g., user history sequence) for downstream VMs, without requiring real-time FM inference at serving and architectural coupling between FM and VM. We provide a theoretical framework for LoopFM with a gain decomposition and transfer-ratio analysis. On three public benchmarks, LoopFM demonstrates strong AUC improvements (e.g., 6\%+ on TaobaoAd) and complementary knowledge transfer capability with KD. On industrial-scale systems (billions of examples, trillion-parameter FMs), LoopFM approximately doubles the knowledge transfer ratio on top of KD, delivering a +0.5\% conversion improvement in Y1H1, and a +1.03\% and +1.22\% conversion improvement from two individual launches respectively in Y1H2.

</details>


### [207] [Do Physics Foundation Models Learn Generalizable Physics? A Bias-Aware Benchmark Across Physical Regimes and Distribution Shifts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29283)
*Mengdi Chu,Yang Liu,Ayan Biswas,Han-Wei Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Current physics foundation models exhibit conditional (not universal) generalization, failing under distribution shifts despite pretraining and scaling, necessitating better transferable physical knowledge mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations collapse performance into single average scores under fixed training distributions, obscuring whether models learn generalizable physical dynamics or merely perform well under specific settings.

Method: Construct a benchmark with 8 physical dynamics, 3 training-data mixtures, 25 test regimes (in-distribution, shift, OOD), evaluating 5 architectures × 4 variants (scratch + 3 pretrained sizes) → 60,000 measurements.

Result: Physics foundation models act as conditional generalists; performance depends on physical regime, temporal scale, initial conditions, pretraining, model size, and architecture. Training data expansion, pretraining, and scaling cannot reliably remove ability biases.

Conclusion: Progress requires moving beyond scaling models/data toward learning mechanisms that capture transferable physical knowledge across regimes, temporal scales, and distribution shifts.

Abstract: Recent physics foundation models claim general spatiotemporal forecasting ability, yet their evaluations often collapse performance into a single average score under a fixed training distribution. This makes it difficult to determine whether a model has learned generalizable physical dynamics or only performs well under particular settings. We construct a benchmark with 8 physical dynamics, 3 training-data mixtures, and 25 test regimes induced by dynamic-scale and initial-condition complexity shifts, covering in-distribution, distribution-shift, and out-of-distribution settings. We evaluate five physics foundation model architectures and four model variants per architecture (scratch and three pretrained sizes), resulting in 60,000 measurements. Our results show that current physics foundation models behave as conditional rather than universal generalists: their generality depends on the physical regime, temporal scale, initial-condition setting, pretraining, model size, and architecture. Improving the training data distribution only partially mitigates this limitation. Pretraining and scaling are also unable to reliably remove their ability biases. We argue that improving physics foundation models requires moving beyond scaling models or expanding data, toward learning mechanisms that better capture transferable physical knowledge across regimes, temporal scales, and distribution shifts.

</details>


### [208] [NeuroEdge: Real-Time Hand Gesture Recognition with High-Density EMG Using Deep Learning at the Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29326)
*Peter Chudinov,Zhenyu Lin,Jay Motamarry,Srihita Panati,Xiaorong Zhang,Zhuwei Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NeuroEdge is a real-time HD-EMG gesture recognition system deployed entirely on resource-constrained microcontrollers (ESP32 + Sony Spresense), achieving 90% accuracy across 7 hand gestures with 83ms latency using a lightweight 1D-CNN and pipelined DMA/SPI communication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning-based HD-EMG gesture recognition achieves high accuracy but cannot be deployed on embedded hardware due to computational and memory constraints, limiting real-world NMI applications like prosthetics and rehabilitation.

Method: NeuroEdge consists of two custom modules: (1) HD-EMG StreamBridge (wireless interface streaming raw data from Quattrocento amplifier to ESP32), and (2) EdgeDL Inference Engine (lightweight 1D CNN running on Sony Spresense microcontroller). Data streaming and inference are pipelined using DMA for data transfer and SPI burst communication between ESP32 and Spresense.

Result: The system achieves 90% real-time classification accuracy across 7 hand gestures with 83ms average total latency, using 192 HD-EMG channels recorded from the forearm.

Conclusion: NeuroEdge demonstrates the feasibility of deploying complex HD-EMG-based gesture recognition on microcontroller-based edge devices, bridging high-resolution biosignal acquisition with deep learning-based embedded inference for next-generation neural-machine interfaces.

Abstract: High-density electromyography (HD-EMG) has emerged as a powerful modality for decoding fine-grained neuromuscular activity, enabling real-time neural-machine interfaces (NMIs) for applications such as prosthetic control, rehabilitation, and augmented interaction. While deep learning approaches such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs)have demonstrated high classification accuracy for EMG-based gesture recognition, their deployment on embedded hardware remains a major challenge due to computational and memory constraints. This paper presents NeuroEdge, a real-time HD EMG-based NMI system that performs gesture recognition entirely on resource-constrained microcontrollers. The system features two custom-designed modules: the HD-EMG StreamBridge, a wireless communication interface that streams raw HD-EMG data from a Quattrocento amplifier to an ESP32 microcontroller; and the EdgeDL Inference Engine, a lightweight deep learning framework executing on a Sony Spresense microcontroller. A compact 1-dimensional CNN optimized for embedded inference processes, sliding windows of EMG data in real time. Data streaming and inference are pipelined and synchronized through an architecture that utilizes Direct Memory Access (DMA) for data transfer and Serial Peripheral Interface (SPI) burst communication between the ESP32 and Spresense, ensuring low-latency performance. Experimental results show that NeuroEdge achieves a real-time classification accuracy of 90% across seven hand gestures, with a total average latency of 83 ms using 192 channels of HD-EMG recorded from the forearm. Our system demonstrates the feasibility of deploying complex HD-EMG-based gesture recognition on microcontroller-based edge devices, bridging the gap between high-resolution biosignal acquisition and deep learning-based embedded inference for next-generation NMIs.

</details>


### [209] [Attention as In-Context Empirical Bayes: A Two-Stage View via Particle Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29351)
*Matthew Smart,Soumya Ganguly,Nilava Metya,Alexandre V. Morozov,Anirvan M. Sengupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Minimal attention-only transformers under all-token corruption can be interpreted as performing two-stage empirical Bayes inference, where depth refines distributions via particle dynamics and skip-connections carry noisy queries for posterior estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a statistical (empirical Bayes) interpretation of how attention mechanisms perform in-context inference, and to isolate the distinct roles of depth and residual connections without explicit density modeling.

Method: Analyzes minimal attention-only transformers under all-token corruption; models a single attention step as kernel-weighted posterior mean; depth refines distribution via particle dynamics (Stage 1); skip-connection carries noisy input as query (Stage 2).

Result: Denoising emerges without explicit noise schedule (fixed kernel bandwidth + finite horizon suffices); posterior-mean recovery guarantee for well-behaved priors; empirical estimator converges to Bayes-optimal predictor asymptotically.

Conclusion: Attention can be interpreted as in-context inference via sample-based posterior estimation, with connections to reverse-diffusion limits.

Abstract: We study minimal attention-only transformers under all-token corruption and show they admit a two-stage empirical Bayes interpretation. A single attention step computes a kernel-weighted posterior mean with respect to the empirical distribution defined by the context. Depth refines this distribution through particle dynamics (Stage 1), while a long-range skip-connection carries the noisy input as a query for posterior inference (Stage 2), revealing distinct statistical roles for depth and attention residuals. The framework isolates a minimal setting in which the context itself induces a depth-dependent energy landscape governing in-context inference. We show that effective denoising can emerge without an explicit noise schedule: a fixed kernel bandwidth and finite integration horizon suffice, yielding a principled depth-noise relationship. We further establish a posterior-mean recovery guarantee for a class of well-behaved priors, where the empirical estimator converges to the Bayes-optimal predictor under asymptotic conditions. Connecting these dynamics to reverse-diffusion limits, our results provide a statistical interpretation of attention as in-context inference via sample-based posterior estimation, without explicit density modeling.

</details>


### [210] [Neural-Behavioral Representation of Natural Whole-body Movement in Monkeys](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29355)
*Jieshi He,Puzhe Li,Yanan Sui,Mu-ming Poo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a neural-behavioral recording and modeling framework for freely moving monkeys, combining large-scale epidural cortical signals with multi-view motion capture to decode whole-body kinematics using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of previous motor decoding studies that focused on constrained tasks and limited limb movements, by understanding how cortical activity represents natural whole-body behaviors in primates.

Method: Combined large-scale epidural cortical signals from sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture in freely moving monkeys, using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model with a compact behavior prior to reconstruct whole-body kinematics.

Result: The model successfully decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movement without explicit physical constraints, providing a proof-of-concept approach for decoding natural whole-body movements.

Conclusion: Large-scale intracranial neural activity can effectively decode natural whole-body behaviors in primates, offering a novel framework for studying neural representation of complex movements.

Abstract: Understanding how cortical activity represents natural whole-body behaviors in primates remains challenging. Limited by the diversity of movements and inaccessibility of large-scale neural representation of whole-body kinematics, previous motor decoding studies focused on constrained tasks and limited limb movements. Here, we present a neural-behavioral recording and modeling framework for freely moving monkeys, combining large-scale epidural cortical signals from distributed sensory- and motor-related areas with synchronized multi-view motion capture through a custom-made data collection platform. We reconstructed whole-body monkey kinematics and learned a compact behavior prior using an autoregressive encoder-decoder model. Conditioned on neural signals, the model decoded accurate and realistic whole-body movement without explicit physical constraints. Our results provide a novel proof-of-concept approach for decoding natural whole-body movements in primates using large-scale intracranial neural activity.

</details>


### [211] [Solving Integer Linear Programming with Parallel Tempering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29366)
*Kyuil Sim,Sanghyeok Choi,Jinkyoo Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a solver-free, sampling-based optimization framework for Integer Linear Programming (ILP) that uses a Locally-Balanced Proposal and Parallel Tempering (including a novel penalty tempering) to directly explore discrete feasible regions without training or external solvers, achieving competitive performance against classical solvers like SCIP and Gurobi while being more robust to distribution shifts than learning-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning-based ILP methods suffer from poor generalization to out-of-distribution instances and dependence on external solvers. The authors aim to develop a solver-free approach that directly explores the discrete feasible region without training or external solver reliance.

Method: A sampling-based optimization framework that (1) uses a Locally-Balanced Proposal to construct transition kernels exploiting ILP's linear structure, avoiding gradient approximation; (2) integrates Parallel Tempering to address multimodal energy landscapes; (3) introduces penalty tempering that modulates constraint barriers while preserving the objective landscape over feasible solutions.

Result: The method consistently outperforms SCIP across all four benchmarks, matches/exceeds Gurobi on two of four tasks within 200-second budget, is more robust to distribution shift than learning-based methods, and remains competitive on MIPLIB 2017 instances without problem-specific tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed solver-free, sampling-based framework is an effective and robust alternative to both classical solvers and learning-based methods for ILP, with strong generalization and no need for training or external solvers.

Abstract: Integer Linear Programming (ILP) serves as a versatile framework for modeling a wide range of combinatorial optimization problems, typically addressed by sophisticated exact solvers or heuristics. While learning-based approaches have recently shown their effectiveness, they suffer from poor generalization to out-of-distribution instances and inherent dependence on external solvers. In this work, we propose a solver-free, sampling-based optimization framework for ILP that directly explores discrete feasible regions without training or external solvers. Exploiting the linear structure of ILP, we employ a Locally-Balanced Proposal to construct a transition kernel, thereby avoiding the gradient approximation. To overcome the highly multimodal nature of ILP energy landscapes, we integrate Parallel Tempering. In addition to standard temperature tempering, we introduce penalty tempering, which modulates constraint barriers while preserving the objective landscape over feasible solutions. Empirically, our method consistently outperforms SCIP across all four benchmarks, matches or exceeds Gurobi on two of four tasks within a 200-second budget, and is substantially more robust to distribution shift than learning-based methods. Furthermore, on MIPLIB 2017 instances, our framework remains competitive with classical solvers without any problem-specific tuning.

</details>


### [212] [Deep Adaptive Dimension Reduction for Bayesian Inference in Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29373)
*Yueyang Wang,Xili Wang,Kejun Tang,Xiaoliang Wan,Tao Zhou,Chao Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A deep adaptive dimension-reduction Bayesian inference framework using Variational Flow (VF) with iterative prior updating and FNO surrogate for solving high-dimensional PDE-governed inverse problems with complex non-Gaussian posteriors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional PDE-governed inverse problems face challenges: complex non-Gaussian posteriors, expensive forward model evaluations, and misspecified prior information.

Method: Proposes Variational Flow (VF) combining VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows, iterative prior updating strategy, and adaptively fine-tuned FNO surrogate in a closed adaptive loop.

Result: Outperforms MCMC, UKI, and SVGD on 100D Rosenbrock and three PDE inverse problems, especially in high-noise and high-dimensional settings.

Conclusion: The proposed framework delivers competitive/superior accuracy to baselines, with most pronounced advantages in challenging scenarios.

Abstract: Solving high-dimensional PDE-governed inverse problems is often challenging due to complex non-Gaussian posterior distributions, expensive forward model evaluations, and misspecified prior information. To address these issues, we propose a deep adaptive dimension-reduction Bayesian inference framework based on the Variational Flow (VF) model. Since standard normalizing flows are restricted by bijective mappings and cannot directly reduce dimensions, VF overcomes this limitation by integrating VAE-based nonlinear dimension reduction with dual normalizing flows for the latent prior and encoder. This design provides a strictly higher evidence lower bound than VAE and allows more flexible approximation of complex posterior distributions. We further introduce an iterative prior updating strategy that gradually moves the prior mean toward high-probability posterior regions, avoiding manual prior tuning. These components form a closed adaptive loop together with an adaptively fine-tuned Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) surrogate: VF generates posterior-concentrated samples to refine the surrogate, while the updated surrogate further improves posterior inference. Numerical experiments on a 100-dimensional Rosenbrock problem and three standard PDE-governed inverse problems show that our method delivers competitive or superior accuracy compared with MCMC, UKI, and SVGD baselines across all tested configurations, with the most pronounced advantages emerging in challenging scenarios such as high-noise observations and high-dimensional parameter spaces.

</details>


### [213] [TRACER: Persistent Regularization for Robust Multimodal Finetuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29380)
*Hesam Asadollahzadeh,Feng Liu,Christopher Leckie,Sarah M. Erfani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRACER is a theoretically-grounded framework for multimodal contrastive finetuning that uses Weighted Moving Average (WMA) teacher distillation instead of standard EMA to prevent catastrophic forgetting and maintain OOD robustness in CLIP finetuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard finetuning of pretrained multimodal models degrades out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness due to catastrophic forgetting. The paper aims to develop a theoretical framework to understand this phenomenon and design a principled solution.

Method: The authors develop a theoretical framework with closed-form solutions for multimodal contrastive finetuning, revealing that self-distillation outperforms other regularization methods. They identify that EMA teachers suffer from collapse and prove WMA teachers provide persistent regularization and bias-free convergence. This leads to TRACER, combining contrastive learning with WMA-guided multi-perspective distillation.

Result: Experiments on CLIP finetuning show consistent OOD accuracy and calibration improvements across three backbone architectures. Ablations confirm TRACER is principled and robust to hyperparameter choices.

Conclusion: The proposed TRACER framework effectively addresses catastrophic forgetting in multimodal finetuning, offering both theoretical foundations and practical performance gains for OOD robustness.

Abstract: Mainstream strategies for finetuning pretrained multimodal models often degrade out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness, a phenomenon known as catastrophic forgetting. In this paper, we develop a theoretical framework for multimodal contrastive finetuning, yielding closed-form solutions and a geometric decomposition for each strategy. This framework shows that self-distillation is more effective than other regularization approaches to retain the knowledge of the pretrained model. Our analysis reveals a largely overlooked limitation: standard Exponential Moving Average (EMA) teachers, widely used in robust finetuning, suffer from collapse. To solve this, we prove that a Weighted Moving Average (WMA) teacher maintains a persistent regularizing force over finite horizons and yields bias-free convergence in the task subspace while preserving orthogonal knowledge. These insights motivate **TRACER** (**T**rajectory-**R**obust **A**nchoring for **C**ontrastive **E**ncoder **R**egularization), which combines contrastive learning with WMA-guided multi-perspective distillation. Extensive experiments on CLIP finetuning demonstrate consistent OOD accuracy and calibration gains across three backbone architectures, and comprehensive ablations confirm that TRACER is both principled and robust to hyperparameter choices. Code is available at [https://github.com/HesamAsad/TRACER](https://github.com/HesamAsad/TRACER).

</details>


### [214] [On the Optimizer Dependence of Neural Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29387)
*Vansh Ramani,Shourya Vir Jain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The scaling exponent α in neural scaling laws depends on optimizer choice, not just architecture and data. In random-feature regression experiments, preconditioned optimizers yield steeper scaling (larger α), with natural gradient achieving 2.6× larger α than gradient descent at spectral characteristics of natural language.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the common assumption that the scaling exponent α is a fixed constant determined solely by architecture and data, and to investigate whether optimizer choice systematically influences scaling laws.

Method: Controlled random-feature regression experiments across five optimizer variants and six spectral conditions, measuring the scaling exponent α under different preconditioned and non-preconditioned optimizers.

Result: Preconditioned optimizers consistently produce steeper scaling (larger α). The shift varies across spectral ranges, peaking near s=1.5 and remaining large at s=2.0. At s≈1.0 (natural language regime), natural gradient yields α≈0.31 vs α≈0.12 for gradient descent (2.6× larger).

Conclusion: Scaling-law forecasts should account for optimizer choice. The authors provide a spectral diagnostic to predict when advanced optimizers will yield benefits, though transferability to large-scale LLM training remains an open question.

Abstract: The scaling exponent $α$ in neural scaling laws $L(N) \propto N^{-α}$ is commonly treated as a fixed constant set by architecture and data. We present evidence that $α$ depends systematically on the optimizer. In controlled random-feature regression experiments -- the canonical theoretical framework for neural scaling -- we measure $α$ across five optimizer variants and six spectral conditions. Preconditioned optimizers consistently yield steeper scaling (larger $α$), with the $α$-shift increasing across most of the tested spectral range, peaking near $s = 1.5$, and remaining large at $s = 2.0$. At $s \approx 1.0$ (characteristic of natural language), the full natural gradient achieves $α\approx 0.31$ versus $α\approx 0.12$ for gradient descent -- a $2.6\times$ larger fitted exponent that, within the random-feature model, compounds with each model-size doubling. Whether and how this exponent shift transfers to large-scale LLM training -- where recent evidence suggests the advantage may attenuate with scale -- remains an important open question. Our results imply that scaling-law forecasts should account for optimizer choice, and we provide a spectral diagnostic predicting when advanced optimizers will pay off.

</details>


### [215] [GDSD: Reinforcement Learning as Guided Denoiser Self-Distillation for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29398)
*Xiaohang Tang,Keyue Jiang,Che Liu,Qifang Zhao,Xiaoxiao Xu,Sangwoong Yoon,Ilija Bogunovic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GDSD proposes a likelihood-free self-distillation method for reinforcement learning in diffusion LLMs, avoiding biases from ELBO-based surrogate likelihoods and achieving up to +19.6% accuracy gains on planning, math, and coding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL for diffusion LLMs requires policy likelihood, which is intractable. Existing methods use ELBO as a surrogate, but this introduces training-inference mismatch (TIM) bias that degrades performance.

Method: GDSD distills the denoiser of dLLMs using an advantage-guided self-teacher derived from the closed-form optimum of reverse-KL regularized RL. It matches denoiser logits to the teacher's via a normalization-free objective, reducing RL to likelihood-free self-distillation and bypassing TIM biases.

Result: On planning, math, and coding benchmarks with LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, GDSD consistently outperforms prior ELBO-based methods with more stable training reward dynamics, achieving test-accuracy improvements up to +19.6%.

Conclusion: Direct denoiser self-distillation without relying on an ELBO likelihood surrogate provides a more stable and effective RL procedure for dLLMs.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) can be used to improve the policy (denoiser) of diffusion large language models (dLLMs), while being hindered by the intractability of the policy likelihood. A dominant and efficient family of methods replaces the likelihood in standard RL with its evidence lower bound (ELBO), estimated from randomly masked sequences. Despite being well aligned with pre-training, these approaches introduce bias through training--inference mismatch by using the ELBO as a likelihood surrogate, which can degrade performance. In this work, we propose Guided Denoiser Self-Distillation (GDSD) to directly distill the denoiser of dLLMs from an advantage-guided self-teacher, derived from the closed-form optimum of reverse-KL regularized RL. GDSD matches the dLLM's denoiser logits to the teacher's via a normalization-free objective, which reduces RL to likelihood-free self-distillation and thus bypasses the TIM biases. Recent ELBO-based methods emerge as instances of applying different distillation divergences, but with diagnosable pathologies that GDSD avoids. On planning, math, and coding benchmarks with LLaDA-8B and Dream-7B, GDSD consistently outperforms prior state-of-the-art ELBO-based methods with a more stable training reward dynamics, achieving test-accuracy improvements of up to $+19.6\%$. These results suggest that direct denoiser self-distillation, without relying on an ELBO likelihood surrogate, can provide a more stable and effective RL procedure for dLLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/GaryBall/GDSD.

</details>


### [216] [Rethinking Post-Training Recipes for Multimodal Time-Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29401)
*Haoxin Liu,Yichen Zhou,Rajat Sen,B. Aditya Prakash,Abhimanyu Das*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PostTime post-trains LLMs to act as context-guided revisors over numerical TSFM priors for multimodal time-series forecasting, achieving state-of-the-art results by combining SFT and RLVR with automated reasoning traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TSFMs excel at zero-shot unimodal forecasting using numerical data but cannot consume multimodal, non-numerical context (e.g., text, images) that real-world trajectories depend on.

Method: PostTime is a post-training recipe that combines Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), with automated reasoning traces for forecast revisions. It teaches an LLM to generate context-conditioned forecast interventions (revise, preserve, or ignore the TSFM prior).

Result: Evaluated on TimesX multimodal forecasting benchmark using Gemma-3-4B LLM + TimesFM-2.5 TSFM, PostTime significantly outperforms standalone TSFMs, LLM-only baselines, and existing multimodal forecasting approaches.

Conclusion: Bridging the gap between TSFMs and LLMs via multimodal post-training yields superior forecasting performance by leveraging both numerical priors and rich contextual information.

Abstract: Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) excel at zero-shot unimodal forecasting using numerical data, but unlike LLMs they cannot consume multimodal, non-numerical context that often shape real-world trajectories. In this work, we bridge this gap and argue for a multimodal time-series forecasting approach that post-trains LLMs to act as context-guided revisors over strong numerical TSFM priors. We introduce PostTime, a post-training recipe combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), along with a methodology to generate automated reasoning traces for forecast revisions. PostTime teaches an LLM to generate context-conditioned forecast interventions -- decisions to revise, preserve, or ignore the TSFM prior based on the multimodal context. We evaluate this approach on the TimesX multimodal forecasting benchmark using a Gemma-3-4B LLM and TimesFM-2.5 TSFM, and show that it significantly outperforms standalone TSFMs, LLM-only baselines, and existing multimodal forecasting approaches.

</details>


### [217] [Information-Directed Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29405)
*Keru Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formalizes residual uncertainty in offline-to-online decision-making using conditional mutual information, proposes information-directed sampling (IDS) as a principled exploration strategy, proves Bayesian regret bounds via a ratio certificate, and shows IDS outperforms Thompson sampling when offline data is informative but leaves biased or low-probability residual uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formalize and address the residual uncertainty that remains after offline pre-training, and to develop a principled exploration strategy that trades off regret against information gain in offline-to-online settings.

Method: Formalizes residual uncertainty via conditional mutual information I(χ;τ_{1:T}|D_N), proposes information-directed sampling (IDS) parameterized by η≥0, proves a generic offline-to-online Bayesian regret bound via a ratio certificate, and analyzes the method in Bayesian linear-reward models with known dynamics.

Result: Proves that IDS inherits any information-ratio bound from Thompson sampling via the ratio certificate. In Bayesian linear models, vanilla IDS achieves Õ(H d min{√T, T√(C†/N)}). Shows a constant-factor Bayesian regret separation between IDS and Thompson sampling in a warm-start regime. Experiments on bandit and D4RL offline-to-online RL validate the mechanism.

Conclusion: IDS is most beneficial when offline data is informative but leaves biased or low-probability residual uncertainty that targeted online exploration can resolve—a regime common in offline RL, offline black-box optimization, and Bayesian optimization.

Abstract: Decision-making from offline datasets typically warm-starts a policy or score model from fixed offline data and then refines it with limited online interaction. Offline data reduces uncertainty, but it does not remove the need for exploration; it changes what remains to be explored. We formalise this residual uncertainty by the conditional mutual information $I(χ;τ_{1:T}\mid\mathcal{D}_N)$ between a learning target $χ$ and the online trajectories after conditioning on the offline dataset. This view leads naturally to information-directed sampling (IDS), a family parameterised by $η\ge 0$ that selects actions by trading off instantaneous regret against information gain. We prove a generic offline-to-online Bayesian regret bound for IDS through a ratio certificate: any information-ratio bound satisfied by a reference Thompson-sampling policy over the same randomised policy class is inherited by IDS. In a known-dynamics Bayesian linear-reward model, the conditional mutual information has a log-determinant form, and vanilla IDS ($η=0$) satisfies $\widetilde O\!\left(Hd\min\left\{\sqrt T,\,T\sqrt{C^\dagger_{β,\mathrm{IDS}_0}(N,T)/N}\right\}\right),$ where the coverage coefficient is tied to the visitation distribution induced by vanilla IDS itself. We also identify a warm-start regime with a dominated but informative probe in which vanilla IDS selects the probe while Thompson sampling never does, giving a constant-factor Bayesian regret separation. Controlled bandit experiments and D4RL offline-to-online RL experiments validate this mechanism: IDS is most beneficial when offline data is informative but leaves biased or low-probability residual uncertainty that targeted online actions can resolve, a regime shared by offline RL, offline black-box optimization, and Bayesian optimization.

</details>


### [218] [The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly of Markov Boundary for Tabular Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29411)
*Shu Wan,Abhinav Gorantla,Huan Liu,K. Selçuk Candan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The Markov boundary theoretically identifies the minimal predictive feature set, but in practice on 3,450 SCM tasks, oracle boundaries improve prediction, yet causal discovery pipelines fail due to structural (not predictive) optimization, asymmetric error costs, and multiple competing feature sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether the theoretically optimal Markov boundary is practically useful for tabular prediction, given that modern regressors still train on full feature sets despite the boundary's promise of conditional independence.

Method: Evaluated on SCM3K (3,450 synthetic SCM tasks, 40-1000 features, six SCM families) using six regressors. Compared full feature training, oracle boundary restriction, and boundary recovery via causal discovery.

Result: Oracle boundary improves prediction substantially, especially in larger/sparser spaces. But causal discovery pipelines fail: estimators exhaust compute before reaching helpful regimes, rarely beat full features. Three causes identified: structural vs predictive optimization, asymmetric cost of false positives/negatives, and existence of many equally good feature sets.

Conclusion: The Markov boundary is useful in principle but current discovery pipelines are misaligned with prediction goals; implications for prediction-aligned feature selection and causally-aware tabular models.

Abstract: Under standard graphical assumptions, the Markov boundary of a target variable is the smallest set of features that renders every other feature redundant. Once the boundary is observed, the target is conditionally independent of the rest of the table. This is a tempting object for tabular prediction, since it names exactly the columns a model should need. Yet modern regressors are still trained on the full feature set. We ask whether the Markov boundary is genuinely useful for prediction on SCM3K, a 3,450-task synthetic SCM benchmark with feature counts from 40 to 1000 and six SCM families, evaluated with six regressors. The answer is more nuanced than the theory suggests. Restricting a regressor to the oracle boundary often improves prediction substantially, and the improvement grows as the feature space becomes larger and sparser. But the natural pipeline of recovering the boundary with causal discovery and training on the recovered mask does not deliver. Existing estimators exhaust the compute budget before reaching the regime where the boundary helps most, and even where they run they rarely beat the full feature set. We trace this to three causes. Discovery optimizes structural recovery rather than prediction. False negatives and false positives carry sharply asymmetric predictive cost. The exact boundary is only one of many feature sets that beat all features. We then develop what these facts imply for prediction-aligned feature selection and for tabular models that learn to use causal structure.

</details>


### [219] [Forget Less, Generalize More: Unifying Temporal and Structural Adaptation for Dynamic Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29453)
*Qian Chang,Ciprian Doru Giurcaneanu,Runsong Jia,Xia Li,Guoping Hu,Xiufeng Cheng,Jinqing Yang,Mengjia Wu,Yi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DSRD is a dynamic graph representation learning framework with dual-scale retentive states and learnable decay kernels, achieving SOTA on 14 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamic graph methods use fixed temporal decay or fixed structural propagation depths, which cannot generalize across graphs with diverse interaction frequencies and topological characteristics.

Method: DSRD introduces (i) a retentive state with dual-scale adaptation jointly modeling temporal dynamics and structural propagation in a single recurrent formulation, and (ii) adaptive decay kernels with learnable time-sensitivity parameters that balance short-term and long-term retention.

Result: State-of-the-art on 14 real-world benchmarks for link prediction and node classification, with strong transductive and inductive generalization.

Conclusion: The unified dual-scale retentive formulation with learnable decay effectively captures diverse temporal-structural dependencies, with theoretical guarantees on stability and boundedness.

Abstract: Representation learning on dynamic graphs requires capturing complex dependencies that evolve across both time and structure. Existing approaches typically adopt fixed temporal decay schemes or predetermined structural propagation depths, limiting their ability to generalize across graphs with diverse interaction frequencies and topological characteristics. We propose Dual-Scale Retentive Dynamics (DSRD), a unified framework that maintains a retentive representation state encoding both temporal memory and structural context.
