  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents operating as digital employees are unpredictable (hallucination, misinterpretation, adversarial manipulation) yet technically capable, making it unsafe to trust them with security-critical metadata like access policies and data classifications.

Method: Out-of-band metadata channels carry security context, policy signals, and audit trails deterministically outside the agent's read/write path, enforcing governance at three stages: scoping data access (in), constraining actions (during), and capturing tamper-proof transcripts (out).

Result: Demonstrated with a multi-agent portfolio rebalancing system where per-client data scoping, trade approval thresholds, and tamper-proof audit trails are enforced by channels agents cannot see or bypass.

Conclusion: Moving security-critical metadata out of the agent's path provides deterministic governance without relying on agent trustworthiness, enabling safer autonomous agent deployment.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly expected to operate as digital employees: accessing enterprise data, making decisions, and taking actions autonomously. But agents are simultaneously less predictable than humans -- prone to hallucination, misinterpretation, and adversarial manipulation -- and more technically capable: with deep system knowledge and high-throughput interfaces cascading damage at machine speed. This combination makes it unsafe to rely on agents to faithfully interpret or propagate security-critical metadata such as access policies, data classifications, and behavioral constraints.
  We present the Redpanda Agentic Data Plane (ADP), an architecture built around out-of-band metadata channels: infrastructure pathways that carry security context, policy signals, and audit trails deterministically, entirely outside the agent's read and write path and across heterogeneous infrastructure. These channels enforce governance at every stage of the agent lifecycle -- scoping data access on the way in, constraining actions during execution, and capturing tamper-proof transcripts on the way out.
  We demonstrate ADP with a multi-agent portfolio rebalancing system in which autonomous agents monitor markets, make trade decisions, and execute orders across isolated client accounts -- with per-client data scoping, trade approval thresholds, and tamper-proof audit trails all enforced by out-of-band channels the agents can neither see nor bypass.

</details>


### [331] [The Chain Holds, the Answer Folds: Trace-Answer Dissociation in Reasoning Models Under Adversarial Pressure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29087)
*Yubo Li,Ramayya Krishnan,Rema Padman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Reasoning models exhibit "unfaithful capitulation" (UC) in multi-turn dialogue: chain-of-thought remains correct but final answer flips wrong under adversarial pressure. This is missed by standard metrics, happens ~50% in think mode vs 11-15% without reasoning, varies across models, and naive defenses backfire.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of reasoning models uses single-turn benchmarks, but real-world deployment involves multi-turn dialogue where users challenge correct answers. The paper seeks to uncover failure modes missed by existing metrics.

Method: The authors introduce a 2×2 latent-versus-behavioral framework to isolate unfaithful capitulation (UC). They test across three datasets (MT-Consistency, MMLU-Pro, GSM8K), compare think vs no_think modes, use an independent GPT-4o judge for labeling, token-level probes, and test a trace-anchored defense.

Result: UC occurs in ~50% of behavioral flips in think mode, collapsing to 11-15% in no_think mode. The effect varies by model (high in Qwen3-32B and GPT-OSS-20B, low in Gemma-4-31B-it). GPT-4o judges agree 86% of the time; token-level argmax is correct in 84% of UC cases; the naive defense backfires.

Conclusion: Unfaithful capitulation is a real, measurable failure of reasoning models in multi-turn settings. Reasoning creates the vulnerability gap, and simple defenses are insufficient. The released data and metrics enable further research into this problem.

Abstract: Reasoning models are evaluated on single-turn benchmarks but deployed in multi-turn dialogue, where users push back on correct answers. Under sustained adversarial pressure we find a previously undocumented failure mode: the chain-of-thought stays factually correct from first turn to last while the emitted answer flips wrong. We call this unfaithful capitulation (UC) and isolate it with a $2\times 2$ latent-versus-behavioral framework that flip-rate metrics and single-turn faithfulness probes both miss. Across three datasets (MT-Consistency, MMLU-Pro, GSM8K), the latent-correct rate at the behavioral flip clusters near 50% in think mode and collapses to 11-15% under no_think -- paired, within-model causal evidence that reasoning creates the gap. Across models the effect tracks the reasoning channel (high in Qwen3-32B and GPT-OSS-20B, low in inline-CoT Gemma-4-31B-it). An independent GPT-4o judge corroborates $86\%$ of UC labels; a token-level probe shows the answer-slot argmax is correct in $84\%$ of UC cells; and a naive trace-anchored defense backfires. We release all trajectories, traces, and judge labels.

</details>


### [332] [Trends in AI and Human-AI Interaction in Clinical Trials -- A Hybrid Human-AI Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29096)
*Sandra Woolley,Tim Collins,Khalid Khattak,Illia Chernomorets,Ariane Arevalo,Chris Richardson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper analyzes AI-related clinical trials from ClinicalTrials.gov, finding a sharp increase in AI trials over time (especially ML, deep learning, chatbots, LLMs), led by China and the US. It also tests a hybrid human-GPT-5.5 screening method, showing good agreement on non-AI studies but lower agreement on human-AI interaction classification, suggesting hybrid screening is viable but needs clearer reporting and definitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To characterize temporal and geographical trends in AI-related clinical trials and assess the viability of a hybrid human-AI workflow for screening and categorizing such trials.

Method: A hybrid workflow using GPT-5.5 (frontier generative AI) combined with human review to screen and categorize clinical trial records from ClinicalTrials.gov based on AI-focused search terms.

Result: Marked increase in AI-related trials over time; China and US lead in numbers; recent growth in Italy, France, Spain, UK, Turkey. Human-AI classifiers showed good agreement on non-AI studies but lower agreement on human-AI interaction classification, especially when health professional interaction was ambiguous.

Conclusion: Hybrid human-AI screening of clinical trial records is potentially viable, but improved trial reporting clarity and more precise definitions of human-AI interaction are needed.

Abstract: This paper examines records retrieved from the ClinicalTrials.gov registry to characterize temporal trends in AI terminology and the geographical distribution of AI trials. The work also reports on an exploratory hybrid human-AI approach to analyzing human-AI interaction trends in registered clinical trials. The hybrid workflow comprised a frontier generative AI model (GPT-5.5) and human review to screen and categorize records returned by an AI-focused search. The findings indicate a marked increase in AI-related trials over time, with recent growth in references to machine learning, deep learning, chatbots, GPTs, and large language models. Geographically, China and the United States accounted for the largest numbers of AI-related trials, with notable recent increases in several other countries including Italy, France, Spain, the UK and Turkey (Türkiye). In a random sample of 100 records, human and AI classifiers showed good agreement in identifying studies not substantively using AI, but lower agreement in classifying human-AI interaction, particularly where health professional interaction was ambiguous or insufficiently described. Overall, the results suggest that hybrid human-AI screening of clinical trial records is potentially viable, but clearer trial reporting and more precise interaction definitions will benefit the process.

</details>


### [333] [Beyond Consensus: Trace-Level Synthesis in Mixture of Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29116)
*Shreyas Fadnavis,Praitayini Kanakaraj,Felix Wyss*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM aggregation should use full reasoning traces rather than compressed answers; Self-Consistent Mixture of Agents (SC-MoA) recovers correct solutions even when all agents unanimously agree, outperforming majority voting and heterogeneous model pools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current standard practice compresses each LLM agent's reasoning into a majority vote or layered synthesis, treating agreement as the finish line. The authors show this is unnecessarily lossy, as unanimous agreement can still be incorrect and trace-level information is discarded.

Method: Self-Consistent Mixture of Agents (SC-MoA): generates trace diversity through semantic-preserving input perturbations, safeguards majority via anchored refinement with provable non-degradation guarantees, and always synthesizes traces rather than gating on consensus.

Result: A single model with perturbation-induced trace variation outperforms heterogeneous model pools across structured reasoning, PhD-level science, competition mathematics, and competitive programming. The aggregation paradox: an LLM aggregator reading complete reasoning traces recovers correct solutions even when agents unanimously agree.

Conclusion: The unit of aggregation should be the reasoning trace, not the answer. Trace-level complementarity from minority chains that voting discards is key to improvement.

Abstract: When multiple LLM agents solve the same problem, standard practice compresses each agent's reasoning into a majority vote or layered synthesis, treating agreement as the finish line. We show this is unnecessarily lossy: an LLM aggregator that reads complete reasoning traces recovers correct solutions even when agents unanimously agree, with beneficial corrections consistently outweighing harmful ones -- the \emph{aggregation paradox}. Majority voting has a ceiling that perturbation diversity does not raise (error correlations are identical); the aggregator's gain comes from trace-level complementarity, assembling correct intermediate steps from minority chains that voting discards. These findings motivate Self-Consistent Mixture of Agents which generates trace diversity through semantic-preserving input perturbations, safeguards the majority via anchored refinement with provable non-degradation guarantees, and always synthesizes -- never gates on consensus. A single model with perturbation-induced trace variation outperforms heterogeneous model pools across structured reasoning, PhD-level science, competition mathematics, and competitive programming. The unit of aggregation should be the reasoning trace, not the answer.

</details>


### [334] [PRO-CUA: Process-Reward Optimization for Computer Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29119)
*Yifei He,Rui Yang,Hao Bai,Tong Zhang,Han Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PRO-CUA is a process-reward optimization framework that trains computer use agents using iterative step-level reinforcement learning with a process reward model, overcoming limitations of filtered behavior cloning and trajectory-level RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CUA training suffers from costly live environment interaction, distribution shift from expert demonstrations, absence of negative learning signals, and sparse rewards with ambiguous credit assignment in trajectory-level RL.

Method: PRO-CUA decouples on-policy environment interaction from policy optimization by collecting states via live rollouts, generating diverse candidate actions per state, receiving step-level feedback from a process reward model (PRM), and optimizing with group-relative advantages.

Result: Experiments on live web benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PRO-CUA and the reliability of PRM-guided step-level training.

Conclusion: PRO-CUA enables dense and flexible credit assignment without golden answers or offline expert trajectories, while reducing distribution shift by training on the agent's own execution states.

Abstract: Computer use agents (CUAs) have shown strong potential for automating complex digital workflows, yet their training remains constrained by costly live environment interaction and limited high-quality supervision. Existing filtered behavior cloning pipelines suffer from imitation bottlenecks, including distribution shift from the expert demonstration and the absence of negative learning signals. Meanwhile, standard trajectory-level reinforcement learning struggles with sparse rewards, ambiguous credit assignment, and high infrastructure costs for long-horizon GUI interaction. In this work, we propose PRO-CUA, a process-reward optimization framework for training CUAs with iterative step-level reinforcement learning. PRO-CUA decouples on-policy environment interaction from policy optimization: the current policy collects states through live rollouts, generates diverse candidate actions for each state, receives step-level feedback from a process reward model (PRM), and is optimized with group-relative advantages. This design enables dense and flexible credit assignment without relying on golden answers or offline expert trajectories, while reducing distribution shift by training on the agent's own execution states. Experiments on live web benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PRO-CUA and the reliability of PRM-guided step-level training.

</details>


### [335] [The Confidence Shortcut: A Reasoning Failure Mode of Masked Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29123)
*Dueun Kim,Albert No*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Confidence-based decoding in masked diffusion language models is misaligned with complex reasoning, and confidence-aligned training amplifies errors on challenging inputs. Random masking better preserves reasoning-trajectory conditionals for difficult tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors argue that confidence-based decoding, the standard inference policy for MDMs, is inherently misaligned with the logical-flow trajectories required for complex reasoning. They show that confidence-aligned training entrenches this misalignment, leading to high-confidence errors on challenging inputs.

Method: The study uses multi-digit addition as a concrete example, along with four other reasoning tasks, to compare confidence-based decoding vs. random masking under different training schemes (confidence-aligned vs. random masking training).

Result: Confidence-based decoding produces high-confidence errors on challenging inputs; confidence-aligned training amplifies error rates by an order of magnitude on difficult tails. Random masking robustly preserves reasoning-trajectory conditionals needed for complex problem-solving.

Conclusion: Confidence-based decoding and alignment are ill-suited for complex reasoning in MDMs. Random masking, despite its perceived inefficiency, better supports solving challenging reasoning tasks by maintaining proper conditional dependencies.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models (MDMs) uniquely support any-order generation, with confidence-based decoding currently serving as the de facto standard inference policy. To optimize for this, recent training schemes attempt to align training mask patterns directly with those observed during generation. However, we argue that confidence-based decoding is inherently misaligned with the logical-flow trajectories required for complex reasoning, and that confidence-aligned training actively entrenches this misalignment. We make this concrete using multi-digit addition, where the decoding strategy prematurely predicts locally easy digits before resolving their long-range dependencies, producing high-confidence errors on challenging inputs. While traditional random masking keeps the failure rate low on this challenging tail, confidence-aligned training amplifies the error rate by an order of magnitude. Across five distinct reasoning tasks, this same pattern emerges with task-dependent severity: confidence-based decoding induces failures on highly complex inputs, and confidence-aligned training exacerbates them. In contrast, random masking -- despite its perceived inefficiency -- robustly preserves the reasoning-trajectory conditionals essential for solving the challenging tail.

</details>


### [336] [Governing Technical Debt in Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29129)
*Muhammad Zia Hydari,Raja Iqbal,Narayan Ramasubbu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces two key concepts for managing AI agent systems: Agentic Technical Debt (accumulated liability from patching together agent components) and Stochastic Tax (recurring operating costs from managing probabilistic behavior), along with practical governance controls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in understanding and managing governance challenges specific to agentic AI systems that are not captured by traditional software or ML technical debt frameworks.

Method: Conceptual framework development defining two core constructs (Agentic Technical Debt and Stochastic Tax), distinguishing stock vs. flow liabilities, and proposing lightweight dashboard and governance control solutions.

Result: A clear distinction between design/governance liability (debt) and operating costs (tax) for agentic systems, with practical management approaches.

Conclusion: Agentic systems require specialized governance; managers can use lightweight dashboards to make both technical debt and stochastic tax visible and manageable.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems are increasingly being explored as production infrastructure: they reason over multiple steps, call tools, act through workflows, and adapt through memory and feedback. These systems create governance challenges that are not fully captured by traditional software or predictive ML technical debt. We define Agentic Technical Debt as the accumulated liability created when prompts, memory, tool schemas, orchestration graphs, control policies, and observability routines are patched together faster than they can be validated, standardized, and governed. We define Stochastic Tax as the recurring operating burden of keeping probabilistic agent behavior within acceptable bounds. The distinction matters: debt is a stock of design and governance liability, while the tax is a flow of operating cost that arises because stochastic agents act through tools and workflows. We outline how managers can make both visible through lightweight dashboards and governance controls.

</details>


### [337] [Better Later Than Sooner: Neuro-Symbolic Knowledge Graph Construction via Ontology-grounded Post-extraction Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29168)
*Lorenzo Loconte,Timothy Hospedales,Cristina Cornelio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a neuro-symbolic framework for ontology-grounded knowledge graph construction that improves consistency while reducing token usage, enabling better symbolic querying for complex QA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph-based RAG methods lack the structure needed for symbolic operations (e.g., aggregation, exhaustive listing) to answer complex questions reliably. LLM-based KG extraction introduces consistency issues like ontology violations.

Method: A neuro-symbolic framework combining open-domain extraction, embedding-based canonicalization of types/predicates, and targeted LLM-based correction of ontology violations in a post-extraction stage to avoid repeated LLM calls.

Result: Reduced token usage and improved KG consistency while preserving downstream QA quality. Extracted KGs are well suited for symbolic querying as measured by SPARQL graph pattern occurrences.

Conclusion: Post-extraction ontology correction is an efficient alternative to inline LLM corrections, achieving better KG consistency and supporting reliable symbolic querying for complex QA.

Abstract: Question answering (QA) is a core challenge in AI, particularly for complex queries requiring multi-hop reasoning across documents, or symbolic operations like aggregation or exhaustive listing. Retrieval-augmented generation has become the dominant approach to QA, with recent graph-based variants addressing part of these issues by organizing knowledge to better support compositional questions. However, most textual graph-based RAG methods still lack the structure needed for symbolic operations useful to answer complex questions reliably. This motivates symbolic graph-based approaches, which extract knowledge graphs (KGs) whose relations are logic predicates that enable SQL-like querying. Yet these pipelines typically use LLMs for KG extraction, which can introduce consistency issues, where extracted facts may violate commonsense ontology constraints. We propose a neuro-symbolic framework for ontology-grounded KG construction combining open-domain extraction, embedding-based canonicalization of types and predicates, and targeted LLM-based correction of ontology violations. By deferring corrections to a post-extraction stage, our method avoids repeated LLM calls, substantially reducing token usage while improving KG consistency and preserving downstream QA quality. Finally, we show that the extracted KGs are well suited for symbolic querying by measuring the occurrence of SPARQL graph patterns.

</details>


### [338] [Paper Agents, Paper Gains: An Empirical Analysis of DeFi Investment Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29174)
*Jay Yu,Amy Zhao,Danning Sui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A survey of 1,900+ AI-crypto projects finds that current DeFi investment agents remain early-stage, with weak fundamentals: agent treasuries hold $30M+ in paper gains while token holders lost $191.7M, market-cap-to-AUM ratios exceed 10,000x, and median returns are negative across all platforms. The paper proposes a maturity framework along autonomy, profitability, and stakeholder alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically evaluate the rapidly growing field of AI-powered DeFi investment agents, which have reached $3B+ in combined token valuations, and assess whether these systems deliver on their promises of autonomous, profitable trading.

Method: Surveyed 1,900+ AI-tagged crypto projects, filtered to investment-focused agents, curated 10 representative projects; deep-dive architectural analysis of ElizaOS and Virtuals Protocol; quantitative on-chain analysis of 11 Solana-based agent treasuries covering 925,323 token holders.

Result: (1) Many projects lack clear evidence of autonomous trade execution; (2) Agent treasuries have $30M+ paper gains but token holders lost $191.7M, top 1% captured 81.4% of gains; (3) Market-cap-to-AUM ratios >10,000x vs <1x for established DeFi; (4) Aggregate user gains peaked at $2.4B then declined to net losses, tokens down 93% from highs.

Conclusion: Current DeFi agents represent a permissionless first-generation market with rapid experimentation but lacking standards for autonomy, performance, and stakeholder alignment. A maturity framework is proposed addressing autonomous execution, risk-adjusted profitability, and stakeholder alignment to bridge to investment-grade systems.

Abstract: DeFi investment agents, systems that use AI for autonomous on-chain trading, have attained over USD 3 billion in combined token valuations since late 2024. We survey over 1,900 AI-tagged crypto projects, filter to investment-focused agents, and curate 10 representative projects spanning strategy and observability dimensions. We then conduct a deep-dive architectural analysis of two prominent agent frameworks, ElizaOS and Virtuals Protocol, and a quantitative on-chain performance analysis of 11 Solana-based agent treasuries with publicly attributable trading activity, covering 925,323 token holders. We find that current deployments remain early and heterogeneous: (1) in our sample, many projects do not yet provide clear evidence of autonomous trade execution, and developer interviews suggest that many visible deployments remain basic API integrations; (2) agent treasuries retain over USD 30M in paper gains while token holders collectively lost USD 191.7M, with the top 1% of wallets capturing 81.4% of all gains (USD 1.81B); (3) token valuations are weakly connected to treasury fundamentals, with market-cap-to-AUM ratios exceeding 10,000x versus below 1x for established DeFi protocols; and (4) aggregate user gains peaked at USD 2.4B before declining to net losses, with median returns negative on every platform and tokens declining 93% on average from all-time highs. We interpret these outcomes as characteristic of a permissionless, first-generation market in which open infrastructure enables rapid experimentation but also allows naive or speculative agents to launch before robust standards for autonomy, performance, and stakeholder alignment emerge. We therefore propose a maturity framework along three dimensions: autonomous execution, risk-adjusted profitability, and stakeholder alignment, to characterize the gap between current deployments and future investment-grade agent systems.

</details>


### [339] [ReasonOps: Operator Segmentation for LLM Reasoning Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29192)
*Daniel Lee,Owen Queen,James Zou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReasonOps is an unsupervised method that identifies 7 universal reasoning operators (e.g., backtracking, inferring, hypothesizing) from chain-of-thought traces across 12 LLMs. These operators reveal model-specific reasoning fingerprints, predict answer correctness, and enable early quality estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-thought traces from large reasoning models can be extremely long (tens of thousands of tokens), yet there is no adequate vocabulary for describing their internal structure. Existing analysis methods are either too rigid or insufficiently expressive.

Method: ReasonOps uses unsupervised clustering of sentence-initial 3-token pivots to discover 7 recurring reasoning operators. The method is applied to 44,662 traces from 12 thinking LLMs across 6 families and 8 benchmarks. Three independent LLM judges validate the operators with 70-76% accuracy.

Result: 7 universal reasoning operators emerge across all model families and domains. Reflective operators help on hard problems but hurt on easy ones. Operator distributions are highly model-identifying (distinct reasoning fingerprints). Structural operator features predict answer correctness well above baselines, and enable early quality estimation at 50% trace completion.

Conclusion: ReasonOps provides a powerful, annotation-free pipeline for deep analysis of LLM reasoning traces, enabling strong downstream results in model identification and correctness prediction.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought traces from large reasoning models can span tens of thousands of tokens, yet we lack a vocabulary for describing their internal structure. Previous methods developed to analyze chain-of-thought traces are either too rigid or not expressive enough, failing to capture features across domains and models. To remedy this, we develop ReasonOps, an unsupervised, expressive method for annotating chain-of-thought traces, providing succinct universal operators. Using ReasonOps, we analyze 44,662 traces from 12 thinking LLMs spanning 6 families across 8 reasoning benchmarks and discover that they share a common compositional structure: 7 recurring reasoning operators -- discourse-level moves such as backtracking, inferring, and hypothesizing -- that emerge from unsupervised clustering of sentence-initial 3-token pivots. These operators appear across every model family and benchmark domain, confirmed by three independent LLM judges who classify held-out samples at 70 -76% accuracy. We analyze the structure of operators on easy vs. hard problems, revealing that reflective operators are more helpful on hard problems and harm performance on easy problems. Operator sequences are highly model-identifying: a classifier trained on operator distributions alone recovers the source model with macro-AUC, revealing that each model family has a distinctive reasoning fingerprint. Structural operator features predict within-problem answer correctness well above baselines. Classifiers built on these operators reach WP-AUC and on AIME specifically. ReasonOps further enables early quality estimation well before the trace completes: we predict at WP-AUC for only 50% of the trace. The ReasonOps pipeline is unsupervised and annotation-free, enabling deep insights into LLM reasoning traces as well as strong downstream results on model identification and correctness prediction.

</details>


### [340] [GTA: Generating Long-Horizon Tasks for Web Agents at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29218)
*Tenghao Huang,Kung-Hsiang Huang,Prafulla Kumar Choubey,Yilun Zhou,Muhao Chen,Jonathan May,Chien-Sheng Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GTA is a scalable framework that automatically generates realistic, multi-hop web agent tasks with executable trajectories by integrating crawling, retrieval, in-context generation, and quality control, revealing significant human-agent performance gaps across 50+ websites.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing web agent benchmarks are manually constructed with coarse annotations and lack intermediate trajectories; automatic generation efforts remain expensive, biased, and shallow, limiting progress on multi-hop, cross-page tasks.

Method: GTA integrates crawling, retrieval-based seeding, in-context generation, and automated quality control. It decouples crawling from generation, grounds tasks in site graphs for compositionality, and ensures dense supervision via deterministic replays and systematic validation.

Result: Applied to 50+ websites across e-commerce, government, forums, and news with multilingual and multi-hop coverage. The benchmark reveals a significant human-agent performance gap and enables detailed diagnostics.

Conclusion: Three contributions: (i) formalizing multi-hop web-agent task generation, (ii) an efficient, validated pipeline for automatic data creation, (iii) a dynamic benchmark with reproducible evaluation.

Abstract: Web agents, which couple language models with browsing and tool-use capabilities, show promise as open web assistants. Yet progress is increasingly limited by the lack of scalable, process-level supervision. Existing benchmarks are largely manually constructed, providing only coarse start-goal annotations without intermediate trajectories, while recent automatic generation efforts remain expensive, biased, and shallow. These limitations prevent reliable training and evaluation of agents that must generalize to realistic, multi-hop, cross-page tasks. We introduce a scalable framework, GTA, that integrates crawling, retrieval-based seeding, in-context generation, and automated quality control to produce realistic tasks paired with executable trajectories. This design decouples crawling from generation for greater efficiency, grounds tasks in the site graph to enforce compositionality, and ensures dense supervision through deterministic replays and systematic validation. We instantiate the pipeline on over 50 websites covering e-commerce, government, forums, and news, with multilingual and multi-hop coverage. The resulting benchmark reveals a significant human-agent performance gap and enables detailed diagnostics. Our contributions are three-fold: (i) formalizing multi-hop web-agent task generation, (ii) proposing an efficient and validated pipeline for automatic data creation, and (iii) releasing a dynamic benchmark with reproducible evaluation.

</details>


### [341] [BenchTrace: A Benchmark for Testing Reflection Ability and Controlled Evolution in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29225)
*Jiahao Huang,Fei Cheng,Junfeng Jiang,Zefan Yu,Akiko Aizawa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BenchTrace is a benchmark (1,821 annotated episodes across 6 tasks) for evaluating self-evolution in LLM agents, introducing Reflection Evaluation (QA-based failure diagnosis) and Evolution Evaluation (controlled simulation), along with a failure avoidance rate (FAR) metric. Experiments show Qwen3-32B and GPT-4.1 both <30% pass rate on reflection, with diagnosis as bottleneck; self-evolution improves FAR but suffers from forgetting and negative transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-evolving agent evaluation is limited: it only measures task scores (ignoring reflection quality) and relies on agents' own episode runs (no control over failure patterns). The authors wanted a targeted, model-agnostic framework to evaluate reflection and evolution abilities separately.

Method: Built a dataset of 1,821 annotated episodes across 6 tasks. Designed Reflection Evaluation (targeted QA probing failure identification) and Evolution Evaluation (controlled simulation testing whether past failures lead to avoidance). Introduced FAR metric. Evaluated Qwen3-32B and GPT-4.1.

Result: Both models <30% pass rate on reflection evaluation (diagnosis is the bottleneck). Self-evolution improves FAR over baseline, but agents forget early lessons with noise episodes and fail to generalize reflections across contexts (negative transfer). Only fully correct reflection strongly correlates with higher FAR.

Conclusion: BenchTrace exposes concrete limits of current self-evolution approaches and provides a controlled, model-agnostic framework for targeted evaluation of self-evolution abilities in LLM agents.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents improve over time by reflecting on past failures, but existing evaluation is limited in two ways: it measures only task scores, leaving reflection quality unknown, and it relies on agents' own episode runs, offering no mechanism to target specific failure patterns. We present \textbf{BenchTrace}, a benchmark for evaluating self-evolution ability in LLM agents. BenchTrace is built on a snapshot-reflection dataset of 1,821 annotated episodes spanning six diverse tasks, and comprises a \textbf{Reflection Evaluation} that probes failure identification through targeted QA tasks, and an \textbf{Evolution Evaluation} that tests whether past failure experience translates into avoidance behavior in a controlled self-evolution simulation. Building on BenchTrace, we propose \textbf{failure avoidance rate (FAR)}, a new evaluation metric measuring the fraction of test cases in which the agent successfully avoids the target failure instance. Experiments with Qwen3-32B and GPT-4.1 reveal that both models fall below a 30\% end-to-end pass rate on reflection evaluation, with diagnosis as the primary bottleneck. Evolution evaluation shows that self-evolution methods generally improve FAR over the non-evolving baseline, but agents forget early lessons as noise episodes accumulate, and agents fail to generalize their reflections beyond the specific context, causing negative transfer across task contexts. Our correlation analysis further reveals that only a fully correct reflection is strongly associated with higher FAR. BenchTrace exposes concrete limits of current self-evolution approaches and provides a controlled, model-agnostic framework for targeted evaluation.

</details>


### [342] [Tailoring the Curriculum: Student-Centered Reasoning Distillation via Dynamic Data-Model Compatibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29229)
*Jiahao Huang,Fei Cheng,Junfeng Jiang,Akiko Aizawa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces DMC metric to assess data-model compatibility for reasoning distillation, showing strong correlation with performance and enabling effective data selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning distillation success depends on alignment between training data and student model, but there was no metric to assess this compatibility.

Method: Proposes DMC metric that jointly considers data quality, relative difficulty, and student capability; validates via correlation analysis and data selection experiments.

Result: DMC strongly correlates with distillation performance; using DMC for data selection improves reasoning distillation; dynamic selection during training further enhances results.

Conclusion: DMC is an effective metric for assessing data-model compatibility, and dynamic DMC-based selection during training yields the best distillation outcomes.

Abstract: Reasoning distillation transfers complex reasoning abilities from large language models (LLMs) to smaller ones, yet its success depends on how well the training data align with the student model. This paper introduces the Data-Model Compatibility (DMC) metric, which can be used to assess the suitability of a dataset for reasoning distillation on a student model. DMC provides an assessment by jointly considering data quality, relative difficulty, and student capability. We validated the effectiveness of DMC from two perspectives: (1) DMC exhibits a strong correlation with reasoning distillation performance; and (2) using DMC as the criterion for data selection leads to improved reasoning distillation performance. Both findings are consistently demonstrated across multiple student models and tasks. Moreover, since the DMC of each dataset dynamically changes during training, our experiments demonstrate that dynamically selecting datasets based on DMC can further enhance performance.

</details>


### [343] [Rethinking Literature Search Evaluation: Deep Research Helps, and Human Citation Lists Are Not a Ground Truth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29234)
*Gaurav Sahu,Laurent Charlin,Christopher Pal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Deep Research pipeline for literature search that improves recall from below 20% to above 80%, while also revealing that human reference lists are biased (only 51% moderately relevant vs. 86-88% for AI re-rankers) with a 2.5x higher likelihood of citing direct collaborators, arguing for multi-axis evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in large-scale literature search—both by improving retrieval performance and by critically evaluating whether human reference lists serve as reliable ground truth for benchmarking.

Method: (1) A Deep Research pipeline that processes the full query paper and expands retrieved results breadth-first along bibliographies; (2) LLM-as-a-judge to assess citation relevance; (3) OpenAlex co-authorship graph analysis to study citation bias.

Result: Recall on RollingEval-Jun25 improved from <20% to >80%; only 51% of human citations were moderately relevant vs. 86-88% for top AI re-rankers; humans are 2.5x more likely to cite direct collaborators than top AI systems.

Conclusion: Literature-search evaluation should not rely on a single axis; recall, topical relevance, ranked-list diversity, and co-authorship distance diagnostics should be reported jointly.

Abstract: We study large-scale literature search from two complementary angles: improving the retrieval pipeline, and stress-testing the human reference list as an evaluation target. First, we implement a Deep Research pipeline that processes the full query paper and expands the retrieved results breadth-first along their bibliographies, and show that it substantially outperforms vanilla API-only search, raising recall on RollingEval-Jun25 (a 250-paper literature-search benchmark) from below 20% to above 80%. Second, we use a neutral LLM-as-a-judge to determine if human references are sound ground truth for the task. We find significant limitations: only 51% of human citations are judged moderately relevant or higher, against 86--88% for the strongest AI-based re-rankers. We study this gap on the OpenAlex co-authorship graph, finding that humans are 2.5x more likely than the best AI re-rankers to cite a direct collaborator. Together, our results argue against single-axis literature-search evaluation: recall, topical-relevance scoring, ranked-list diversity, and a co-authorship-distance diagnostic each measure complementary properties of citation quality and should be reported jointly.

</details>


### [344] [Planning with the Views via Scene Self-Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29563)
*Kangrui Wang,Linjie Li,Zhengyuan Yang,Shiqi Chen,Zihan Wang,Li Fei-Fei,Jiajun Wu,Leonidas Guibas,Lijuan Wang,Manling Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VLMs struggle with multi-turn view planning despite basic view-action knowledge. The paper proposes ViewSuite (3D benchmark) and an iterative self-exploration + view graph distillation framework, improving Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 2.5% to 47.8%, surpassing GPT-5.4 Pro and Gemini 3.1 Pro.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs lack the ability to compose view transformations across multi-turn plans for view planning in 3D space, creating a critical gap between single-action understanding and multi-step reasoning.

Method: Proposed ViewSuite benchmark on real ScanNet 3D point clouds; iterative framework alternating self-exploration (collecting trajectories) with view graph distillation (converting all trajectories into a structured graph for supervised tasks) to reshape policy distribution.

Result: Qwen2.5-VL-7B improved from 2.5% to 47.8% on interactive view planning, outperforming GPT-5.4 Pro (18.5%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (21.4%).

Conclusion: Self-exploration with view graph distillation is a promising direction for enabling VLMs to actively reason and plan in 3D space.

Abstract: Can VLMs predict how each camera move changes the view, and plan many such moves ahead? We call this capability view planning, requiring (1)understanding how a single action transforms the view, and (2)composing many such transformations across multi-turn plans to identify a target view. We probe both abilities in our proposed ViewSuite, a 3D point-cloud environment on real ScanNet scenes. Across 13 frontier VLMs, a critical planning gap emerges: they possess basic view-action knowledge but fail to compose it across multi-turn plans, with the gap widening as viewpoint distance grows. To close this gap, we propose an iterative framework that alternates self-exploration with view graph distillation. The key insight is that all exploration trajectories, regardless of their outcome, collectively form a view graph that compactly captures how viewpoints connect across a scene. Distilling this graph into diverse supervised tasks reshapes the policy distribution and overcomes the sparse rewards that stall pure RL. This improves Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 2.5% to 47.8% on interactive view planning, surpassing GPT-5.4 Pro (18.5%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (21.4%). Self-exploration emerges as a promising path toward VLMs that can actively reason and plan in 3D space.

</details>


### [345] [Surfacing Isolated Learners with Outcome-Independent Mediation of Feedback between Teachers and Students Using AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29240)
*Junsoo Park,Youssef Medhat,Htet Phyo Wai,Ploy Thajchayapong,Ashok K. Goel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an interpretable decision-layer that ranks course topics needing attention by combining three signals (student difficulty prevalence, self-report vs. observed difficulty disagreement, and unresolved teacher concerns) without using grades. Results show alignment with instructor concerns and student-reported difficulty, with improved identification of at-risk learners.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-augmented classrooms generate rich feedback signals before grades are available, but these signals are hard to translate into timely instructional decisions. The paper aims to create a transparent mechanism to bridge this gap.

Method: The approach combines three signals: (1) prevalence of student learning difficulty, (2) disagreement between learner self-reports and observed difficulties, and (3) unresolved teacher concerns. It outputs a ranked set of topic priorities with per-topic decision records.

Result: In a graduate CS course (n=279 surveys, n=5 instructor interviews), prioritized topics aligned with instructor concerns (top-5 overlap 3/5; Spearman ρ=0.80) and student-reported difficulty (ρ=0.46, p=.048). Multi-signal integration improved at-risk learner identification (AUC=0.96 vs 0.91 for gap prevalence alone).

Conclusion: Transparent coordination mechanisms may help support human-AI co-agency when feedback is incomplete, though findings are preliminary.

Abstract: AI-augmented classrooms generate rich teacher and student feedback before graded outcomes become available, yet these signals can be difficult to translate into timely instructional decisions. We propose an interpretable decision layer: a transparent mechanism that ranks course topics requiring attention without using grades or post-hoc outcome labels. The approach combines three signals: student learning difficulty prevalence, disagreement between learner self-reports and observed difficulties, and unresolved teacher concerns. The output is a ranked set of topic priorities with per-topic decision records explaining each ranking. In one graduate CS course offering ($n=5$ instructor interviews; $n=279$ survey responses), prioritized topics aligned with instructor concerns (top-5 overlap 3/5; Spearman $ρ=0.80$) and student-reported topic difficulty ($ρ=0.46$, $p=.048$). Multi-signal integration also surfaced learners not identified through individual signal sources alone (AUC $=0.96$ vs. $0.91$ for gap prevalence alone). Reflective thinking, help-seeking, and self-efficacy provided additional evidence that student behavioral signals align with learning-related constructs. While preliminary, these findings suggest that transparent coordination mechanisms may help support human-AI co-agency when feedback is incomplete.

</details>


### [346] [DenseSteer: Steering Small Language Models towards Dense Math Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29247)
*Yang Ouyang,Shuhang Lin,Jung-Eun Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes DenseSteer, a training-free framework that improves small LLMs' reasoning by steering them toward dense reasoning patterns (fewer steps, higher information density per step).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small LLMs (≤3B parameters) significantly underperform on multi-step reasoning tasks compared to larger models. The authors aim to bridge this gap without costly fine-tuning.

Method: DenseSteer is a training-free, inference-time steering framework that modulates internal representations of small models toward "dense reasoning" patterns (fewer steps but higher information density per step), as observed in more proficient LLMs.

Result: Consistent accuracy improvements on math reasoning benchmarks without increasing token-level Negative Log-Likelihood.

Conclusion: Dense reasoning is an effective structural approach to mathematical problem solving, offering a practical way to enhance small-model reasoning without additional training.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning abilities, while smaller models (<= 3B parameters) significantly underperform on multi-step reasoning tasks. Based on empirical analyses of the Qwen-2.5 model family on math reasoning benchmarks, we find that more proficient reasoning is associated with fewer reasoning steps but higher information density per step, a property we term Dense Reasoning. Motivated by this observation, we propose DenseSteer, a training-free inference-time steering framework that enhances small-model reasoning by modulating internal representations toward dense reasoning patterns. Experiments show that our method yields consistent accuracy improvements without increasing token-level Negative Log-Likelihood, highlighting dense reasoning as an effective structural approach to mathematical problem solving.

</details>


### [347] [Provably Secure Agent Guardrail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29251)
*Benlong Wu,Weiming Zhang,Kejiang Chen,Han Fang,Nenghai Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Proof-Constrained Action (ePCA) framework that uses formal first-order logic verification to enforce deterministic security bounds for LLM-based agents, achieving zero attack success rate and zero false positive rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing defense mechanisms rely on empirical semantic guardrails and probabilistic adjudicators, which fail to provide deterministic security guarantees against semantic symbol decoupling attacks — creating a fundamental security crisis as LLMs transition to autonomous agents.

Method: The ePCA framework employs a neural-symbolic isolation architecture that requires agents to losslessly formalize intentions into first-order logical constraints before executing physical operations, abandoning semantic trust in natural language in favor of formal verification.

Result: Empirical evaluations across macroscopic and microscopic two-dimensional dynamic adversarial systems show zero attack success rate, zero false positive rate, and extremely low computational latency.

Conclusion: This research provides a conditional formal foundation for constructing deterministic defense mechanisms in future intelligent systems, establishing a new security paradigm based on fundamental limitations of logical reasoning.

Abstract: As large language models transition from bounded generative engines to agents with expansive execution privileges, AI going out of control precipitates a fundamental crisis in artificial intelligence security. Existing defense architectures heavily rely on empirical semantic guardrails and probabilistic large model adjudicators, mechanisms that fail to provide deterministic security lower bounds when facing complex semantic symbol decoupling attacks. To overcome this empirical semantic guardrail dilemma, this paper proposes a new security paradigm for agents based on the fundamental limitations of logical reasoning. Based on this paradigm, we further introduce an executable Proof-Constrained Action (ePCA) framework with a neural symbolic isolation architecture. This framework abandons semantic trust in natural language, forcing agents to losslessly formalize their intentions into first-order logical mathematical constraints before performing physical operations. Empirical evaluations of macroscopic and microscopic two-dimensional dynamic adversarial systems demonstrate that our formal verification mechanism achieves zero attack success rate and zero false positive rate across the evaluated scenarios, with extremely low computational latency. This research provides a conditional formal foundation under explicit system assumptions and an engineering paradigm for constructing the underlying defense foundation for future intelligent systems.

</details>


### [348] [OpenClawBench: Benchmarking Process-side Anomalies in Real-world Agent Execution Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29253)
*Yibing Liu,Yangze Liu,Xiaolong Yin,Bin Wang,Chong Zhang,Hao Yin,Zhongyi Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenClawBench is a large-scale dataset (31,264 trajectories) revealing the Outcome-Process Gap: where agent tasks pass final oracles but hide process anomalies (e.g., unresolved ambiguity, unsafe writes). FullTax provides structured supervision (binary labels, severity, localization, 5-class taxonomy). Key finding: 2,904/31,135 oracle-passing executions are process-anomalous. A LoRA-fine-tuned Gemma 3 12B detector achieves F1=0.729.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Task success can conceal process anomalies in real agent executions. The paper aims to measure and supervise these hidden process-side failures that outcome-only evaluation misses.

Method: Built OpenClawBench from BFCL-driven OpenClaw sessions (6 source models, 31,264 annotated trajectories). Developed FullTax to convert trajectories into structured anomaly supervision including binary labels, onset/span localization, severity, recoverability, and a 5-class anomaly taxonomy.

Result: 31,135 oracle-passing executions; 2,904 process-anomalous. A LoRA-fine-tuned Gemma 3 12B detector on high-confidence FullTax data achieves binary F1=0.729 on cleaner-labels held-out test split.

Conclusion: Success-only evaluation misses a concrete class of process-side failures. OpenClawBench enables auditable, reusable supervision for studying and monitoring runtime agent reliability.

Abstract: Task success can hide process anomalies in real-world agent executions. An agent may pass the final task oracle while still accumulating unresolved ambiguity, unsafe external writes, ignored errors, weakly grounded commitments, or capability-boundary overcommitment. We study this mismatch as the Outcome-Process Gap and introduce OpenClawBench, a large-scale dataset for measuring and supervising process-side anomalies in real agent execution processes. OpenClawBench is built from BFCL-driven OpenClaw sessions produced by 6 source models and contains 31,264 annotated trajectories. It aligns task-oracle outcomes with structured process evidence. FullTax converts the aligned trajectories into structured anomaly supervision: binary labels, supporting evidence, onset/span localization, severity, recoverability, and a 5-class anomaly taxonomy. Using OpenClawBench, we make the Outcome-Process Gap measurable. Among 31,135 oracle-passing executions, 2,904 are still labeled process-anomalous under FullTax. These results show that success-only evaluation misses a concrete class of process-side failures in real agent executions. A LoRA-fine-tuned Gemma 3 12B detector trained on the high-confidence FullTax supervised pool reaches binary F1=0.729 on the cleaner-labels held-out test split. Together, OpenClawBench turns real agent execution logs into auditable and reusable supervision for studying, diagnosing, and operationally monitoring runtime agent reliability.

</details>


### [349] [Harmonizing Real-Time Constraints and Long-Horizon Reasoning: An Asynchronous Agentic Framework for Dynamic Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29262)
*Shijie Cao,Yuan Yuan,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RACE-Sched is an asynchronous agent-based framework for Dynamic Flexible Job Shop Scheduling that uses a dual-stream architecture to combine low-latency symbolic heuristics (Reactive Stream) with LLM-based reasoning (Deliberative Stream), enabling real-time dispatching while continuously improving scheduling rules through sandboxed validation and semantic indexing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DFJSP requires balancing real-time reactions to disturbances with global optimization. Priority rules are inflexible, learning methods lack interpretability/generalization, and LLMs have high inference latency incompatible with millisecond-level industrial control cycles.

Method: A dual-stream architecture: (1) Reactive Stream executes low-latency symbolic heuristics for real-time dispatching, (2) parallel Deliberative Stream uses an LLM to synthesize, validate, and evolve rules via sandbox testing and atomic updates. A semantic rule repository indexes validated heuristics for retrieval-based initialization.

Result: Outperforms Deep Reinforcement Learning and other LLM-based baselines on GEN-Bench, MK-Bench, and JMS-Bench benchmarks.

Conclusion: RACE-Sched successfully harmonizes real-time constraints with long-horizon reasoning, achieving superior solution quality and robust adaptation to dynamic events.

Abstract: The Dynamic Flexible Job Shop Scheduling Problem (DFJSP) necessitates a trade-off between instant reaction to stochastic disturbances and global optimization of production goals. Conventional priority rules are insufficiently flexible to handle complex disruptions, whereas learning-based approaches often compromise interpretability or fail to generalize across problem scales. Although Large Language Models (LLMs) offer advanced reasoning capabilities to bridge this gap, their substantial inference latency is incompatible with the millisecond-level decision cycles of industrial control systems. To resolve this conflict, we introduce RACE-Sched, an asynchronous agent-based framework that decouples policy execution from logical reasoning via a dual-stream architecture. The Reactive Stream executes low-latency symbolic heuristics to enable real-time dispatching, while the parallel Deliberative Stream leverages an LLM to synthesize, validate, and evolve these rules. Candidate rules undergo rigorous testing in a sandbox and are deployed via atomic updates, ensuring safety without blocking the control loop. Additionally, a semantic rule repository indexes validated heuristics for retrieval-based initialization which enhances transferability across problem scales. Extensive evaluations on GEN-Bench, MK-Bench, and JMS-Bench demonstrate that RACE-Sched outperforms leading Deep Reinforcement Learning and other LLM-based baselines. This approach harmonizes real-time constraints with long-horizon reasoning to achieve superior solution quality and robust adaptation to dynamic events.

</details>


### [350] [When and How Human Curation Backfires: Preference Alignment under Multi-Model Self-Consuming Loop](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29267)
*Yang Zhang,Xiukun Wei,Xueru Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies self-consuming training (models training on synthetic data from prior iterations) in multi-model settings, showing that cross-model interactions can reduce or invert the alignment benefits of human curation that work in isolated models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on self-consuming training focused on single, isolated models and showed human curation helps alignment. This paper extends the analysis to realistic multi-model scenarios where models interact and train on each other's outputs.

Method: Formalizes a framework for interacting self-consuming models, analyzes convergence conditions of the dynamical system, and studies how human curation's effects propagate (self-influence vs. cross-influence) across models.

Result: Cross-model interactions can dampen or invert the alignment benefits of human curation, leading to long-term alignment degradation—contrary to the isolated setting where curation always helps.

Conclusion: Multi-model self-consuming training introduces complex dynamics that can undermine the expected benefits of human curation, highlighting the need to consider ecosystem-level effects in training pipelines.

Abstract: Foundation models are increasingly trained on synthetic data generated by prior model iterations rather than exclusively on real data. This self-consuming training paradigm can lead to model collapse, divergence, or bias amplification. Recent work (Ferbach et al., 2024) shows that incorporating human curation into the loop can steer a self-consuming model toward human-aligned behavior, but these analyses focus on a single, isolated model that solely consumes its own outputs. In practice, however, models often interact and train on input-output pairs produced by other models. This paper studies self-consuming training in the multi-model regime. We first formalize a framework for interacting self-consuming models and characterize when the resulting dynamical system converges to a stable point. We then examine how human curation of one model affects its own alignment (self-influence) and how such effects propagate to other models (cross-influence). Unlike isolated settings where human curation always enhances model alignment, we show that cross-model interactions can dampen or even invert this effect, ultimately degrading long-term alignment.

</details>


### [351] [Indexing the Unreadable: LLM-Native Recursive Construction and Search of Service Taxonomies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29270)
*Wei Zheng,Yang Yan,Yiyang Shao,Jinyang Li,Zeze Chang,Yukuang Jia,Qiming Mao,Chihyung Wang,Jingbin Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A2X is a progressive-disclosure service discovery system that organizes MCP servers, A2A endpoints, and skills into a hierarchical taxonomy, enabling LLMs to browse relevant subsets layer by layer, overcoming context window limits and Lost-in-the-Middle issues. It achieves higher hit rates while using far fewer tokens than full-context or embedding-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents need to discover and orchestrate a growing number of services (MCP servers, A2A endpoints, skills), but existing methods that dump all descriptions into the prompt suffer from context window overflow and Lost-in-the-Middle phenomenon—effective context does not scale with registry size.

Method: An LLM-native progressive-disclosure pipeline that (1) automatically organizes registered services into a hierarchical taxonomy, and (2) at query time walks the taxonomy layer by layer so each LLM call only sees a small, highly relevant candidate subset.

Result: A2X achieves a 6.2-point Hit Rate gain over full-context dumping at one-ninth the prompt-token cost, and improves Hit Rate by more than 20 points over the state-of-the-art open-source embedding-based baseline.

Conclusion: Progressive-disclosure decouples effective-context scarcity from registry size, significantly reducing token consumption while improving retrieval accuracy—a promising direction for service discovery in the Internet of Agents era.

Abstract: The era of the Internet of Agents (IoA) is taking shape: LLM agents are expected to fulfill user goals by orchestrating fast-growing populations of Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, Agent-to-Agent (A2A) endpoints, reusable skills, and other LLM-callable services. Yet LLMs face a structural mismatch with this regime: effective context is a scarce resource that does not scale with the number of services. Concatenating thousands of service descriptions into a prompt overflows the context window, and even when the window is large enough, models systematically under-attend to information in the middle of long inputs, the well-documented Lost-in-the-Middle phenomenon. This is fundamentally a question of context management for service discovery. To address this, we propose an LLM-native progressive-disclosure scheme and its concrete instantiation, A2X (Agent-to-Anything service discovery): an LLM-driven pipeline that automatically organizes the registered services into a hierarchical taxonomy and walks it layer by layer at query time, so that every LLM call sees only a small candidate set highly relevant to the user query. This decouples effective-context scarcity from registry size and significantly reduces token consumption while improving retrieval accuracy. Compared to full-context dumping, A2X achieves a 6.2-point Hit Rate gain at one-ninth the prompt-token cost; compared to the state-of-the-art open-source embedding-based baseline, A2X improves Hit Rate by more than 20 points.

</details>


### [352] [CoHyDE: Iterative Co-Training of LLM Rewriter & Dense Encoder for Tool Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29271)
*Vaishali Senthil,Ashutosh Hathidara,Sebastian Schreiber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoHyDE is a co-training framework that iteratively improves both a dense encoder and an LLM-based query rewriter for tool retrieval, achieving significant gains over single-component baselines, especially on vague queries (+6.3 pp NDCG@5).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool retrieval over large API catalogs faces a fundamental mismatch: user queries are colloquial and underspecified, while API catalogs use technical vocabulary. Existing approaches (contrastive encoder fine-tuning and HyDE-style query expansion) each solve one side of this problem but fail on the other.

Method: CoHyDE iteratively co-trains a dense encoder and LLM rewriter: the encoder is retrained with InfoNCE on catalog-style hypothetical descriptions from the rewriter, while the rewriter is preference-aligned via DPO using the encoder's retrieval scores. Both components are warm-started on the tool catalog before the loop begins.

Result: On a ~10k tool subset of ToolBench, three rounds of CoHyDE improve over the best single-component baseline by +2.5 pp NDCG@5 on standard queries and +6.3 pp on vague queries, with up to +8 pp on the hardest vague tier. Ablations confirm co-training is essential.

Conclusion: Co-training the encoder and rewriter as a co-evolving system resolves the complementary failure modes of existing approaches, significantly improving tool retrieval for LLM agents across both well-formed and underspecified queries.

Abstract: Tool retrieval over large API catalogs is a core bottleneck for LLM agents: user queries arrive in colloquial, often underspecified language, while the catalog uses technical API vocabulary that no fixed encoder can bridge on its own. The two dominant training approaches, contrastive encoder fine-tuning and HyDE-style query expansion with a frozen LLM, address this problem from opposite ends and fail in complementary directions: the fine-tuned encoder excels when the query's surface form already matches the catalog but collapses when it does not, while zero-shot HyDE is more robust to underspecified queries yet generates catalog-unaware hypothetical descriptions that degrade retrieval when queries are well-formed. We introduce CoHyDE, an iterative procedure that trains the dense encoder and the LLM rewriter as a single co-evolving system: the encoder is retrained with InfoNCE on catalog-style hypothetical descriptions produced by the rewriter, and the rewriter is preference-aligned via DPO against the encoder's retrieval scores, with both sides warm-started on the tool catalog before the loop begins. On a ~10k tool subset of the ToolBench catalog, three rounds of CoHyDE improve over the strongest single-component baseline by +2.5 pp NDCG@5 on standard queries and +6.3 pp on held-out vague queries, with gains as large as +8 pp on the hardest vague tier. Ablations confirm that co-training is the key ingredient: using either component in isolation fails to match CoHyDE on both well-formed and vague queries, with losses of up to -8 pp on vague queries.

</details>


### [353] [Diagnosing Harmful Continuation in Answer-Correct Long-CoT Training Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29288)
*Chen He,Yuhao Wu,Lei Wang,Wenxuan Zhang,Fumin Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies "harmful continuation" in long chain-of-thought (CoT) data—where answer-correct reasoning traces continue beyond necessary support—and shows that removing this suffix improves SFT outcomes. The authors propose HCC, a lightweight method to detect and cut these harmful continuations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Answer-correct long CoT traces can lead to varying fine-tuning outcomes, and the authors aim to understand why some traces are less effective for SFT despite being correct.

Method: A delete-only editor removes post-conclusion continuation (suffix) from answer-correct CoT traces, preserving the answer. SFT outcomes are compared between original and processed traces. The removed continuation is further characterized via uncertainty and hidden-state progress analysis.

Result: Removing post-conclusion continuation improves SFT outcomes. The removed continuation exhibits persistent local uncertainty with weakened terminal-directional progress (uncertainty-geometry mismatch).

Conclusion: Post-conclusion continuation is harmful for CoT-based SFT. HCC (Harmful Continuation Cut) is introduced as a lightweight proxy to approximate the boundary where harmful continuation begins.

Abstract: Long chain-of-thought (CoT) traces are widely used as supervision for reasoning-oriented LLM SFT, yet answer-correct traces can still lead to markedly different fine-tuning outcomes. We study post-conclusion continuation in answer-correct long-CoT data: a continuation where the answer appears sufficiently supported, but the trace continues with additional reasoning that remains in the supervised target. To test its training effect, we use a delete-only editor to construct answer-preserving suffix removal and compare CoT-based SFT on the original and processed traces. We observe improved SFT outcomes after removing the editor-identified post-conclusion continuation, suggesting that this continuation is harmful to training in our setting. We therefore refer to this empirically supported phenomenon as harmful continuation. Beyond this intervention, we further characterize the removed post-conclusion continuation through uncertainty and hidden-state progress. We observe persistent local uncertainty together with weakened terminal-directional progress, forming an uncertainty--geometry mismatch. Finally, we instantiate Harmful Continuation Cut (HCC), a lightweight boundary proxy that approximates the editor-identified post-conclusion continuation boundary.

</details>


### [354] [Entropy-KL Divergence-based Token Masking: A Novel Approach for Selective Fine-tuning of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29303)
*Qi Liu,Mingdi Sun,Yongyi He,Zhi Zheng,Tong Xu,Yi Zheng,Zhefeng Wang,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EKSFT selectively masks high-entropy/high-KL tokens during supervised fine-tuning to preserve pre-trained distribution, improving subsequent RL exploration for LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SFT followed by RL uses limited SFT data, causing models to overfit and shift from pre-trained distribution, which harms exploration in the RL stage.

Method: EKSFT selectively masks tokens with high entropy or high KL divergence from a reference model during SFT, preventing the model from overfitting to distribution-shifting tokens while still injecting task knowledge.

Result: EKSFT consistently outperforms standard SFT on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, and further RL fine-tuning from EKSFT yields better post-RL performance.

Conclusion: In low-data SFT regimes, focusing on activating task-relevant capabilities (via selective token masking) rather than memorization better preserves pre-trained distribution and enhances downstream RL exploration.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard post-training paradigm for large language models. This paradigm provides a cold-start for RL exploration, avoiding the inefficiency of pure RL where on-policy sampling yields insufficient positive samples. However, in practice, existing approaches often use a small amount of data for SFT initialization compared to the RL phase, which can cause the model to fit the limited samples and shift away from its pre-trained distribution. This distribution shift impedes the model's ability to effectively explore during subsequent RL training. To address this challenge, we propose that in low-data regimes, SFT should prioritize activating task-relevant capabilities rather than memorizing specific content. Along this line, we propose EKSFT (Entropy-KL Selective Fine-Tuning), which selectively masks tokens that exhibit either high entropy or high KL divergence from a reference model. By excluding these high-uncertainty, distribution-shifting tokens from imitation, EKSFT injects task-specific knowledge while preserving the integrity of the model's pre-trained distribution. Empirical evaluations on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that EKSFT consistently outperforms standard SFT. Further RL fine-tuning from the EKSFT model yields consistently better post-RL performance, indicating improved exploration for the RL stage. Our codes and datasets are available at https://github.com/MINE-USTC/EKSFT.

</details>


### [355] [Rubric-Guided Process Reward for Stepwise Model Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29310)
*Shenghao Ye,Yu Guo,Zhengheng Li,Shuangwu Chen,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RoRo introduces a rubric-guided process reward framework for stepwise model routing in Large Reasoning Models, using both outcome and process rewards to train the router more effectively than outcome-only methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing stepwise model routing methods use only outcome rewards (final answer correctness) to supervise the router, failing to evaluate intermediate routing decisions, which weakens performance and generalization.

Method: RoRo collects diverse routing trajectories, constructs preference pairs based on outcome, cost, and process quality, trains a Rubricor to generate query-specific rubrics and a Judge to score trajectories under those rubrics via alternating optimization, then combines process rewards with outcome rewards to optimize routing policy via GRPO.

Result: RoRo consistently outperforms strong baselines on five reasoning benchmarks under both same-family and cross-family settings, achieving better accuracy and cost trade-offs.

Conclusion: Incorporating process rewards through rubric-guided evaluation significantly improves stepwise model routing effectiveness over outcome-only reward approaches.

Abstract: Stepwise model routing improves the efficiency of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) by assigning each reasoning step to a suitable model. Recent methods formulate routing as a sequential decision process and train the router with reinforcement learning. However, although they model routing as a process, they still supervise the router with outcome rewards. Such rewards only reflect final answer correctness and fail to evaluate intermediate routing decisions, which can weaken performance and generalization. To address this gap, we propose RoRo, a rubric-guided process reward framework for stepwise model routing. RoRo first collects diverse routing trajectories and constructs preference pairs based on outcome, cost, and process quality. It then trains a Rubricor to generate a query-specific evaluation rubric and a Judge to score routing trajectories under this rubric through alternating optimization. The resulting process rewards are combined with outcome rewards to optimize the routing policy via GRPO. Experiments on five reasoning benchmarks under both same-family and cross-family settings show that RoRo consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves better accuracy and cost trade-offs.

</details>


### [356] [ConMoE: Expert-Pool Consolidation via Prototype Reassignment for MoE Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29350)
*Yilun Yao,Jiaming Pan,Elsie Dai,Peizhuang Cong,Yaoming Li,Tong Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ConMoE is a train-free framework that reduces MoE language model memory by selecting a small set of expert prototypes and deterministically remapping original expert calls to them, achieving competitive performance without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models reduce per-token computation but require storing all experts, making deployment memory-intensive. Existing compression methods (pruning or merging experts) have limitations.

Method: Formulates MoE compression as expert-pool consolidation: retain a smaller set of pretrained experts as prototypes, then deterministically remap original expert references to selected prototypes using calibration-based contribution and replaceability signals, without weight updates or fine-tuning.

Result: ConMoE matches or outperforms pruning/merging baselines on three MoE models (deepseek-moe-16b-base, Qwen3-30B-A3B, OLMoE-1B-7B-0125), achieving best average scores at 25% and 50% routed-expert reduction on deepseek-moe-16b-base.

Conclusion: Deterministic reassignment is the most stable component; cross-layer sharing and post-hoc weight fusion are model-dependent. ConMoE offers an effective, train-free compression alternative.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models reduce per-token computation but still require storing and serving all experts, making deployment memory-intensive. Existing post-training compression methods mainly shrink this cost by pruning experts or merging their weights. We formulate post-training MoE compression as expert-pool consolidation: retaining a smaller set of pretrained experts as reusable prototypes and deterministically remapping each original expert reference to one selected prototype. This view separates the reduced expert pool from the reuse structure that represents the original expert slots, and allows prototype sharing within local layer scopes while preserving the original router interface. We propose ConMoE, a train-free prototype remapping framework that selects retained experts using calibration-based contribution and replaceability signals, then redirects original expert calls to the selected prototypes without weight updates or post-compression fine-tuning. Experiments on three pretrained MoE language models show that ConMoE matches or outperforms strong pruning and merging baselines in several settings, achieving the best average score on deepseek-moe-16b-base at both 25% and 50% routed-expert reduction, while remaining competitive on Qwen3-30B-A3B and OLMoE-1B-7B-0125. Ablations indicate that deterministic reassignment is the most stable component, whereas broader cross-layer sharing and post-hoc weight fusion are model-dependent.

</details>


### [357] [PassNet: Scaling Large Language Models for Graph Compiler Pass Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29357)
*Yiqun Liu,Yingsheng Wu,Ruqi Yang,Enrong Zheng,Honglei Qiu,Sijun He,Tai Liang,Jingjing Wu,Yuhan Zhou,Yiwei Zhang,Dongyan Chen,Weihan Yi,Xinqi Li,Siqi Bao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PassNet is a large-scale ecosystem for LLM-based compiler pass generation, featuring 18K+ computational graphs from 100K real-world models. It reveals a 37% aggregate gap between the best LLM and TorchInductor, yet up to 3x speedup on individual subgraphs, showing consistency is the bottleneck. Fine-tuning on ~4K trajectories yields 2.67x improvement, approaching frontier-model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern tensor compilers (e.g., TorchInductor) underperform on 43% of long-tail subgraphs. Existing LLM-based optimization focuses on standalone kernel generation rather than structured graph transformations integrated into compiler pipelines.

Method: PassNet proposes pass generation (LLM-authored graph transformations) as the key abstraction. It includes: (1) PassNet-Dataset (18K+ graphs from 100K models), (2) PassBench (200 curated long-tail fusible tasks, 2,060 subgraphs), and (3) Error-aware Speedup Score (ES_t) metric with layered integrity defenses against LLM exploitation.

Result: Best frontier model trails TorchInductor by 37% aggregate. On individual subgraphs, LLMs achieve up to 3x speedup. Fine-tuning a small model on ~4K PassNet trajectories yields 2.67x improvement, approaching frontier-model performance.

Conclusion: The bottleneck in LLM-driven compiler optimization is consistency, not capability. PassNet provides live training infrastructure for advancing this area, with all data and benchmarks publicly available.
