Motivation: To enable secure distributed hypothesis testing where a central server can determine the hypothesis class of a distribution without learning any additional information about the distribution itself.

Method: Proves impossibility in the standard form, then augments the model with a shared secret key. Uses symmetric canonical reduction for simple classes with a single-bit key, and employs Private Simultaneous Messages (PSM) protocols for arbitrary hypothesis classes.

Result: Standard secure distributed hypothesis testing is impossible; a single-bit secret key enables perfect security for simple classes; arbitrary classes over finite domains can be achieved via PSM reduction with polynomial communication and key lengths.

Conclusion: Secure distributed hypothesis testing requires a shared secret key, but with such a key, practical solutions exist for both simple and arbitrary hypothesis classes.

Abstract: In distributed hypothesis testing, a central server performs hypothesis testing based on information received from distributed sensors/clients. We study a secure variant of this problem in which the central server determines the hypothesis class of an underlying distribution without learning any additional information about the distribution itself. We prove that, in its standard form, this is impossible to achieve, even for simple and highly restricted cases. To bypass this impossibility, we augment the model with a shared secret key available to clients but hidden from the server. We show that a single-bit secret key enables perfectly secure testing for simple classes by reducing the test distributions to a symmetric, canonical instance. Finally, for arbitrary hypothesis classes over finite domains, we establish a reduction to standard hypothesis testing using Private Simultaneous Messages (PSM) protocols, achieving polynomial communication and key lengths.

</details>


### [492] [Tackling Interference in HAPS Networks via Angular-Aware Clustering and RSMA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29813)
*Afsoon Alidadi Shamsabadi,Animesh Yadav,Halim Yanikomeroglu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes an angular-aware user clustering and interference-aware RB allocation framework with RSMA to manage interference in HAPS-empowered networks, achieving significant spectral efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interference management in HAPS-empowered networks requires special attention due to strong LoS conditions causing limited channel variability and intensified inter-user interference.

Method: The authors propose a framework that strategically clusters users based on angular information, designs beams per cluster, allocates RBs across clusters, and incorporates RSMA to mitigate intra-RB interference.

Result: The proposed clustering and RSMA-based approach significantly outperforms baseline schemes in terms of achievable per-user spectral efficiency.

Conclusion: The angular-aware clustering with RSMA is an effective solution for interference mitigation in HAPS multi-beam systems, enhancing spectral efficiency.

Abstract: High Altitude Platform Stations (HAPS) have emerged as a promising enabler for next-generation wireless networks, offering ubiquitous connectivity to ground users. Operating either in standalone mode or in integration with terrestrial networks, HAPS can significantly enhance both coverage and capacity due to their strategic placement in the stratosphere. However, interference management in HAPS-empowered networks requires special attention due to the unique propagation characteristics of HAPS links. In particular, the strong line-of-sight (LoS) conditions between HAPS and ground users result in limited channel variability, thereby intensifying inter-user interference. In this work, we consider a single HAPS serving multiple ground users through multiple beams over a limited number of orthogonal resource blocks (RBs). To address the resulting interference, we propose a novel angular-aware user clustering and interference-aware RB allocation framework that strategically clusters users, designs beams to serve each cluster, and allocates RBs to users across clusters. To further mitigate intra-RB interference, a rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) scheme is incorporated. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed clustering and RSMA-based approach significantly outperforms baseline schemes in terms of achievable per-user spectral efficiency.

</details>


### [493] [On the Effect of Pulse Shaping Filters in Zak-OTFS Waveform for Radar Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29824)
*Abhishek Bairwa,Ananthanarayanan Chockalingam*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper investigates alternative pulse shaping filters (sinc and Gaussian-sinc) for Zak-OTFS radar, showing they offer better target resolvability with narrower main lobes compared to Gaussian filters, especially in dense target scenarios and when using interference-mitigation receivers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gaussian filters in Zak-OTFS radar have wide main lobes that compromise target resolvability; the authors seek filters with better ambiguity characteristics to improve performance in multi-target scenarios.

Method: The authors derive closed-form expressions for self-ambiguity functions of Zak-OTFS waveforms with sinc and Gaussian-sinc (GS) filters, and compare their performance against Gaussian filters under different target density scenarios and receiver types (peak-detection vs. interference-mitigation).

Result: Sinc and GS filters provide narrower main lobes, yielding better resolvability in dense target scenes with peak-detection receivers. Gaussian filters perform better in sparse scenes due to low sidelobes. With interference-mitigation receivers, sinc and GS outperform Gaussian in both dense and sparse scenarios.

Conclusion: The choice of pulse shaping filter should depend on target density and receiver processing; sinc and GS filters are preferable for dense targets or when interference mitigation is available, while Gaussian remains competitive for sparse scenes with simple receivers.

Abstract: In radar sensing, the self-ambiguity function of the probing waveform plays a crucial role in the resolvability and detection of multiple targets. In the recent Zak-OTFS based radar literature, Gaussian pulse shaping filter has been considered, and it has been shown to offer better range/velocity estimation performance compared to the traditional chirp waveform in scenes with multiple targets. While the self-ambiguity function with Gaussian filter has very low side lobes, its main lobe is wide which compromises resolvability and performance. Motivated by this, we seek filters with better ambiguity characteristics. Specifically, we explore two other known filters, namely, sinc and Gaussian-sinc (GS) filters, and demonstrate that these filters offer better performance compared to Gaussian filter under different scenarios and receiver processing. Towards demonstrating this, we derive closed-form expressions for the self-ambiguity functions of Zak-OTFS waveform with sinc and GS filters. The ambiguity functions of sinc and GS filtered waveforms have narrow main lobes, resulting in better resolvability in scenes with densely populated targets for the basic peak-detection based receiver. The ambiguity function of Gaussian filtered waveform has very low sidelobes, resulting in better performance in sparsely populated scenes. When a receiver with inter-target interference mitigation is used, the sinc and GS filters perform better in both dense and sparsely populated scenes compared to Gaussian filter.

</details>


### [494] [Gesture-Aware Indoor THz ISAC Systems for Adaptive Resource Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29913)
*Zhonghao Liu,Yinchao Yang,Yahao Ding,Yixuan Wang,Mohammad Shikh-Bahaei*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes an adaptive joint optimization algorithm for power allocation and beamforming in a THz multi-user ISAC system that uses EKF-based gesture recognition to dynamically adjust resource allocation, maximizing sensing SINR while meeting gesture-dependent communication QoS constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable adaptive communication based on gesture recognition in multi-user indoor THz ISAC systems, improving both sensing accuracy and communication efficiency through dynamic resource allocation.

Method: The system uses an extended Kalman filter (EKF) for gesture tracking, and develops an adaptive joint optimization algorithm for power allocation and beamforming to maximize sensing SINR under gesture-dependent QoS constraints.

Result: The proposed method achieves superior sensing accuracy and communication performance compared to conventional single-variable optimization baselines, effectively responding to gesture dynamics.

Conclusion: The adaptive joint optimization approach is effective for multi-user THz ISAC systems, demonstrating that gesture-aware resource allocation can simultaneously enhance sensing and communication performance.

Abstract: This paper investigates a multi-user indoor integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system operating in the terahertz (THz) band, designed for adaptive communication based on gesture recognition. Leveraging gesture tracking through an extended Kalman filter (EKF), the access point (AP) dynamically adjusts resource allocation in response to detected gesture variations, thereby improving sensing accuracy. Based on the gesture recognition results, the AP further updates the communication quality requirements of different users, enabling efficient resource allocation. To this end, an adaptive joint optimization algorithm for power allocation and beamforming is developed to maximize the overall sensing signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) while satisfying the gesture-dependent communication quality of service (QoS) constraints. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method effectively responds to gesture dynamics, achieving superior sensing accuracy and communication performance compared with conventional single-variable optimization baselines.

</details>


### [495] [CRB-Guided Framework Design and Resource Allocation for Indoor mmWave ISCC Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29939)
*Zhonghao Liu,Yahao Ding,Yinchao Yang,Mohammad Shikh-Bahaei*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes a CRB-guided resource allocation framework for mmWave ISCC systems that jointly optimizes sensing power and model depth to minimize human pose prediction error under communication, latency, and energy constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable continuous human tracking and resource allocation in advance for indoor human-centric ISCC applications, there is a need to minimize human pose prediction error while operating under stringent resource constraints.

Method: The authors characterize range-estimation uncertainty via CRB, adopt an adaptive-depth Mamba-based pose prediction model with lightweight heads, establish a quantitative relationship among sensing power, model depth, and prediction error, and develop an AO-based algorithm with closed-form solutions.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate significant reduction in pose prediction error compared to baseline methods, validating effectiveness for resource-constrained ISCC systems.

Conclusion: The proposed CRB-guided resource allocation framework effectively minimizes human pose prediction error in indoor mmWave ISCC systems by jointly optimizing sensing and computation resources.

Abstract: Integrated sensing, communication, and computation (ISCC) provides a promising framework for indoor human-centric applications. In these applications, short-term human pose prediction facilitates continuous human tracking and resource allocation in advance. In this paper, we propose a Cramer-Rao bound (CRB) guided resource allocation framework for indoor mmWave ISCC systems to minimize the human pose prediction error under communication, latency, and energy constraints. We characterize the impact of sensing power on range-estimation uncertainty and point-cloud perturbation based on the CRB. To capture the impact of computation resources on prediction performance, we adopt an adaptive-depth Mamba-based pose prediction model, where lightweight prediction heads are attached after every layer to enable inference with different model depths. With this unified sensing-computation modeling, we establish a quantitative relationship among sensing power, model depth, and prediction error. Furthermore, we formulate a joint resource allocation problem to minimize the pose prediction error. To solve this problem efficiently, we develop an alternating optimization (AO)-based algorithm, where closed-form solutions are derived for the sensing power and model depth update steps. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme significantly reduces pose prediction error compared with baseline methods, validating its effectiveness for resource-constrained indoor human-centric ISCC systems.

</details>


### [496] [Low-Overhead Receiver Design for Data-Dependent Superimposed Training via Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29995)
*Xinjie Li,Xingyu Zhou,Jing Zhang,Chao-Kai Wen,Xiao Li,Shi Jin*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a low-overhead transmission framework that enhances data-dependent superimposed training (DDST) with non-iterative pilot-data decoupling for quasi-static channels, a mix transmission scheme combining DDST and orthogonal pilots for fast-varying channels, and a Vision Transformer-based neural receiver to capture structured interference, achieving significant gains in low-to-medium SNR with better computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Superimposed pilot (SIP) transmission eliminates dedicated pilot overhead, improving spectral efficiency, but suffers from severe pilot-data coupling that creates a critical performance-complexity bottleneck at the receiver.

Method: (1) Enhanced DDST receiver with non-iterative pilot-data decoupling using data-dependent algebraic structures for quasi-static channels. (2) Mix transmission scheme applying DDST to a subset of REs to combine OP's interference-free property with SIP's zero-pilot-overhead advantage. (3) Vision Transformer-based neural receiver to capture orthogonal structure and channel correlations, relaxing quasi-static assumptions.

Result: The proposed framework achieves significant performance gains in the low-to-medium SNR regime under time-varying channels while providing superior computational efficiency compared to state-of-the-art SIP receivers.

Conclusion: The proposed low-overhead transmission framework effectively addresses the pilot-data coupling bottleneck of SIP, offering a practical solution for both quasi-static and fast time-varying channels with improved performance and efficiency.

Abstract: Superimposed pilot (SIP) transmission improves spectral efficiency by eliminating the dedicated pilot overhead required in orthogonal pilot (OP)-based schemes. However, SIP suffers from severe pilot-data coupling, which leads to a critical performance-complexity bottleneck at the receiver. To address this issue, this paper proposes a low-overhead transmission framework that revitalizes data-dependent superimposed training (DDST) with enhanced interference mitigation strategies. First, for quasi-static block-fading channels, an enhanced DDST receiver is developed to achieve non-iterative pilot-data decoupling by exploiting data-dependent algebraic structures. Second, to overcome the sensitivity of conventional DDST to channel variations and symbol misidentification in fast time-varying environments, a mix transmission scheme is developed. By strategically applying DDST to a subset of resource elements, the proposed scheme combines the interference-free transmission property of OP with the zero-pilot-overhead advantage of SIP, thereby improving demapping reliability and interference suppression. Furthermore, under the proposed mix scheme, a Vision Transformer-based neural receiver is designed to capture the orthogonal structure between pilots and perturbation-bearing data, as well as the underlying channel correlations, thereby relaxing the stringent quasi-static assumption required for interference disentanglement. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves significant performance gains in the low-to-medium SNR regime under time-varying channels while providing superior computational efficiency compared with state-of-the-art SIP receivers.

</details>


### [497] [List Recovery for Random Low-Rate Linear Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30101)
*Isaac M Hair,Amit Sahai*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proves that random low-rate linear codes over large prime fields achieve near-optimal list recovery simultaneously for exponentially large input list sizes, with a nearly matching lower bound showing the result is tight up to logarithmic factors in the exponent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To establish list recovery guarantees for random linear codes, a property important for coding theory and combinatorial applications, and to determine how large input list sizes can be while still maintaining recoverability.

Method: The proof combines a deterministic graph-theoretic certificate (inspired by Matoušek, Přívětivý, and Škovroň), a nonvanishing determinant criterion, and the Schwartz--Zippel lemma. A lower bound is also provided via a combinatorial argument.

Result: Random d-dimensional linear codes over large prime fields are (α,ℓ,(1+ε)/(1-α)·ℓ)-list recoverable for ℓ ≤ 2^{O_{α,ε,d}(n/log n)} simultaneously. Any linear code with dimension ≥2 cannot achieve this for ℓ ≥ 2^{Ω_{α,ε}(n)}, showing near-optimality.

Conclusion: Random low-rate linear codes achieve nearly optimal list recovery, with the result being tight up to logarithmic factors in the exponent of the list size.

Abstract: We prove a list recovery guarantee for random low-rate linear codes over sufficiently large prime fields. For fixed dimension $d$, error fraction $α$, and accuracy parameter $\varepsilon$, a random $d$-dimensional linear code $C \subseteq \mathbb{F}_p^n$ is, with high probability, $(α,\ell,\frac{1+\varepsilon}{1-α}\ell)$-list recoverable simultaneously for all input list sizes $\ell\le 2^{O_{α, \varepsilon, d}(n/\log n)}$. The proof is inspired by work of Matoušek, Přívětivý, and Škovroň on reconstructing point sets from their projections. It combines a deterministic graph-theoretic certificate, a nonvanishing determinant criterion, and the Schwartz--Zippel lemma. We also give a lower bound showing that any linear code $C \subseteq \mathbb{F}_p^n$ of dimension at least two cannot be $(α,\ell,\frac{1+\varepsilon}{1-α}\ell)$-list recoverable for feasible list sizes $\ell \geq 2^{Ω_{α, \varepsilon}(n)}$. In this sense, our result is nearly optimal.

</details>


### [498] [Majorization precursors to supermodularity and subadditivity on the majorization lattice](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30331)
*Alexander Stévins,Michael G. Jabbour,Serge Deside,Nicolas J. Cerf*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper establishes two structural majorization relations (precursors) that show sum-concave functions are supermodular and subadditive on the majorization lattice. Using these, the authors prove supermodularity and subadditivity of Tsallis entropies (all α) and Rényi entropies (α>1), recovering these for Shannon entropy, and strengthen these to strict versions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover the fundamental structural relations underlying supermodularity and subadditivity on the majorization lattice, particularly for entropic functionals.

Method: The authors establish two precursor majorization relations, then apply them to prove supermodularity and subadditivity properties of sum-concave functions and entropies (Tsallis, Rényi, Shannon).

Result: (i) All sum-concave functions are supermodular and subadditive on the majorization lattice. (ii) Tsallis entropies (all α) and Rényi entropies (α>1) are supermodular and subadditive. (iii) These entropies are strictly subadditive, and Tsallis/Shannon entropies are strictly supermodular.

Conclusion: The precursor relations provide a unifying framework for understanding supermodularity and subadditivity of entropic functionals on the majorization lattice, with strict versions strengthening known results.

Abstract: We establish two structural majorization relations, which we call precursors, underlying the properties of supermodularity and subadditivity on the lattice induced by majorization. These are precursors in that they immediately imply that all sums of concave functions, which we dub sum-concave functions, are supermodular and subadditive on the majorization lattice. Using these majorization relations, we then show the supermodularity and subadditivity (in the lattice-theoretic sense) of Tsallis entropies (for all $α$) and Rényi entropies (for all $α> 1$), also recovering these properties for the Shannon entropy in the process. We further strengthen these inequalities, showing that: (i) all these entropic functionals are strictly subadditive on the majorization lattice; (ii) Tsallis entropies (and therefore the Shannon entropy as well) are strictly supermodular on the majorization lattice.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [499] [FRAPPE: Full Input, Residual Output Autoencoding with Projection Pursuit Encoder](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28992)
*Dan Jacobellis,Neeraja J. Yadwadkar*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: FRAPPE is a novel autoencoding framework using Projection Pursuit Encoder that predicts residual output from full input, enabling variable-rate coding with parallel DAG-based encoding, achieving 47x faster encoding than AVIF at high compression ratios with real-time 1080p CPU-only performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Media compression standards have plateaued in rate-distortion-complexity trade-off, limiting cloud offloading of AI perception for resource-constrained platforms like robotics and wearables. Existing DNN codecs struggle with bitrate adaptation and require expensive GPUs for real-time encoding.

Method: FRAPPE uses a Projection Pursuit Encoder that predicts residual output from full input, with latent channels naturally sorted by importance for zero-overhead variable-rate coding. Its analysis path is an embarrassingly parallel DAG of independent input projections, unlike sequential RNN or RVQ approaches.

Result: At high compression ratios (~0.1 bpp), FRAPPE-Image provides higher perceptual quality than AVIF with 47x faster encoding, enabling real-time 1080p 30fps CPU-only encoding.

Conclusion: FRAPPE achieves a superior rate-distortion-complexity trade-off, making learned video compression practical for resource-constrained platforms without specialized hardware.

Abstract: Media compression standards have reached a plateau in terms of the rate-distortion-complexity trade-off, limiting the ability to offload expensive AI perception to the cloud in applications like robotics, wearables, and remote sensing. DNN-based codecs improve compression efficiency, but at a cost: they cannot easily adapt to large changes in available bitrate, and real-time encoding requires expensive, power-hungry GPUs that prohibit use on low-cost or resource-constrained platforms. To address these limitations, we propose a novel autoencoding framework (FRAPPE) that uses the Full input to predict the Residual output via a Projection Pursuit Encoder. FRAPPE's encoding objective naturally sorts latent channels by importance, allowing zero-overhead variable-rate coding. Unlike RNN-based learned codecs, whose encoder consumes the previous reconstruction's residual, or RVQ-style codecs, whose codebooks must be applied sequentially, FRAPPE's analysis path is an embarrassingly parallel DAG of independent input projections. Using FRAPPE, we build a variable-rate RGB image codec (FRAPPE-Image), and evaluate its rate-distortion-complexity trade-off against standard image codecs. At high compression ratios (approx. 0.1 bpp) FRAPPE-Image provides higher perceptual quality than AVIF with 47 times faster encoding, making it capable of real-time 1080p, 30fps CPU-only encoding. Our code and pre-trained models are available: https://github.com/UT-SysML/FRAPPE .

</details>


### [500] [Accelerating HEVC Intra Partitioning via a CNN-Hierarchical Attention Transformer Hybrid](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29063)
*Krishna Kumar Sharma,Somdyuti Paul*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: HFViT is a hybrid CNN-Transformer architecture that accelerates HEVC intra-mode partition prediction by fusing reparameterized depthwise-separable convolutions with a Hierarchical Attention Transformer and carrier token scheme, achieving better VMAF BD-rate savings (2.4-7.9 percentage points) than CNN baselines while keeping CPU latency within 8% of CNN and outpacing it by 40% on GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HEVC partition prediction faces a trade-off: CNNs are efficient but spatially myopic (cannot capture long-range dependencies), while transformers capture global context but incur prohibitive CPU latency, hindering deployment in CPU-bound encoder environments.

Method: HFViT fuses a reparameterized depthwise-separable convolutional backbone with a Hierarchical Attention Transformer (HAT) mechanism using a carrier token scheme for efficient global information propagation at sub-quadratic complexity, plus post-training structural fusion to collapse batch normalization.

Result: On JCT-VC sequences, HFViT reduces VMAF BD-rate penalty by 2.4pp (Class A), 2.6pp (Class B), and 7.9pp (Class E) vs. ETH-CNN baseline. CPU latency within 8% of CNN; GPU latency 40% better than CNN.

Conclusion: The hybrid approach successfully balances global context capture and computational efficiency, establishing practical viability for real-time HEVC encoder integration on CPU-bound systems.

Abstract: The recursive quad-tree partitioning in High Efficiency Video Coding (HEVC) incurs considerable computational overhead, with exhaustive rate-distortion optimization for CTU partition prediction consuming the dominant share of encoding time. Although partition prediction through deep learning has emerged as a viable encoding accelerator, an architectural dichotomy remains largely unaddressed: CNNs are computationally efficient but spatially myopic due to their localized effective receptive fields, failing to capture long range semantic relationships and repetitive textures; conversely, transformer based architectures are better at capturing global context but incur prohibitive CPU latency, a critical liability that impedes deployment which is predominantly CPU-bound. This paper introduces Hybrid Fast Vision Transformer (HFViT), a hybrid architecture designed to accelerate HEVC intra-mode partition prediction. HFViT fuses a reparameterized depthwise-separable convolutional backbone with a Hierarchical Attention Transformer (HAT) mechanism, leveraging a carrier token scheme to enable efficient global information propagation at sub-quadratic complexity. Post-training structural fusion collapses batch normalization into preceding layers to further reduce latency. Comprehensive evaluation reveals the efficacy of HFViT in accelerating HEVC intra-encoding across resolutions. On standard JCT-VC test sequences, HFViT reduces the average VMAF BD-rate penalty by 2.4, 2.6, and 7.9 percentage points on Classes A, B and E, respectively, as compared to the competing ETH-CNN baseline while maintaining CPU inference latency within 8% of the CNN baseline and surpassing it on GPU by 40%, establishing practical viability for real-time encoder integration.

</details>


### [501] [BCER Agent: Reliable Long-Horizon MRI Workflow Execution via Compilation, Artifact Binding, and Bounded Local Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29163)
*Ziyang Long,Xinqi Li,Junzhou Chen,Yifan Gao,Debiao Li,Hsin-Jung Yang*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: BCER is a controller architecture for reliable long-horizon MRI analysis that decouples high-level planning from execution with bounded local recovery, outperforming reactive baselines on multi-organ MRI tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical VLM/agent benchmarks focus on 2D images or short tool-calling exchanges, but real MRI analysis requires long, interdependent 3D/4D pipelines where reactive agents suffer from cascading breakdowns due to faulty references, mismatched arguments, and poor cross-step dependency control.

Method: BCER (Brain-Cerebellum-Extremity-Reflector) decouples high-level planning from execution and provides bounded local recovery for dependable long-horizon MRI workflow execution.

Result: On a multi-organ MRI benchmark (brain, prostate, cardiac), BCER shows consistent improvements in end-to-end execution over reactive baselines, with largest gains on long-chain workflows. It also enables auditability by linking final outputs to intermediate artifacts.

Conclusion: BCER effectively addresses cascading breakdowns in long-horizon MRI workflows, achieving reliable execution and auditability, with code and benchmark publicly released.

Abstract: Many recent medical VLM and agent studies are benchmarked on 2D images or comparatively short tool-calling exchanges, whereas real MRI analysis typically demands long, interdependent pipelines that operate on 3D/4D volumetric data. Under these conditions, reactive tool-calling agents are prone to cascading breakdowns triggered by faulty intermediate references, mismatched tool arguments, and limited control over cross-step dependencies. To address this, we introduce BCER (Brain-Cerebellum-Extremity-Reflector), a controller architecture aimed at dependable long-horizon MRI workflow execution. BCER decouples high-level planning from execution and provides bounded local recovery. We assess BCER on a multi-organ MRI benchmark covering brain, prostate, and cardiac tasks with both short- and long-chain workflows, using matched task contracts across controller variants and several backbone models. Relative to reactive baselines, BCER yields consistent improvements in end-to-end execution, with the most pronounced gains observed on long-chain workflows. BCER additionally enables auditability by maintaining explicit links between final outputs and intermediate artifacts and measurements. Code and benchmark are released at https://github.com/Albertlongzi/BCER.

</details>


### [502] [Constructing efficient channels for ideal observers using the conjugate gradient method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29415)
*Weimin Zhou*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper presents a conjugate gradient-based method for constructing efficient channels to approximate Ideal Observer and Hotelling Observer performance in task-based medical image quality assessment, addressing computational challenges with high-dimensional image data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Task-based assessment of image quality is critical for medical imaging system design, but ideal observers (IO and HO) are computationally intractable for high-dimensional data, motivating the need for efficient channel-based approximations.

Method: The authors propose a conjugate gradient (CG)-based method to construct efficient channels for approximating IO and HO performance in signal detection tasks.

Result: The CG-based method provides an effective framework for dimensionality reduction that facilitates the computation of ideal observers.

Conclusion: The proposed approach enables practical application of ideal observer approximations for task-based image quality assessment in medical imaging systems.

Abstract: Task-based assessment of image quality (IQ) is critically important for the design and optimization of medical imaging systems. Ideal observers, including the Bayesian Ideal Observer (IO) and the ideal linear observer, i.e., the Hotelling observer (HO), provide objective figures of merit (FOMs) that quantify system performance on signal detection tasks. However, the application of ideal observers to high-dimensional image data is often computationally intractable. Channel mechanisms provide an effective framework for dimensionality reduction that can facilitate the computation of ideal observers. This work presents a conjugate gradient (CG)-based method to construct efficient channels for approximating the IO and HO performance.

</details>


### [503] [A unified deeplearning framework for contrast-phase-specific virtual monochromatic imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29753)
*Antony Jerald,Hemant K Aggarwal,Brian Nett,Avinash Gopal,Phaneendra K Yalavarthy,Bipul Das,Rajesh Langoju*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a unified deep learning framework to synthesize virtual monochromatic 50 keV images from single-energy CT data across four contrast phases, using contrast phase information as a prior to improve contrast enhancement without requiring dual-energy CT hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DECT provides improved contrast resolution via VMI but has limited clinical adoption due to high hardware complexity and cost. The authors aim to achieve similar benefits using widely available SECT systems.

Method: A unified deep learning model trained on DECT-derived 70 keV and 50 keV image pairs across four contrast phases (Angio, Arterial, Portal, Delayed). It uses a prior conditioning architecture that integrates contrast phase information as a prior into the energy transformation process.

Result: The unified model achieves contrast enhancement and generalizes well across different contrast phases. It can generate 50 keV-like images from SECT inputs while preserving contrast phase-specific dynamics.

Conclusion: The proposed framework can effectively synthesize contrast-phase-specific VMI from SECT data, potentially enabling wider clinical adoption of VMI benefits without requiring dedicated DECT hardware.

Abstract: Dual-energy CT (DECT) enables virtual monochromatic imaging (VMI) and improved contrast resolution, but its clinical adoption is limited by hardware complexity and cost. In this work, we propose a unified deep learning framework that synthesizes contrast-phase-specific virtual monochromatic 50 keV images from single-energy CT (SECT) data by leveraging contrast phase information as a prior. The model is trained using DECT-derived 70 keV and 50 keV image pairs across four contrast phases -- Angio, Arterial, Portal, and Delayed -- using a novel prior conditioning architecture that integrates contrast phase priors into the energy transformation process. We demonstrate that the proposed unified model achieves contrast enhancement and generalizes well across contrast phases. Additionally, we show that the model can generate 50 keV-like images from SECT inputs, preserving contrast phase-specific dynamics.

</details>


### [504] [Absorption and Phase-Contrast Microtomography Using Direct X-ray Detection With COTS CMOS Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29808)
*Damian L. Corzi,Jose Lipovetzky,Fabricio Alcalde Bessia,German Mato,Andres Cicuttin,Maria L. Crespo,Martin Perez,Mariano Gomez Berisso*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper presents a low-cost, high-resolution X-ray microtomography system using off-the-shelf CMOS sensors as direct detectors, achieving 3.9-5.2 μm voxel sizes and phase-contrast imaging without complex optics, validated as a viable alternative to expensive synchrotron or nanofocus systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a simpler, lower-cost alternative to synchrotron or nanofocus X-ray microtomography systems that avoids complex optics and slow scans while maintaining high-resolution imaging capabilities.

Method: Uses COTS CMOS image sensors as direct detectors with a microfocus X-ray source in cone-beam geometry, combined with a dynamic flat-field correction algorithm to address radiation-induced degradation in consumer-grade hardware.

Result: Achieved voxel sizes from 3.9 μm to 5.2 μm, enabled both absorption-contrast and propagation-based phase-contrast imaging, and successfully visualized soft tissue boundaries undetectable by conventional radiography.

Conclusion: COTS CMOS sensors are a viable alternative for laboratory-scale high-resolution microtomography, offering a simpler and more cost-effective solution compared to traditional high-end systems.

Abstract: This work presents a high-resolution X-ray microtomography system that uses commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) CMOS image sensors as direct detectors, relying on the sensor s intrinsic resolution to achieve tomographic reconstructions without optical components. The system employs a microfocus X-ray source in cone-beam geometry, enabling both absorption-contrast and propagation-based phase-contrast imaging. A dynamic flat-field correction algorithm mitigates radiation-induced degradation during long acquisitions, helping to overcome limitations of consumer-grade hardware. The setup provides voxel sizes from 3.9 micron to 5.2.micron. Phase contrast visualizes soft tissue boundaries that would be undetectable by conventional radiography. Compared to synchrotron or nanofocus systems, our solution is simpler, lower-cost, and avoids complex optics or slow scans. COTS CMOS sensors appear as a viable alternative for laboratory-scale high-resolution microtomography.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [505] [Dimming Space-Time Code (DSTC) for Visible Light Communication with Semi-Blind Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29085)
*Igor S. C. Rodrigues,Leandro R. Ximenes,André L. F. de Almeida*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a dimming space-time code (DSTC) for CSK-based VLC that enables dimming control while preserving color, and develops two receivers (ZF and tensor-based PARAFAC) showing diversity gains and reduced training overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dimming in CSK-based VLC systems is challenging and underexplored because average optical power must be controlled without altering perceived chromaticity, which is critical for practical deployment.

Method: The paper proposes a DSTC with a structured dimming matrix that controls temporal power variations while satisfying physical feasibility, color preservation, and identifiability. Two receivers are developed: pilot-assisted ZF and tensor-based semi-blind PARAFAC using only one training slot.

Result: DSTC provides diversity gains and substantial BER reductions over conventional CSK; the tensor-based receiver improves spectral efficiency by reducing training overhead, especially beneficial in large-scale MIMO configurations.

Conclusion: The proposed DSTC framework jointly satisfies communication and lighting constraints in CSK-VLC systems, offering practical dimming control with improved reliability and efficiency.

Abstract: Visible light communication (VLC) provides a unified framework for wireless data transmission and illumination, but its practical deployment requires transmission schemes that jointly satisfy communication and lighting constraints. In color-shift keying (CSK) systems, dimming remains a challenging and underexplored problem because the average optical power must be controlled without altering the perceived chromaticity. This paper proposes a dimming space-time code (DSTC) for CSK-based VLC systems, where a structured dimming matrix introduces controlled temporal power variations while satisfying physical feasibility, color preservation, and identifiability conditions. Two receiver architectures are developed: a pilot-assisted zero-forcing (ZF) receiver and a tensor-based semi-blind PARAFAC receiver that jointly estimates the channel and transmitted symbols using only one training time slot. Simulation results show that the proposed DSTC provides diversity gains and substantial BER reductions with respect to conventional CSK, while the tensor-based receiver improves spectral efficiency by reducing training overhead, with particular benefits in large-scale MIMO configurations.

</details>


### [506] [Low-Complexity Tensor-Based Monostatic Sensing for IRS-Assisted Communication Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29164)
*Kenneth B. A. Benício,Bruno Sokal,André L. F. de Almeida,Fazal-E-Asim,Behrooz Makki,Gábor Fodor*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a tensor-based parameter estimation algorithm for IRS-assisted sensing that uses HOSVD to jointly estimate delay, Doppler, and angular information with lower complexity than baseline methods while maintaining similar performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a low-complexity parameter estimation algorithm for intelligent reflecting surface-assisted sensing systems that can jointly estimate target's delay, Doppler, and angular information.

Method: A tensor-based approach using higher-order singular value decomposition (HOSVD) that exploits the multilinear structure of the received echo signal. The method enables parallel computation for individual parameter estimation.

Result: The proposed tensor-based method achieves the same estimation performance as the baseline method while drastically reducing computational complexity.

Conclusion: Exploiting the intrinsic tensor structure of sensed signals enables efficient parameter estimation with significant complexity reduction without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: This paper proposes a tensor-based parameter estimation algorithm for sensing in an intelligent reflecting surface-assisted system. We present a higher-order singular value decomposition-based solution that exploits the tensor structure of the received echo signal to jointly estimate the target's delay, Doppler, and angular information. Our tensor-based solution can estimate the parameters individually at low complexity, benefiting from parallel computation. Complexity analysis is carried out in comparison with a baseline scheme that does not exploit the intrinsic multilinear structure of the sensed signal. Simulation results show that our proposed tensor-based method can achieve the same performance as the reference method while drastically reducing the computational complexity.

</details>


### [507] [PARAFAC-Based Time-Varying Channel Estimation for IRS-Aided Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29171)
*Kenneth B. A. Benício,André L. F. de Almeida,Bruno Sokal,Fazal-E-Asim,Behrooz Makki,Gabor Fodor*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a tensor-based parametric channel estimation technique for IRS-assisted communication systems using a 3rd-order PARAFAC tensor model solved by ALS algorithm, achieving better NMSE performance without increasing computational complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance channel estimation performance for IRS-assisted communication systems with time-varying channel parameters by exploiting the multidimensional structure of received signals.

Method: Develops a 3rd-order PARAFAC tensor model of the received signal and solves it using the iterative Alternating Least Squares (ALS) algorithm to estimate time-varying channel parameters.

Result: The proposed approach achieves improved Normalized Mean Square Error (NMSE) performance for the concatenated channel compared to competing solutions.

Conclusion: The tensor-based approach capitalizes on the intrinsic multidimensional structure of the received signal, providing enhanced channel estimation without increasing computational complexity.

Abstract: This paper proposes a tensor-based parametric channel estimation technique for IRS-assisted communication systems with time-varying channel parameters. We exploit the multidimensional structure of the received signal by developing a $3$rd-order PARAFAC tensor model that is solved by employing the iteratively ALS algorithm. Our simulation results show that the proposed approach provides enhanced performance in terms of NMSE of the concatenated channel compared to the competing solutions by capitalizing on the intrinsic tensor structure of the received signal without increasing the computational complexity of the channel estimation.

</details>


### [508] [Channel Estimation for Flexible Intelligent Metasurface Aided MIMO Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29227)
*Vinícius L. Romano,André L. F. de Almeida,Daniel C. Araújo*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a channel estimation framework for MIMO systems with active Flexible Intelligent Metasurfaces (FIMs) that morph to provide new spatial degrees of freedom, using a split single-time-scale training protocol and PARAFAC decomposition with ALS algorithm to estimate steering matrices and path gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flexible Intelligent Metasurfaces (FIMs) enable wireless systems to adapt their 3D geometry through morphing, offering new spatial degrees of freedom, but continuous deformation complicates accurate CSI acquisition.

Method: A multidimensional framework for MIMO systems with active FIM arrays at both ends, using a split single-time-scale training protocol that sequentially introduces spatial variation by morphing the receiver then transmitter. The signal model is formulated as PARAFAC decomposition and solved via alternating least squares (ALS) algorithm.

Result: Numerical results show the proposed channel estimation method yields accurate CSI recovery for different system setups.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively handles the channel estimation challenge posed by continuously deforming FIMs, enabling accurate CSI recovery.

Abstract: Flexible Intelligent Metasurfaces (FIMs) enable wireless systems to adapt their three-dimensional geometry through morphing, thereby providing new spatial degrees of freedom. However, continuous deformation complicates the accurate acquisition of Channel State Information (CSI). This work proposes a multidimensional framework for MIMO systems with active FIM arrays at both the transmitter and receiver. A split single-time-scale training protocol sequentially introduces spatial variation by morphing the receiver, then the transmitter. The resulting signal model is formulated as a PARAFAC decomposition, and an alternating least squares (ALS) algorithm is employed to estimate steering matrices and path gains. Our numerical results show that the proposed channel estimation method yields accurate CSI recovery for different system setups.

</details>


### [509] [MIMO-OTFS-Based Semantic Communication for High-Mobility Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29332)
*Yu Zhang,Jiarui Yan,Yue Liu,Tenglun Ke,Yimeng Wang,Zhijin Qin*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A semantic communication framework combining MIMO-OTFS with semantic-aware sub-channel allocation to improve performance in high-mobility doubly-selective channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantic communication systems suffer significant performance degradation in high-mobility scenarios with time-frequency doubly-selective channels.

Method: (1) Uses an entropy module to evaluate semantic feature importance; (2) Employs Kendall correlation coefficient to quantify alignment between semantic importance and sub-channel conditions; (3) Jointly optimizes encoder/decoder via a loss function balancing classification accuracy, reconstruction quality, and sub-channel matching.

Result: The proposed framework achieves superior reconstruction quality compared to conventional OFDM-based semantic communication systems in high-mobility channel environments.

Conclusion: MIMO-OTFS with semantic-aware sub-channel allocation effectively mitigates performance degradation in high-mobility doubly-selective channels.

Abstract: In high-mobility scenarios with time-frequency doubly-selective channels, existing semantic communication systems suffer significant performance degradation. To address this issue, we propose a semantic communication framework that synergistically integrates multiple-input multiple-output orthogonal time frequency space (MIMO-OTFS) with semantic-aware sub-channel allocation. First, an entropy module is employed to evaluate importance of different semantic features, and the Kendall correlation coefficient is used to quantify the alignment between semantic importance and sub-channel conditions. Subsequently, joint optimization of the encoder and decoder is achieved through a comprehensive loss function that balances image classification accuracy, reconstruction quality, and sub-channel matching degree. Experimental results confirm the superior reconstruction quality of our proposed framework compared to conventional semantic communication systems based on orthogonal frequency division multiplexing in high-mobility channel environment.

</details>


### [510] [Iterative Reduced-Rank MMSE Estimation of Sparse Range Profiles from Non-Contiguous Radar Transmission Spectra](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29364)
*Gordon Ariho,Hara Madhav Talasila,James M. Stiles,Peng Seng Tan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method for designing non-contiguous radar transmission spectra (using Marginal Fisher Information criterion) and a reduced-rank MMSE estimator for range profile estimation from sparse measurements, achieving near-full-spectrum MSE performance with 50-75% spectral occupancy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Increasing demand for radio spectrum by commercial wireless services is pressuring frequency bands traditionally reserved for radar, motivating the need for radar systems that can operate with non-contiguous, spectrally sparse transmissions while maintaining acceptable estimation accuracy.

Method: (1) MFI criterion to select spectral blocks that contribute most to estimation accuracy. (2) Iterative Reduced-Rank MMSE estimator that starts with single-target hypothesis and grows active target subspace one bin at a time, updating the target covariance using the largest estimated reflection coefficient and its posterior error variance. (3) Bayesian CRLB derivation for non-contiguous spectrum model.

Result: The algorithm recovers sparse range profiles with MSE close to the fully filled baseline when significant scatterers ≤ rank of sparse sensing matrix, demonstrated with 50% and 75% spectrally occupied MFI-designed spectra.

Conclusion: The proposed MFI-based spectral design and RRMMSE estimator effectively enable radar operation in spectrally contested environments, balancing spectrum sharing with estimation accuracy.

Abstract: Ongoing demand for radio spectrum by commercial wireless services has steadily increased pressure on the frequency bands traditionally reserved for radar. This paper addresses the joint problem of designing non-contiguous radar transmission spectra and estimating the range profile from the resulting reduced measurement set. Transmission spectra are constructed using a Marginal Fisher Information (MFI) criterion that removes blocks of frequencies contributing least to estimation accuracy. To process the underdetermined signals acquired from the resulting sparse measurement vector, an iterative Reduced-Rank Minimum Mean-Square Error (RRMMSE) estimator is proposed. The estimator starts with a single-target hypothesis and grows the active target subspace one range bin at a time, updating the a~priori target covariance matrix in each iteration using both the largest estimated reflection coefficient and its posterior error variance. This avoids inversion of the full $M{\times}M$ covariance matrix that would be required by a one-step MMSE and concentrates the rank of the estimator on the support of significant scatterers. The Bayesian Cramér--Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) on the per-bin reflection coefficient is derived for the non-contiguous spectrum measurement model, and the computational complexity of the proposed estimator is shown to scale as $\Order(G^2 M K^2)$, where $G$ is the number of detectable scatterers, $M$ is the number of range bins, and $K$ is the number of preserved spectral samples. Simulations using $50\%$ and $75\%$ spectrally occupied MFI-designed spectra confirm that the algorithm recovers sparse range profiles with Mean-Square Error (MSE) close to the fully filled baseline when the number of significant scatterers is not larger than the rank of the sparse sensing matrix.

</details>


### [511] [Column-Wise Analog Processing for Hybrid Precoding in Millimeter Wave Downlink Multi-User Massive MIMO Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29449)
*Yu Zhang,Ziyang Meng,Fangfang Yin*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a column-wise analog precoding (CWAP) algorithm for mmWave massive MIMO systems that reduces complexity by computing the analog precoder in closed-form (one step) instead of iterative optimization, achieving comparable or better spectral efficiency than existing AltMin algorithms with nearly an order of magnitude complexity reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AltMin hybrid precoding algorithms achieve near-optimal spectral efficiency but require hundreds of iterations to optimize the analog precoder, leading to high computational complexity that is impractical for real-time mmWave massive MIMO systems.

Method: The paper simplifies the achievable SE expression under the assumption of perfect digital precoding, decomposes the SE optimization into sub-problems per column of the analog precoder, and derives closed-form solutions for each column, eliminating the need for iterative optimization.

Result: (a) When RF chains = data streams, CWAP achieves approximately the same sum-rate as AltMin; (b) When RF chains > data streams, CWAP achieves higher sum-rate than AltMin; (c) CWAP has nearly an order of magnitude lower complexity than AltMin algorithms.

Conclusion: The proposed CWAP algorithm effectively reduces hybrid precoding complexity while maintaining or improving spectral efficiency, offering a more practical solution for mmWave massive MIMO systems.

Abstract: In millimeter wave (mmWave) massive MIMO systems, existing alternating minimization (AltMin) based hybrid digital-analog precoding algorithms can achieve near-optimal spectral efficiency (SE) of the fully-digital precoding. However, this kind of AltMin algorithms require several hundreds of iterations to optimize the analog precoder, thus increasing the complexity. This paper focuses on reducing complexity of the analog precoding and proposes a column-wise analog precoding (CWAP) algorithm. The main idea is to seek closed-form solution of the analog precoder, through which the analog precoder can be easily computed in one step instead of iterations, thus reducing the complexity. Specifically, by assuming that perfect digital precoder is deployed at the base station (BS) to eliminate interferences among users, we simplify the expression of achievable SE for each user. Subsequently, the simplified SE is further converted to the sum of a series of sub-rates, each of which is related to the corresponding column of the analog precoder. The optimization problem of maximizing the SE is then transformed into a series of sub-problems of maximizing each sub-rate. Upon solving each sub-problem, closed-form solution of each column of the analog precoder can be directly obtained without iterations, resulting in reduced complexity. Simulation results demonstrate that (a) when the number of RF chains equals the number of data streams, the proposed scheme can achieve approximately same sum-rate as the AltMin algorithms; (b) when the number of RF chains is larger than the number of data streams, the proposed scheme can achieve higher sum-rate than the AltMin algorithms; (c) the proposed scheme has lower complexity than the AltMin algorithms (almost one order of magnitude reduction in some cases).

</details>


### [512] [Hybrid Digital and Analog Airy Beamforming for Near-Field Multi-User Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29481)
*Shupei Zhang,Boya Di,Lingyang Song*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes an Airy beamforming scheme for near-field multi-user communications that uses bending wavefronts to bypass obstacles, achieving better spectral efficiency than conventional focused beamforming without requiring full channel state information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-frequency focused beams in near-field 6G communications are highly susceptible to blockages from obstacles, which degrades spectral efficiency in multi-user scenarios with hybrid precoding.

Method: (1) Develops near-field Airy wavefront with bending trajectory for obstacle bypassing and energy concentration; (2) Designs hierarchical Airy beam training to select beams that circumvent obstacles and align with users; (3) Configures analog beamformer using selected Airy beams without full CSI; (4) Applies digital beamformer to mitigate inter-user interference.

Result: Beam patterns show successful obstacle circumvention and multi-user alignment. The proposed scheme outperforms conventional focused beamforming in spectral efficiency across mmWave to THz bands.

Conclusion: Airy beamforming is an effective solution for obstacle-avoiding multi-user near-field communications, offering significant spectral efficiency gains over traditional focused beamforming.

Abstract: The demands for high data rates in 6G networks have driven the transition toward higher frequencies and larger antenna apertures, giving rise to the near-field communications. In the near-field region, spherical waves enable beam focusing to enhance the received power. However, high-frequency focused beams are highly susceptible to ubiquitous obstacles due to rectilinear trajectories. Particularly in multi-user communications with hybrid precoding, focused beamforming suffers from impaired spectral efficiency under potential multi-user link blockages. In this paper, we propose an Airy beamforming enabled multi-user transmission scheme. The near-field Airy wavefront with a bending trajectory is first developed to cope with the obstructed channels, possessing the dual capability of bypassing obstacles and concentrating energy. Moreover, a low-complexity Airy beamforming enabled multi-user communication scheme is designed. Specifically, Airy beams capable of circumventing obstacles and aligning with users are first obtained through hierarchical Airy beam training. Then, the selected Airy beams are leveraged to configure the analog beamformer to achieve multi-user obstacle-avoiding access without full channel state information acquisition. Finally, the digital beamformer is utilized to further mitigate inter-user interference. In simulations, the beam patterns demonstrate that the proposed Airy beamforming successfully circumvents blockages and aligns with multiple users. Across typical mmWave to THz bands, the proposed scheme outperforms conventional focused beamforming in terms of spectral efficiency.

</details>


### [513] [Multi-Snapshot Deep Denoising for Channel Estimation in OTFS Modulated Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29777)
*Surbhi Gehlot,Siddhi Shinde,Suraj Srivastava,Sandeep Kumar Yadav*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes a deep denoising framework for OTFS channel estimation by treating CSI recovery as an image restoration problem, leveraging structural invariance in the delay-Doppler domain over geometric coherence time, and using a lightweight NAFNet denoiser for improved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve reliable channel state information (CSI) recovery in OTFS modulated systems with low computational complexity, especially under low pilot SNR and fractional delay/Doppler effects.

Method: Formulates CSI recovery as an image restoration problem; exploits structural invariance of the DD domain channel over geometric coherence time to obtain multiple noisy snapshots; uses a lightweight NAFNet (nonlinear activation free network) as the denoiser.

Result: Significant performance gains over existing methods, low computational complexity, reliable operation even at low pilot SNR, and accommodation of fractional delay and fractional Doppler effects.

Conclusion: The proposed deep denoising framework effectively and efficiently recovers OTFS channel state information by leveraging temporal structural invariance and a lightweight denoiser, outperforming existing approaches.

Abstract: A deep denoising based channel estimation framework is proposed for orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) modulated systems, wherein channel state information (CSI) recovery is formulated as an image restoration problem. A salient attribute of the approach is the exploitation of structural invariance in the delay Doppler (DD) domain channel over a geometric coherence time, allowing multiple OTFS frames captured during this period to serve as noisy snapshots of the approximately identical channel. These snapshots jointly enhance the effectiveness of the proposed lightweight denoiser based on nonlinear activation free network (NAFNet). The method exhibits low computational complexity, operates reliably even at low pilot signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), and can accommodate both fractional delay and fractional Doppler effects. Simulation results demonstrate significant performance gains over the existing methods.

</details>
